0: Date of final oral examination: July 20, 2021 The dissertation is approved by the following members of the Final Oral Committee:

Victor M. Zavala, Associate Professor, Chemical and Biological Engineering Brian F. Pfleger, Professor, Chemical and Biological Engineering Ross E. Swaney, Associate Professor, Chemical and Biological Engineering Rebecca A. Larson, Associate Professor, Biological Systems Engineering
1: I still remember the first day I was in Madison (08/15/2016), feeling curious and unsettled at the same time when facing my empty apartment and the unfamiliar yet beautiful town. And now, I'm just thinking that the past five years in UW-Madison have been an unforgettable journey in my life thanks to all the wonderful people I met.

First of all, I want to express my sincere gratitude to my advisor, Prof. Victor Zavala for mentoring me through my study in graduate school. On the first day we met in 2016, Victor told me "you'll get there" when he noticed that I was struggling to understand some math in one of his papers; and now, I am finishing my Ph.D. dissertation. His constant encouragement and support have always been the energy that motivates me to pursue and enjoy my research. I want to also express my respect and admiration to him for his solid expertise in the field of mathematical modeling, optimization, and data science. With his wide erudition, he guided me to explore different areas, including optimization, game theory, statistics, etc. His great qualities of being a critical thinker, extraordinary leader, and excellent communicator have also influenced me profoundly in value and skill development.

I would like to thank Prof. Brian Pfleger, Prof. Ross Swaney, and Prof. Rebecca Larson for serving on my defense committee, giving me the opportunity to present my work, and sharing valuable feedback. Special thanks to Prof. Brian Pfleger and Prof. Ross Swaney for being committee members in my preliminary exam and fourth-year progress meeting. And I am very grateful to Prof. Rebecca Larson for her guidance and feedback to my publications. She is one of the most fantastic collaborators I have ever had.

I would also like to thank all members in the scalable systems laboratory, or zavalab. I had great memories of getting group lunch on Fridays, chatting in the office, and attend- ing conferences with you all. I want to thank Apoorva Sampat, Ranjeet Kumar, Yankai Cao, Jordan Jalving, and Alex Dowling for all your help when I first joined the group. I also want to thank Weiqi Zhang, Amy Qin, and all other members for being amazing lab-mates and friends, and creating an easy and fun group environment.

I also feel fortunate to have the opportunity to learn from my amazing collaborators in the last five years. I would like to thank Prof. Noguera and Dr. Scarborough for intro- ducing me to the area of fatty acid recovery and finishing my first publication together. I would like to thank Prof. Larson, Dr. Sharara, and Dr. Aguirre-Villegas for provid- ing valuable insights in the area of livestock manure management. I want to thank Prof. Karthikeyan and Dr. Kim for collaborating in the techno-economic analysis project and

providing valuable first-hand experimental data. And I want to thank Prof. Martín and Edgar Martín for providing experience and feedback on web-based tool development. And I want to thank the INFEWS team; I enjoyed the presentations and discussions we had during the project meetings, and they are crucial to this work. In the year 2018, I had a summer internship at U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in Cincinnati. I want to express my gratitude to Dr. Gerardo Ruiz-Mercado for being my advisor during that time and providing guidance in the area of harmful algal bloom reduction. In the year 2020, I had a summer internship in Isuzu Technical Center, America. I want to thank Yong Sun and Yifan Wei for being my mentors and introducing me to the area of vehicle engineering.

I also want to mention my incredible friends in Madison who made the journey more enjoyable. I would like to thank Zening Duan, Yaqing Wu, Yijiang Yu, Sijie Chen, and Huicheng Shi for providing constant support (special thanks to Yijiang as a Pokemon Go buddy and Sijie as a swimming buddy). I would like to thank Mingyuan Tian and Wenzhao Wu for providing professional advice. And I want to thank Fan Chen, Jun Li, Zhuohan Li, Songying Li, Yaxuan Wang, Sicong Zhang, and all other friends for your accompany in Madison.

I would have never had the opportunity to purse my graduate studies in Madison without the excellent education I received during my undergraduate study in Zhejiang University. I was exposed to fours years of well-rounded chemical engineering curricu- lum. Prof. Xiaoping Hu, Prof. Li Wang, and Prof. Bogeng Li inspired me to pursue my graduate studies in chemical engineering. I also made life-long friends at ZJU who continue to support me even after almost a decade.

It would have been impossible to achieve any of this without my family. As the only child in my family, I want to express my deepest gratitude and love to my Mom, Jianying Chu, and my Dad, Jianguo Hu, for giving all of their unconditional love and support to me.

1: 2 RECOVERY OF FATTY ACID AND BIOGAS
2.3 Case Studies for the State of Wisconsin
3 LOGISTICS NETWORK AND HARMFUL ALGAL BLOOM CONTROL
3.4.1 Logistics Network Design and Nutrient Pollution

With the rapid growth of the human population, it is estimated that global energy de- mand is expected to double by 2050, with the demand for food and water increasing by 60% in tandem. In addition, organic waste production is expected to increase with intensified agricultural and urban activities. Organic waste streams can pose severe en- vironmental threats, including carbon emissions and nutrient release, which can further contribute to climate change and water quality degradation. However, there are economic barriers to deploy waste processing technologies (due to uncertainty in waste value esti- mation, its sparse distribution, and an immature market).

Multi-product supply chain modeling and optimization is an efficient method for understanding organic waste management problems, and it enables systematic evalu- ations from economic and environmental perspectives. The research accomplished in this work includes the development of computational frameworks that combine multi- product supply chain models with a variety of computational and analytical tools to conduct economic and environmental analyses, to resolve conflicts in multiple economic and environmental metrics, and to provide decision-making insights for stakeholders.

We apply our computational frameworks to case studies in Wisconsin at both local and state scales to analyze the performance of novel technologies, the impact of nutrient management strategies on harmful algal blooms, and to provide compromise solutions to optimally balance multiple objectives. A web-based software platform, ADAM, is also developed to automate supply chain modeling, solution, and visualization using an interactive graphical interface. The capabilities of ADAM are demonstrated through a comprehensive case study that considers pretreatment-enhanced anaerobic digestion.

With the rapid growth of the human population, it is estimated that global energy de- mand is expected to double by 2050, with the demand for food and water increasing by 60% in tandem. In addition, organic waste production is expected to increase with intensified agricultural and urban activities. Organic waste streams can pose severe en- vironmental threats, including carbon emissions and nutrient release, which can further contribute to climate change and water quality degradation. However, there are economic barriers to deploy waste processing technologies (due to uncertainty in waste value esti- mation, its sparse distribution, and an immature market).

Multi-product supply chain modeling and optimization is an efficient method for understanding organic waste management problems, and it enables systematic evalu- ations from economic and environmental perspectives. The research accomplished in this work includes the development of computational frameworks that combine multi- product supply chain models with a variety of computational and analytical tools to conduct economic and environmental analyses, to resolve conflicts in multiple economic and environmental metrics, and to provide decision-making insights for stakeholders.

We apply our computational frameworks to case studies in Wisconsin at both local and state scales to analyze the performance of novel technologies, the impact of nutrient management strategies on harmful algal blooms, and to provide compromise solutions to optimally balance multiple objectives. A web-based software platform, ADAM, is also developed to automate supply chain modeling, solution, and visualization using an interactive graphical interface. The capabilities of ADAM are demonstrated through a comprehensive case study that considers pretreatment-enhanced anaerobic digestion.

With the rapid growth of the human population, it is estimated that global energy de- mand is expected to double by 2050, with the demand for food and water increasing by 60% in tandem. In addition, organic waste production is expected to increase with intensified agricultural and urban activities. Organic waste streams can pose severe en- vironmental threats, including carbon emissions and nutrient release, which can further contribute to climate change and water quality degradation. However, there are economic barriers to deploy waste processing technologies (due to uncertainty in waste value esti- mation, its sparse distribution, and an immature market).

Multi-product supply chain modeling and optimization is an efficient method for understanding organic waste management problems, and it enables systematic evalu- ations from economic and environmental perspectives. The research accomplished in this work includes the development of computational frameworks that combine multi- product supply chain models with a variety of computational and analytical tools to conduct economic and environmental analyses, to resolve conflicts in multiple economic and environmental metrics, and to provide decision-making insights for stakeholders.

We apply our computational frameworks to case studies in Wisconsin at both local and state scales to analyze the performance of novel technologies, the impact of nutrient management strategies on harmful algal blooms, and to provide compromise solutions to optimally balance multiple objectives. A web-based software platform, ADAM, is also developed to automate supply chain modeling, solution, and visualization using an interactive graphical interface. The capabilities of ADAM are demonstrated through a comprehensive case study that considers pretreatment-enhanced anaerobic digestion.

With the rapid growth of the human population, it is estimated that global energy de- mand is expected to double by 2050, with the demand for food and water increasing by 60% in tandem. In addition, organic waste production is expected to increase with intensified agricultural and urban activities. Organic waste streams can pose severe en- vironmental threats, including carbon emissions and nutrient release, which can further contribute to climate change and water quality degradation. However, there are economic barriers to deploy waste processing technologies (due to uncertainty in waste value esti- mation, its sparse distribution, and an immature market).

Multi-product supply chain modeling and optimization is an efficient method for understanding organic waste management problems, and it enables systematic evalu- ations from economic and environmental perspectives. The research accomplished in this work includes the development of computational frameworks that combine multi- product supply chain models with a variety of computational and analytical tools to conduct economic and environmental analyses, to resolve conflicts in multiple economic and environmental metrics, and to provide decision-making insights for stakeholders.

We apply our computational frameworks to case studies in Wisconsin at both local and state scales to analyze the performance of novel technologies, the impact of nutrient management strategies on harmful algal blooms, and to provide compromise solutions to optimally balance multiple objectives. A web-based software platform, ADAM, is also developed to automate supply chain modeling, solution, and visualization using an interactive graphical interface. The capabilities of ADAM are demonstrated through a comprehensive case study that considers pretreatment-enhanced anaerobic digestion.

With the rapid growth of the human population, it is estimated that global energy de- mand is expected to double by 2050, with the demand for food and water increasing by 60% in tandem. In addition, organic waste production is expected to increase with intensified agricultural and urban activities. Organic waste streams can pose severe en- vironmental threats, including carbon emissions and nutrient release, which can further contribute to climate change and water quality degradation. However, there are economic barriers to deploy waste processing technologies (due to uncertainty in waste value esti- mation, its sparse distribution, and an immature market).

Multi-product supply chain modeling and optimization is an efficient method for understanding organic waste management problems, and it enables systematic evalu- ations from economic and environmental perspectives. The research accomplished in this work includes the development of computational frameworks that combine multi- product supply chain models with a variety of computational and analytical tools to conduct economic and environmental analyses, to resolve conflicts in multiple economic and environmental metrics, and to provide decision-making insights for stakeholders.

We apply our computational frameworks to case studies in Wisconsin at both local and state scales to analyze the performance of novel technologies, the impact of nutrient management strategies on harmful algal blooms, and to provide compromise solutions to optimally balance multiple objectives. A web-based software platform, ADAM, is also developed to automate supply chain modeling, solution, and visualization using an interactive graphical interface. The capabilities of ADAM are demonstrated through a comprehensive case study that considers pretreatment-enhanced anaerobic digestion.
13: With the rapid growth of the human population, it is estimated that global energy de- mand is expected to double by 2050, with the demand for food and water increasing by 60% in tandem (Lin et al., 2018; Wolfe et al., 2016). Meeting these increasing demands requires the development of intensified food, energy, and water production infrastruc- ture. As a significant component in the food-energy-water (FEW) nexus, the production of organic waste is also expected to increase (Polprasert, 2007; Lin et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020) along with the intensification of agricultural and urban activities. For example, the United States dairy industry is a multi-billion dollar enterprise comprising millions of dairy cows. The State of Wisconsin alone produces 29 billion pounds of milk and 2.8 billion pounds of cheese annually (State of Wisconsin Department of Agriulture and Pro- tection, 2018). The dairy sector is a major social and cultural stake in regions worldwide. However, the dairy industry consumes large amounts of water, energy, and nutrients and creates significant environmental impacts. Such resource intensity is driven by increasing dairy product demands from the human population. When we consider the fact that a single dairy cow produces approximately 9,500 liters of milk and 19,000 liters of manure

a year (approximately two liters of manure are generated per liter of milk produced) a proper dairy manure management system is of critical importance (López-Díaz et al., 2019; Sampat et al., 2018b). Other organic waste streams include food waste, municipal solid waste, industrial waste, crop residues, etc., and there have been extensive studies demonstrating the potential of recovering value-added products from these organic waste streams (Li et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2018; Hansen et al., 2007; Munir et al., 2018).

A common feature existing in those organic waste streams is that they can pose severe environmental impacts without proper management. For example, livestock manure is one of the largest sources of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Eisler et al., 2014) since it releases carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), and nitrous oxide (N2O) into the atmo- sphere. Specifically, the global warming potential of CH4 and N2O are around 28 and 265 times larger than that of CO2 (Boucher et al., 2009; Mosier et al., 2005). Untreated organic waste also contributes to nutrient pollution, which is another area of concern since it can trigger harmful algal blooms (HABs) that release toxins and degrade water quality (Smith et al., 2015). Decreased water quality can directly threaten human health and impact economies in recreational (e.g., tourism, swimming) and industrial (e.g., fish- ing) sectors. It is estimated that in U.S., the tourism and fishing losses due to HABs are in the order of 1 billion and tens of millions of dollars annually, respectively (U.S. En- vironmental Protection Agency, 2021b; Piehler, 2008). Organic waste can also contribute to the spreading of pathogens, heavy metal pollution, odor issues, and ammonia emis- sions (Burtscher et al., 1998; Sahlström, 2003; Gutiérrez et al., 2017; Lopes et al., 2011; Nakhshiniev et al., 2014).

While reducing environmental impacts is one of the main driving forces in organic waste management, there are economic barriers in the deployment of processing tech- nologies (Case et al., 2017; Gloy and Dressler, 2010). Organic waste streams usually con- tain complex chemical components which vary seasonally and geographically, and this nature brings uncertainty in estimating the value of those streams (Alibardi and Cossu, 2015; Barret et al., 2013). Geographical sparsity results in large collection and transporta-

tion costs, and most types of waste lack formal collection infrastructure (e.g., comparable to the infrastructure for human wastewater treatment in urban areas) (Ghafoori et al., 2007b). With both high technology costs and low market values for recovered products (such as biogas and fertilizers) organic waste management technologies are not common- place in the U.S. To give some perspective on this issue, only 16% of concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs) in the State of Wisconsin are equipped with anaerobic di- gestion systems (U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, 2021a), even when their benefits in reducing carbon emissions, improving nutrient availability for crops, and reducing pathogens are well-understood (Wilkie, 2005; SHIH, 1987).

A key research question in organic waste management is how to overcome the eco- nomic barrier, reconcile its conflict with the environmental impacts, and understand the intrinsic trade-offs. Researchers and policy-makers have proposed various methods, in- cluding increasing product yields (Battimelli et al., 2010; Lindmark et al., 2012; Shehu et al., 2012), providing policy incentives (Capodaglio et al., 2016; Benato and Macor, 2019), producing alternative products with high market values (Hu et al., 2018; Claassen et al., 2000), reducing costs by process intensification (McLeod et al., 2019; Davey et al., 2020), and imposing waste and carbon taxes (Björklund and Finnveden, 2007; Martin and Scott, 2003; Metcalf and Weisbach, 2009). However, it is not easy to conclude if those approaches become effective without comprehensive studies at a systems-level. This work targets to provide computational platforms and a series of application case studies to demonstrate the decision-making procedure in organic waste management.

a year (approximately two liters of manure are generated per liter of milk produced) a proper dairy manure management system is of critical importance (López-Díaz et al., 2019; Sampat et al., 2018b). Other organic waste streams include food waste, municipal solid waste, industrial waste, crop residues, etc., and there have been extensive studies demonstrating the potential of recovering value-added products from these organic waste streams (Li et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2018; Hansen et al., 2007; Munir et al., 2018).

A common feature existing in those organic waste streams is that they can pose severe environmental impacts without proper management. For example, livestock manure is one of the largest sources of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Eisler et al., 2014) since it releases carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), and nitrous oxide (N2O) into the atmo- sphere. Specifically, the global warming potential of CH4 and N2O are around 28 and 265 times larger than that of CO2 (Boucher et al., 2009; Mosier et al., 2005). Untreated organic waste also contributes to nutrient pollution, which is another area of concern since it can trigger harmful algal blooms (HABs) that release toxins and degrade water quality (Smith et al., 2015). Decreased water quality can directly threaten human health and impact economies in recreational (e.g., tourism, swimming) and industrial (e.g., fish- ing) sectors. It is estimated that in U.S., the tourism and fishing losses due to HABs are in the order of 1 billion and tens of millions of dollars annually, respectively (U.S. En- vironmental Protection Agency, 2021b; Piehler, 2008). Organic waste can also contribute to the spreading of pathogens, heavy metal pollution, odor issues, and ammonia emis- sions (Burtscher et al., 1998; Sahlström, 2003; Gutiérrez et al., 2017; Lopes et al., 2011; Nakhshiniev et al., 2014).

While reducing environmental impacts is one of the main driving forces in organic waste management, there are economic barriers in the deployment of processing tech- nologies (Case et al., 2017; Gloy and Dressler, 2010). Organic waste streams usually con- tain complex chemical components which vary seasonally and geographically, and this nature brings uncertainty in estimating the value of those streams (Alibardi and Cossu, 2015; Barret et al., 2013). Geographical sparsity results in large collection and transporta-

tion costs, and most types of waste lack formal collection infrastructure (e.g., comparable to the infrastructure for human wastewater treatment in urban areas) (Ghafoori et al., 2007b). With both high technology costs and low market values for recovered products (such as biogas and fertilizers) organic waste management technologies are not common- place in the U.S. To give some perspective on this issue, only 16% of concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs) in the State of Wisconsin are equipped with anaerobic di- gestion systems (U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, 2021a), even when their benefits in reducing carbon emissions, improving nutrient availability for crops, and reducing pathogens are well-understood (Wilkie, 2005; SHIH, 1987).

A key research question in organic waste management is how to overcome the eco- nomic barrier, reconcile its conflict with the environmental impacts, and understand the intrinsic trade-offs. Researchers and policy-makers have proposed various methods, in- cluding increasing product yields (Battimelli et al., 2010; Lindmark et al., 2012; Shehu et al., 2012), providing policy incentives (Capodaglio et al., 2016; Benato and Macor, 2019), producing alternative products with high market values (Hu et al., 2018; Claassen et al., 2000), reducing costs by process intensification (McLeod et al., 2019; Davey et al., 2020), and imposing waste and carbon taxes (Björklund and Finnveden, 2007; Martin and Scott, 2003; Metcalf and Weisbach, 2009). However, it is not easy to conclude if those approaches become effective without comprehensive studies at a systems-level. This work targets to provide computational platforms and a series of application case studies to demonstrate the decision-making procedure in organic waste management.
18: The nature of interactions and interdependencies makes the FEW sector (including or- ganic waste management) a complex system (D'Odorico et al., 2018). Supply chain mod- eling and optimization has been identified as an efficient method in analyzing this holis- tic, integrated, and multi-media problem (Bergendahl et al., 2018; Sharma et al., 2013).

Multi-product supply chain models are capable of capturing material transformations, transportation (logistical issues), and related economic and environmental aspects. Mod- els and applications have been developed in the fields of biomass-to-fuel technologies (Zhang and Hu, 2013; Kim et al., 2011; Iakovou et al., 2010; You and Grossmann, 2008; You et al., 2012; Chen and Fan, 2012; Chen and Onal, 2014; Ghosh and Bakshi, 2019), algae-to-biofuel conversions (Nodooshan et al., 2018; Arabi et al., 2019; Avami, 2012), composting urban waste (Tominac et al., 2020), producing energy from livestock manure (Egieya et al., 2019; Svanberg et al., 2018), and food waste collection and conversions (Aschemann-Witzel et al., 2017; Papargyropoulou et al., 2014; Ramaswami et al., 2017), etc. A modeling abstraction has been established for multi-product supply chain net- works by Sampat et al. (2017) to capture fundamental elements, including geographical nodes, suppliers, consumers, technology providers, and transportation providers. This modeling abstraction has been used in incentive analysis (Sampat et al., 2018b), phospho- rus recovery technology recommendations (Sampat et al., 2018a, 2021), and coordinated management of livestock waste (Sampat et al., 2019).

While classic supply chain models focus on economic issues only, environmental im- pacts need to be considered in organic waste management, which makes the supply chain a multi-objective problem. Trade-off information can be provided by well-developed methods such as weighted-sum or e-constraint method (Zitzler, 1999; Laumanns et al., 2006), but the final decision to be made still requires additional expert knowledge from a decision-maker on relative importance of each metric or some presumed utility functions (Branke et al., 2008). Moreover, for some environmental metrics, an overall summary provided by the supply chain model is not enough to reveal its endpoint and long-term impact, and this can create challenges for policy-makers and other stakeholders who may underestimate the environmental consequences (Pagell and Shevchenko, 2014). There- fore, additional steps, such as simulations and sensitivity analysis, as well as lifecycle assessment should be considered for impact interpretation, uncertainty analysis, and de- cision recommendations

Another key issue in multi-product supply chains is their reliance on experimental and analytical data, such as material transformation factors and related technology costs. These datasets usually come from researchers outside of the process systems engineering community and thus require extensive collaboration to develop and curate. We realize that there is a gap in integrating computational and experimental work in the field of supply chain modeling and organic waste management, engaging researchers with stake- holders and decision-makers, and bridging cutting-edge research and education.
By identifying these issues, this work tries to provide insights to the following research questions about decision-making in organic waste management:

<list>1. How do we apply supply chain optimization tools to recommend rational decisions? 
2. How do we interpret the impact of these decisions? 
3. Can we provide a computational platform to integrate experimental and computa- tional studies?</list>

22: This dissertation is structured as follows:

Chapter 2 - Recovery of Fatty Acid and Biogas. This chapter introduces an application of multi-product supply chain models for analyzing the recovery of fatty acid and biogas considering both profitability and carbon emissions.

Chapter 3 - Logistics Network and Harmful Algal Bloom Control. This chapter provides a computational framework combining dynamic supply chain models, nutrient transport models, and algae growth simulation models to provide insights in controlling harmful algal blooms.

Chapter 4 - Conflict Resolution in Livestock Waste Management. This chapter intro- duces a decision-making procedure to resolve conflicts appearing in livestock waste man- agement. The framework includes life cycle analysis, supply chain modeling and opti- mization, and a utopia-tracking method.

Chapter 5 - Graph-based Supply Chain Modeling Using Software ADAM. This chapter reviews mathematical formulations for supply chain models and proposes a graph-based modeling method. A web-based multi-product supply chain modeling and optimization software, ADAM, is introduced along with its design principles, key features, workflow, and application examples.

Chapter 6 - Case Study in ADAM: Pretreatments of Anaerobic Digestion. This chapter provides an application that integrates experimental outcomes of increasing biogas yield by pretreatments in anaerobic digestion, conceptual system design with techno-economic analysis, and supply chain modeling and optimization in ADAM.
Chapter 7 - Conclusions and Future Directions. This chapter provides a summary of the major contributions of this dissertation. Specific areas for improvement for future research are also identified.

0: This chapter is published in Hu et al. (2018).
1: Identifying cost-effective technologies and transportation logistics to process urban and rural organic waste is a challenging and important social problem. Studies and indus- trial deployments currently available have focused on processing waste to recover biogas from livestock, sewage (wastewater) biosolids, and food waste (Row and Neabel, 2005; Takata et al., 2012; Yoshida et al., 2013). The majority of studies focus on biogas recov- ery reported show significant environmental benefits (e.g. reduction of carbon emissions and reduction of odor and pathogen) but also show important discrepancies on economic viability (i.e., many of the existing biogas facilities rely on either gate fees or govern- ment subsidies for carbon credits). As a result, significant investment risk is currently perceived, hindering the widespread deployment of these technologies. Gebrezgabher et al. (2010) and Budzianowski and Budzianowska (2015) conducted economic analyses of biogas recovery based on anaerobic digestion (AD) in the Netherlands and Poland. Thesee studies found that, in the absence of government subsidies, AD systems are not

economically viable. Ghafoori et al. (2007a) estimated that $125 per tonne of CO2 in car- bon credits are needed to make biogas recovery from AD facilities economically viable in Canada. Akbulut (2012) conducted a techno-economic analysis for a milk production facility equipped with a biogas-fueled electricity generation system in Turkey and found that the revenue collected from electricity generation corresponds to only 5% of the total revenue. Some studies have also assessed the impact of mixing different types of waste for biogas production in order to enhance performance (Zhang et al., 2013a; Dai et al., 2013). In the overview conducted by Shen et al. (2015), it is reported that less than 10% of the wastewater treatment plants (WWTP) in the U.S. use biogas production systems. This is due to several socio-economic, technical, and regulatory barriers. This work also reports that co-digestion of multiple waste streams can help overcome economic obstacles.

Recently, some studies have focused on the recovery of higher-value chemicals such as the medium-chain fatty acids (MCFA) such as caproic acid (C6) and caprylic acid (C8) from diverse organic waste substrates. Grootscholten et al. (2013) investigated the production of MCFA from ethanol in an upflow anaerobic filter and achieved a MCFA production rate of 16.6 g L-1. d-¹. Grootscholten et al. (2014) later demonstrated that MCFA can be produced from real organic waste, municipal solid waste, and diluted ethanol. This system achieved high MCFA production rates and high C6 titers. Kucek et al. (2016) used wine lees (another organic waste containing ethanol) as a substrate and also achieved MCFA production. Compared to biogas, C6 and C8 have a much higher market value, higher energy density, and can be transported in liquid form. However, the yield factors for these chemicals are low and sophisticated separation/purification systems are necessary. To the best of our knowledge, no techno-economic analysis has been reported in the literature to evaluate the potential of recovering C6 and C8 from cow manure, wastewater sludge, and food waste.

Multi-product supply chain modeling provides a powerful framework for analyzing organic waste management in a systematic manner. Multiple studies have proposed to use optimization models to design biomass supply chains strategically. Applications

reported include supply chains for conversions of food crops, cellulosic mass, waste biomass to biodiesel or biofuel (energy), ethanol, and sugar (Chen and Önal, 2014; You and Wang, 2011; Zandi Atashbar et al., 2018). Some of these studies seek to capture trade- offs between economic metrics (e.g. total cost, profit or net present value) and environ- mental metrics (e.g. greenhouse gas emissions, carbon footprint or energy consumption). Optimization studies dealing with different aspects in organic waste management and with the so-called water-energy-food nexus have also been recently reported (Garcia and You, 2016). For example, Niziolek et al. (2015) used optimization strategies to design a municipal waste solid-to-liquid fuel process. Comelli et al. (2015) studied a process for COD (chemical oxygen demand) removal from high-strength wastewater of the nonalco- holic sugar-sweetened beverage industry. Inspired by these works, we propose a com- prehensive supply chain optimization framework for analyzing the waste management problem to simultaneously identify technology types (along with sizes and placement) as well as waste and product transportation strategies (Sampat et al., 2017). We incorporate the traditional biogas recovery technologies, potential C6/C8 recovery technologies, and hybrid systems in our analysis. We also evaluate how external factors and mixing of different organic waste streams from urban and rural areas impact economic and envi- ronmental performance. A series of case studies for the State of Wisconsin is conducted. Sensitivity analysis is used to evaluate the impact of diverse uncertain factors such as technology performance and policy incentives.

For example, Niziolek et al. (2015) used optimization strategies to design a municipal waste solid-to-liquid fuel process. Comelli et al. (2015) studied a process for COD (chemical oxygen demand) removal from high-strength wastewater of the nonalco- holic sugar-sweetened beverage industry. Inspired by these works, we propose a com- prehensive supply chain optimization framework for analyzing the waste management problem to simultaneously identify technology types (along with sizes and placement) as well as waste and product transportation strategies (Sampat et al., 2017). We incorporate the traditional biogas recovery technologies, potential C6/C8 recovery technologies, and hybrid systems in our analysis. We also evaluate how external factors and mixing of different organic waste streams from urban and rural areas impact economic and envi- ronmental performance. A series of case studies for the State of Wisconsin is conducted. Sensitivity analysis is used to evaluate the impact of diverse uncertain factors such as technology performance and policy incentives.
4: Identifying cost-effective technologies and transportation logistics to process urban and rural organic waste is a challenging and important social problem. Studies and indus- trial deployments currently available have focused on processing waste to recover biogas from livestock, sewage (wastewater) biosolids, and food waste (Row and Neabel, 2005; Takata et al., 2012; Yoshida et al., 2013). The majority of studies focus on biogas recov- ery reported show significant environmental benefits (e.g. reduction of carbon emissions and reduction of odor and pathogen) but also show important discrepancies on economic viability (i.e., many of the existing biogas facilities rely on either gate fees or govern- ment subsidies for carbon credits). As a result, significant investment risk is currently perceived, hindering the widespread deployment of these technologies. Gebrezgabher et al. (2010) and Budzianowski and Budzianowska (2015) conducted economic analyses of biogas recovery based on anaerobic digestion (AD) in the Netherlands and Poland. Thesee studies found that, in the absence of government subsidies, AD systems are not

economically viable. Ghafoori et al. (2007a) estimated that $125 per tonne of CO2 in car- bon credits are needed to make biogas recovery from AD facilities economically viable in Canada. Akbulut (2012) conducted a techno-economic analysis for a milk production facility equipped with a biogas-fueled electricity generation system in Turkey and found that the revenue collected from electricity generation corresponds to only 5% of the total revenue. Some studies have also assessed the impact of mixing different types of waste for biogas production in order to enhance performance (Zhang et al., 2013a; Dai et al., 2013). In the overview conducted by Shen et al. (2015), it is reported that less than 10% of the wastewater treatment plants (WWTP) in the U.S. use biogas production systems. This is due to several socio-economic, technical, and regulatory barriers. This work also reports that co-digestion of multiple waste streams can help overcome economic obstacles.

Recently, some studies have focused on the recovery of higher-value chemicals such as the medium-chain fatty acids (MCFA) such as caproic acid (C6) and caprylic acid (C8) from diverse organic waste substrates. Grootscholten et al. (2013) investigated the production of MCFA from ethanol in an upflow anaerobic filter and achieved a MCFA production rate of 16.6 g L-1. d-¹. Grootscholten et al. (2014) later demonstrated that MCFA can be produced from real organic waste, municipal solid waste, and diluted ethanol. This system achieved high MCFA production rates and high C6 titers. Kucek et al. (2016) used wine lees (another organic waste containing ethanol) as a substrate and also achieved MCFA production. Compared to biogas, C6 and C8 have a much higher market value, higher energy density, and can be transported in liquid form. However, the yield factors for these chemicals are low and sophisticated separation/purification systems are necessary. To the best of our knowledge, no techno-economic analysis has been reported in the literature to evaluate the potential of recovering C6 and C8 from cow manure, wastewater sludge, and food waste.

Multi-product supply chain modeling provides a powerful framework for analyzing organic waste management in a systematic manner. Multiple studies have proposed to use optimization models to design biomass supply chains strategically. Applications

reported include supply chains for conversions of food crops, cellulosic mass, waste biomass to biodiesel or biofuel (energy), ethanol, and sugar (Chen and Önal, 2014; You and Wang, 2011; Zandi Atashbar et al., 2018). Some of these studies seek to capture trade- offs between economic metrics (e.g. total cost, profit or net present value) and environ- mental metrics (e.g. greenhouse gas emissions, carbon footprint or energy consumption). Optimization studies dealing with different aspects in organic waste management and with the so-called water-energy-food nexus have also been recently reported (Garcia and You, 2016). For example, Niziolek et al. (2015) used optimization strategies to design a municipal waste solid-to-liquid fuel process. Comelli et al. (2015) studied a process for COD (chemical oxygen demand) removal from high-strength wastewater of the nonalco- holic sugar-sweetened beverage industry. Inspired by these works, we propose a com- prehensive supply chain optimization framework for analyzing the waste management problem to simultaneously identify technology types (along with sizes and placement) as well as waste and product transportation strategies (Sampat et al., 2017). We incorporate the traditional biogas recovery technologies, potential C6/C8 recovery technologies, and hybrid systems in our analysis. We also evaluate how external factors and mixing of different organic waste streams from urban and rural areas impact economic and envi- ronmental performance. A series of case studies for the State of Wisconsin is conducted. Sensitivity analysis is used to evaluate the impact of diverse uncertain factors such as technology performance and policy incentives.

6: The proposed framework considers three sources of organic waste: cow manure (CM) generated at concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs), sludge (S) generated at WWTPs, and food waste (FW). These waste streams have different compositions, proper-

ties and total amounts. Independent processing of these streams is a common strategy, but co-processing can potentially provide additional economic and environmental bene- fits in terms of technology performance and supply chain flexibility. We consider different points of origin (source nodes): counties, CAFOs, WWTPs, and landfills (LFs). We also de- fine collection points (sink nodes) where products are collected to be either consumed or distributed. The different locations define different types of nodes in the network. Three general categories for technologies are considered: biogas recovery, C6/C8 recovery, and hybrid recovery. The hybrid recovery technologies take the intermediate digestate (ID) generated from a C6/C8 recovery technology and send it to a biogas recovery technology, which produces biogas and a final digestate (FD). Within each technology category we consider variants with different capacities and types of waste processed.

8: The nomenclature of the supply chain model is summarized in the Appendix. We define a total set of nodes N, which are split into five types of sub-nodes (subsets): Ncounty,

NCAFO, NWWTP, NLF, and Ncs (product collection sites). We also define a set of products P that include CM, S, FW, ID, biogas, C6, C8, and FD. Figure 2.1 presents a high-level view of the interactions between the different types nodes and products in the supply chain.

The network comprises a set of transportation links (arcs) F=\(i,j,p)i,jEN,pe P}. Every link (i,j, p) E F has an associated sending node i, receiving node j, and product type p. The flow associated with link (i,j,p) E F is given by fi,j,p E R+. The network also comprises of a set of out-of-network sources S = {(i,p) E N, pep} (waste supplies) and a set of out-of-network sinks D = {(i,p)|i E N,PEP} (product demands). The supply flow Si,p E R+ associated with a source (i,p) E S has the following attributes: product type p and sending node i. Similarly, the demand flow di,p E R+ associated with a sink (i, p) E D has the following attributes: product type p and receiving node i.

We also define a set of technologies T, which are split into two categories (subsets): Tbiogas and TC6/C8. Not all technologies are allowed to be installed in all nodes (e.g., CM can only be processed in CAFOs). Consequently, we define subsets Tbiogas,i and TC6/C8,i for each node i E N and note that Tbiogas = UienThiogas,in TC6/C8 = UiENTC6/C8,i and T = Tbiogas TC6/C8. Hybrid technologies are constructed in a modular manner by assembling individual biogas and C6/C8 technologies and connecting them through product flows.
PRODUCT BALANCES. The product balances in the network are given by:
Equation (3.2.1) is a balance for each product at each node. The left-hand side contains

supply Si,p, net generation amount Pi,p, and total inflow EjEN fj,i,p. The right-hand side contains demand di,p and total outflow EjEN fi,j,p. The net generation term Pi,p can be negative (which represents consumption in a technology) or positive (which represents production). Equation (5.2.2) describes the net generation term, which is given by the cumulative net generation in all technologies (xi,p,t) installed at a node. Equation (2.2.3) describes the generation term for each product in each technology (relative to the refer- ence product rt E P) in the form of yield factors At,P (a positive yield factor indicates production while a negative yield indicates consumption).

TECHNOLOGY CAPACITY CONSTRAINTS. Equation (2.2.4) imposes bounds for each tech- nology. If a technology is not installed in a node (Yi,t = 0), the generation amounts are set to zero; otherwise (Yi,t = 1), the generation amounts are bounded by the technology capacity St.

LOGICAL CONSTRAINTS. Equation (2.2.5) is used to enforce the logic that no technolo- gies are allowed to be installed in county nodes and collection sites. Equation (2.2.6) and (2.2.7) enforce the logic that, at CAFO, WWTP, and LF nodes, at most one biogas tech- nology and one C6/C8 technology can be installed. Equation (2.2.8) enforces technology options that are not allowed for node i E N, where T = TThiogas,iUTC6/C8,i.Similar log- ical constraints can be imposed to analyze diverse case studies with different placement restrictions. For example, a decision-maker might be interested in analyzing the impact of installing only certain types of technologies.

ECONOMIC METRIC Equations (3.2.9)-(2.2.12) provide expressions to compute the in- vestment cost Cinv, operational and maintenance cost Com, transportation cost Ctrans, and total cost Ctotal. We consider only a set of technologies with pre-defined capacities that the model can choose from. This allows us to capture nonlinear economies of scale with a linear model (because investment and operating costs Cinv,t, Cop,t are pre-calculated at the different capacities considered). Moreover, from a practical stand-point, we note that technology vendors often only provide certain equipment sizes. Equation (2.2.13) computes income obtained from product sales. The total profit is obtained from (2.2.14).

ENVIRONMENTAL METRIC. Supply chain models reported in the literature often seek to mitigate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (You and Wang, 2011; Zandi Atashbar et al., 2018). In this work, we also consider GHG emissions because this is a major driving force behind waste processing (Ghafoori et al., 2007a). The emissions analyzed in this study include methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2). N2O emissions, although relevant, are not considered here because the technologies considered will have small influence

on them. In the case studies provided we make a simplified analysis on their potential impact. We also note that methane emissions arising from waste collection, land appli- cation, and disposal stages are not considered because these are orders of magnitude smaller than those generated from waste storage (where anaerobic decomposition takes place). For instance, Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2014) estimate methane emissions from cow manure during collection and land application to be 25.3 g. tonne and 7.6 g/ tonne, while methane emissions during six-month storage reach 2500 g/tonne. Limited emissions during collection and land application are due to exposure of the waste to oxygen, which limits anaerobic production of methane. We make the blanket assumption that biogas recovery technologies do not leak methane into the environment but in some cases we analyze the impact of potential leaks. In summary, we only consider direct carbon emis- sions from storage of organic waste and from transportation, and indirect emissions from technologies (resulting from the need of external energy to be operated).

Among the considered carbon emissions, not all of them should be penalized to ac- count for environmental impact. For example, CO2 emitted from biogas combustion is categorized as biotic CO2, which is considered as a part of natural carbon cycle. There- fore, biogas combustion is a carbon-neutral process. Similarly, CO2 emitted during the storage process is biotic. We visualize the carbon conversion process in Figure 2.2, where sources outside circles (total carbon emissions) need to be penalized to mitigate environ- ment impact.

Another important observation is that energy is obtained during biogas combustion, which displaces the use of other fossil fuels. Consequently, an indirect carbon benefit also appears because of displaced fossil fuels used in electricity generation (replaced by bio- gas). In our analysis, we also capture the saved carbon emissions from this displacement.

To summarize, there are two major driving forces associated with carbon emissions: (a) installation of biogas recovery technologies to reduce direct methane emissions from organic waste management and (b) replacement of fossil fuels with biogas for electricity generation.

Considering economic and environmental impact simultaneously leads to a conflict resolution problem. In this work, we use the social cost of carbon (SCC) to monetize environmental impact. SCC is a monetary measure of the long-term economic damage inflicted by a tonne of CO2 emissions in a given year IAWG, US (2010). The U.S. Envi- ronmental Protection Agency (USEPA) and other federal agencies use SCC to evaluate impacts of policies and incentives. Nordhaus (2017) and government studies (IAWG, US, 2010) estimate consistent SCC values of $35-$40 /tonne CO2 during 2015-2020. In this work, we use a nominal SCC value of $38/tonne CO2 in base cases. Sensitivity analysis on the impact of SCC is conducted in our studies.
Figure 2.2: Carbon conversion relationships in the supply chain

Equations (2.2.15)-(2.2.17) give expressions for estimating the total carbon dioxide TCO2 emitted by technologies and by transportation. Equation (2.2.18) captures the total amount of methane TCH4. The (per unit) social cost of carbon (SCC) for CH4 is set to be 28 times of the SCC of CO2. The total social cost of carbon (TSCC) is computed in equation (2.2.19). The saved social cost of carbon (SSCC) can be calculated using (2.2.20) - (2.2.21). In (2.2.20), the coefficient CO2,fuel/biogas represents the average CO2 emissions by fuels when they generate the same amount of electricity as one tonne of biogas.
The net social cost of carbon (NSCC) for the supply chain is computed using equa-

tion (2.2.22). We use NSCC as our final environmental metric, which takes into account the non-biotic CO2 and CH4 emissions and carbon benefits during organic waste man- agement. It is monetized using the SCC, which can help achieve coordination between economic and environmental objectives. A supply chain is considered to be more sus- tainable when it has lower TSCC and NSCC, and higher SSCC.

SOCIAL WELFARE The supply chain problem seeks to maximize the social welfare func- tion:

Maximizing the social welfare seeks to maximize the collective well-being of all stakehold- ers affected by the system (e.g., technology providers, waste suppliers, and consumers of products). In other words, it is assumed that stakeholders cooperate to achieve a com- mon goal. The solution obtained under this approach contrasts with that obtained in a competitive market setting, in which every stakeholder maximizes their own individual objective. The social welfare function used in this work includes economic (the capital in-

vestment cost Cinvi operational and maintenance cost Com, transportation cost Ctrans, and income from recovered products) and environmental factors (GHG emissions weighted by the social cost of carbon). Maximizing the social welfare thus achieves a compromise solution between economic and environmental benefits (the SCC acts as a weight for the environmental metric). The SCC value can be changed in order to capture the trade-off between economic and environmental factors. This will be explored in the case studies presented next.

We point out that other types of socio-economic impacts generated by organic waste can also be counted for in the proposed framework. These include odor and pathogen re- duction and their economic impacts on property values and health. Trading-off multiple objectives simultaneously requires advanced optimization techniques for conflict resolu- tion (Sampat et al., 2017; Dowling et al., 2016). We will explore these issues in future work.

The (per unit) social cost of carbon (SCC) for CH4 is set to be 28 times of the SCC of CO2. The total social cost of carbon (TSCC) is computed in equation (2.2.19). The saved social cost of carbon (SSCC) can be calculated using (2.2.20) - (2.2.21). In (2.2.20), the coefficient CO2,fuel/biogas represents the average CO2 emissions by fuels when they generate the same amount of electricity as one tonne of biogas.
The net social cost of carbon (NSCC) for the supply chain is computed using equa-

tion (2.2.22). We use NSCC as our final environmental metric, which takes into account the non-biotic CO2 and CH4 emissions and carbon benefits during organic waste man- agement. It is monetized using the SCC, which can help achieve coordination between economic and environmental objectives. A supply chain is considered to be more sus- tainable when it has lower TSCC and NSCC, and higher SSCC.

SOCIAL WELFARE The supply chain problem seeks to maximize the social welfare func- tion:

Maximizing the social welfare seeks to maximize the collective well-being of all stakehold- ers affected by the system (e.g., technology providers, waste suppliers, and consumers of products). In other words, it is assumed that stakeholders cooperate to achieve a com- mon goal. The solution obtained under this approach contrasts with that obtained in a competitive market setting, in which every stakeholder maximizes their own individual objective. The social welfare function used in this work includes economic (the capital in-

vestment cost Cinvi operational and maintenance cost Com, transportation cost Ctrans, and income from recovered products) and environmental factors (GHG emissions weighted by the social cost of carbon). Maximizing the social welfare thus achieves a compromise solution between economic and environmental benefits (the SCC acts as a weight for the environmental metric). The SCC value can be changed in order to capture the trade-off between economic and environmental factors. This will be explored in the case studies presented next.

We point out that other types of socio-economic impacts generated by organic waste can also be counted for in the proposed framework. These include odor and pathogen re- duction and their economic impacts on property values and health. Trading-off multiple objectives simultaneously requires advanced optimization techniques for conflict resolu- tion (Sampat et al., 2017; Dowling et al., 2016). We will explore these issues in future work.
10: The nomenclature of the supply chain model is summarized in the Appendix. We define a total set of nodes N, which are split into five types of sub-nodes (subsets): Ncounty,

NCAFO, NWWTP, NLF, and Ncs (product collection sites). We also define a set of products P that include CM, S, FW, ID, biogas, C6, C8, and FD. Figure 2.1 presents a high-level view of the interactions between the different types nodes and products in the supply chain.

The network comprises a set of transportation links (arcs) F=\(i,j,p)i,jEN,pe P}. Every link (i,j, p) E F has an associated sending node i, receiving node j, and product type p. The flow associated with link (i,j,p) E F is given by fi,j,p E R+. The network also comprises of a set of out-of-network sources S = {(i,p) E N, pep} (waste supplies) and a set of out-of-network sinks D = {(i,p)|i E N,PEP} (product demands). The supply flow Si,p E R+ associated with a source (i,p) E S has the following attributes: product type p and sending node i. Similarly, the demand flow di,p E R+ associated with a sink (i, p) E D has the following attributes: product type p and receiving node i.

We also define a set of technologies T, which are split into two categories (subsets): Tbiogas and TC6/C8. Not all technologies are allowed to be installed in all nodes (e.g., CM can only be processed in CAFOs). Consequently, we define subsets Tbiogas,i and TC6/C8,i for each node i E N and note that Tbiogas = UienThiogas,in TC6/C8 = UiENTC6/C8,i and T = Tbiogas TC6/C8. Hybrid technologies are constructed in a modular manner by assembling individual biogas and C6/C8 technologies and connecting them through product flows.
PRODUCT BALANCES. The product balances in the network are given by:
Equation (3.2.1) is a balance for each product at each node. The left-hand side contains

supply Si,p, net generation amount Pi,p, and total inflow EjEN fj,i,p. The right-hand side contains demand di,p and total outflow EjEN fi,j,p. The net generation term Pi,p can be negative (which represents consumption in a technology) or positive (which represents production). Equation (5.2.2) describes the net generation term, which is given by the cumulative net generation in all technologies (xi,p,t) installed at a node. Equation (2.2.3) describes the generation term for each product in each technology (relative to the refer- ence product rt E P) in the form of yield factors At,P (a positive yield factor indicates production while a negative yield indicates consumption).

TECHNOLOGY CAPACITY CONSTRAINTS. Equation (2.2.4) imposes bounds for each tech- nology. If a technology is not installed in a node (Yi,t = 0), the generation amounts are set to zero; otherwise (Yi,t = 1), the generation amounts are bounded by the technology capacity St.

LOGICAL CONSTRAINTS. Equation (2.2.5) is used to enforce the logic that no technolo- gies are allowed to be installed in county nodes and collection sites. Equation (2.2.6) and (2.2.7) enforce the logic that, at CAFO, WWTP, and LF nodes, at most one biogas tech- nology and one C6/C8 technology can be installed. Equation (2.2.8) enforces technology options that are not allowed for node i E N, where T = TThiogas,iUTC6/C8,i.Similar log- ical constraints can be imposed to analyze diverse case studies with different placement restrictions. For example, a decision-maker might be interested in analyzing the impact of installing only certain types of technologies.
(2.2.5) tET E NCAFO U NWWTP UNLF (2.2.6) EThiogas,i

ECONOMIC METRIC Equations (3.2.9)-(2.2.12) provide expressions to compute the in- vestment cost Cinv, operational and maintenance cost Com, transportation cost Ctrans, and total cost Ctotal. We consider only a set of technologies with pre-defined capacities that the model can choose from. This allows us to capture nonlinear economies of scale with a linear model (because investment and operating costs Cinv,t, Cop,t are pre-calculated at the different capacities considered). Moreover, from a practical stand-point, we note that technology vendors often only provide certain equipment sizes. Equation (2.2.13) computes income obtained from product sales. The total profit is obtained from (2.2.14).

ENVIRONMENTAL METRIC. Supply chain models reported in the literature often seek to mitigate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (You and Wang, 2011; Zandi Atashbar et al., 2018). In this work, we also consider GHG emissions because this is a major driving force behind waste processing (Ghafoori et al., 2007a). The emissions analyzed in this study include methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2). N2O emissions, although relevant, are not considered here because the technologies considered will have small influence

on them. In the case studies provided we make a simplified analysis on their potential impact. We also note that methane emissions arising from waste collection, land appli- cation, and disposal stages are not considered because these are orders of magnitude smaller than those generated from waste storage (where anaerobic decomposition takes place). For instance, Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2014) estimate methane emissions from cow manure during collection and land application to be 25.3 g. tonne and 7.6 g/ tonne, while methane emissions during six-month storage reach 2500 g/tonne. Limited emissions during collection and land application are due to exposure of the waste to oxygen, which limits anaerobic production of methane. We make the blanket assumption that biogas recovery technologies do not leak methane into the environment but in some cases we analyze the impact of potential leaks. In summary, we only consider direct carbon emis- sions from storage of organic waste and from transportation, and indirect emissions from technologies (resulting from the need of external energy to be operated).

Among the considered carbon emissions, not all of them should be penalized to ac- count for environmental impact. For example, CO2 emitted from biogas combustion is categorized as biotic CO2, which is considered as a part of natural carbon cycle. There- fore, biogas combustion is a carbon-neutral process. Similarly, CO2 emitted during the storage process is biotic. We visualize the carbon conversion process in Figure 2.2, where sources outside circles (total carbon emissions) need to be penalized to mitigate environ- ment impact.

Another important observation is that energy is obtained during biogas combustion, which displaces the use of other fossil fuels. Consequently, an indirect carbon benefit also appears because of displaced fossil fuels used in electricity generation (replaced by bio- gas). In our analysis, we also capture the saved carbon emissions from this displacement.

To summarize, there are two major driving forces associated with carbon emissions: (a) installation of biogas recovery technologies to reduce direct methane emissions from organic waste management and (b) replacement of fossil fuels with biogas for electricity generation.

Considering economic and environmental impact simultaneously leads to a conflict resolution problem. In this work, we use the social cost of carbon (SCC) to monetize environmental impact. SCC is a monetary measure of the long-term economic damage inflicted by a tonne of CO2 emissions in a given year IAWG, US (2010). The U.S. Envi- ronmental Protection Agency (USEPA) and other federal agencies use SCC to evaluate impacts of policies and incentives. Nordhaus (2017) and government studies (IAWG, US, 2010) estimate consistent SCC values of $35-$40 /tonne CO2 during 2015-2020. In this work, we use a nominal SCC value of $38/tonne CO2 in base cases. Sensitivity analysis on the impact of SCC is conducted in our studies.

For example, CO2 emitted from biogas combustion is categorized as biotic CO2, which is considered as a part of natural carbon cycle. There- fore, biogas combustion is a carbon-neutral process. Similarly, CO2 emitted during the storage process is biotic. We visualize the carbon conversion process in Figure 2.2, where sources outside circles (total carbon emissions) need to be penalized to mitigate environ- ment impact.

Another important observation is that energy is obtained during biogas combustion, which displaces the use of other fossil fuels. Consequently, an indirect carbon benefit also appears because of displaced fossil fuels used in electricity generation (replaced by bio- gas). In our analysis, we also capture the saved carbon emissions from this displacement.

To summarize, there are two major driving forces associated with carbon emissions: (a) installation of biogas recovery technologies to reduce direct methane emissions from organic waste management and (b) replacement of fossil fuels with biogas for electricity generation.

Considering economic and environmental impact simultaneously leads to a conflict resolution problem. In this work, we use the social cost of carbon (SCC) to monetize environmental impact. SCC is a monetary measure of the long-term economic damage inflicted by a tonne of CO2 emissions in a given year IAWG, US (2010). The U.S. Envi- ronmental Protection Agency (USEPA) and other federal agencies use SCC to evaluate impacts of policies and incentives. Nordhaus (2017) and government studies (IAWG, US, 2010) estimate consistent SCC values of $35-$40 /tonne CO2 during 2015-2020. In this work, we use a nominal SCC value of $38/tonne CO2 in base cases. Sensitivity analysis on the impact of SCC is conducted in our studies.
Figure 2.2: Carbon conversion relationships in the supply chain

Equations (2.2.15)-(2.2.17) give expressions for estimating the total carbon dioxide TCO2 emitted by technologies and by transportation. Equation (2.2.18) captures the total amount of methane TCH4. The (per unit) social cost of carbon (SCC) for CH4 is set to be 28 times of the SCC of CO2. The total social cost of carbon (TSCC) is computed in equation (2.2.19). The saved social cost of carbon (SSCC) can be calculated using (2.2.20) - (2.2.21). In (2.2.20), the coefficient CO2,fuel/biogas represents the average CO2 emissions by fuels when they generate the same amount of electricity as one tonne of biogas.
The net social cost of carbon (NSCC) for the supply chain is computed using equa-

tion (2.2.22). We use NSCC as our final environmental metric, which takes into account the non-biotic CO2 and CH4 emissions and carbon benefits during organic waste man- agement. It is monetized using the SCC, which can help achieve coordination between economic and environmental objectives. A supply chain is considered to be more sus- tainable when it has lower TSCC and NSCC, and higher SSCC.

SOCIAL WELFARE The supply chain problem seeks to maximize the social welfare func- tion:

Maximizing the social welfare seeks to maximize the collective well-being of all stakehold- ers affected by the system (e.g., technology providers, waste suppliers, and consumers of products). In other words, it is assumed that stakeholders cooperate to achieve a com- mon goal. The solution obtained under this approach contrasts with that obtained in a competitive market setting, in which every stakeholder maximizes their own individual objective. The social welfare function used in this work includes economic (the capital in-

vestment cost Cinvi operational and maintenance cost Com, transportation cost Ctrans, and income from recovered products) and environmental factors (GHG emissions weighted by the social cost of carbon). Maximizing the social welfare thus achieves a compromise solution between economic and environmental benefits (the SCC acts as a weight for the environmental metric). The SCC value can be changed in order to capture the trade-off between economic and environmental factors. This will be explored in the case studies presented next.

We point out that other types of socio-economic impacts generated by organic waste can also be counted for in the proposed framework. These include odor and pathogen re- duction and their economic impacts on property values and health. Trading-off multiple objectives simultaneously requires advanced optimization techniques for conflict resolu- tion (Sampat et al., 2017; Dowling et al., 2016). We will explore these issues in future work.
13: The nomenclature of the supply chain model is summarized in the Appendix. We define a total set of nodes N, which are split into five types of sub-nodes (subsets): Ncounty,

NCAFO, NWWTP, NLF, and Ncs (product collection sites). We also define a set of products P that include CM, S, FW, ID, biogas, C6, C8, and FD. Figure 2.1 presents a high-level view of the interactions between the different types nodes and products in the supply chain.

The network comprises a set of transportation links (arcs) F=\(i,j,p)i,jEN,pe P}. Every link (i,j, p) E F has an associated sending node i, receiving node j, and product type p. The flow associated with link (i,j,p) E F is given by fi,j,p E R+. The network also comprises of a set of out-of-network sources S = {(i,p) E N, pep} (waste supplies) and a set of out-of-network sinks D = {(i,p)|i E N,PEP} (product demands). The supply flow Si,p E R+ associated with a source (i,p) E S has the following attributes: product type p and sending node i. Similarly, the demand flow di,p E R+ associated with a sink (i, p) E D has the following attributes: product type p and receiving node i.

We also define a set of technologies T, which are split into two categories (subsets): Tbiogas and TC6/C8. Not all technologies are allowed to be installed in all nodes (e.g., CM can only be processed in CAFOs). Consequently, we define subsets Tbiogas,i and TC6/C8,i for each node i E N and note that Tbiogas = UienThiogas,in TC6/C8 = UiENTC6/C8,i and T = Tbiogas TC6/C8. Hybrid technologies are constructed in a modular manner by assembling individual biogas and C6/C8 technologies and connecting them through product flows.
PRODUCT BALANCES. The product balances in the network are given by:
Equation (3.2.1) is a balance for each product at each node. The left-hand side contains

supply Si,p, net generation amount Pi,p, and total inflow EjEN fj,i,p. The right-hand side contains demand di,p and total outflow EjEN fi,j,p. The net generation term Pi,p can be negative (which represents consumption in a technology) or positive (which represents production). Equation (5.2.2) describes the net generation term, which is given by the cumulative net generation in all technologies (xi,p,t) installed at a node. Equation (2.2.3) describes the generation term for each product in each technology (relative to the refer- ence product rt E P) in the form of yield factors At,P (a positive yield factor indicates production while a negative yield indicates consumption).

TECHNOLOGY CAPACITY CONSTRAINTS. Equation (2.2.4) imposes bounds for each tech- nology. If a technology is not installed in a node (Yi,t = 0), the generation amounts are set to zero; otherwise (Yi,t = 1), the generation amounts are bounded by the technology capacity St.

LOGICAL CONSTRAINTS. Equation (2.2.5) is used to enforce the logic that no technolo- gies are allowed to be installed in county nodes and collection sites. Equation (2.2.6) and (2.2.7) enforce the logic that, at CAFO, WWTP, and LF nodes, at most one biogas tech- nology and one C6/C8 technology can be installed. Equation (2.2.8) enforces technology options that are not allowed for node i E N, where T = TThiogas,iUTC6/C8,i.Similar log- ical constraints can be imposed to analyze diverse case studies with different placement restrictions. For example, a decision-maker might be interested in analyzing the impact of installing only certain types of technologies.
(2.2.5) tET E NCAFO U NWWTP UNLF (2.2.6) EThiogas,i

ECONOMIC METRIC Equations (3.2.9)-(2.2.12) provide expressions to compute the in- vestment cost Cinv, operational and maintenance cost Com, transportation cost Ctrans, and total cost Ctotal. We consider only a set of technologies with pre-defined capacities that the model can choose from. This allows us to capture nonlinear economies of scale with a linear model (because investment and operating costs Cinv,t, Cop,t are pre-calculated at the different capacities considered). Moreover, from a practical stand-point, we note that technology vendors often only provide certain equipment sizes. Equation (2.2.13) computes income obtained from product sales. The total profit is obtained from (2.2.14).

ENVIRONMENTAL METRIC. Supply chain models reported in the literature often seek to mitigate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (You and Wang, 2011; Zandi Atashbar et al., 2018). In this work, we also consider GHG emissions because this is a major driving force behind waste processing (Ghafoori et al., 2007a). The emissions analyzed in this study include methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2). N2O emissions, although relevant, are not considered here because the technologies considered will have small influence

on them. In the case studies provided we make a simplified analysis on their potential impact. We also note that methane emissions arising from waste collection, land appli- cation, and disposal stages are not considered because these are orders of magnitude smaller than those generated from waste storage (where anaerobic decomposition takes place). For instance, Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2014) estimate methane emissions from cow manure during collection and land application to be 25.3 g. tonne and 7.6 g/ tonne, while methane emissions during six-month storage reach 2500 g/tonne. Limited emissions during collection and land application are due to exposure of the waste to oxygen, which limits anaerobic production of methane. We make the blanket assumption that biogas recovery technologies do not leak methane into the environment but in some cases we analyze the impact of potential leaks. In summary, we only consider direct carbon emis- sions from storage of organic waste and from transportation, and indirect emissions from technologies (resulting from the need of external energy to be operated).

Among the considered carbon emissions, not all of them should be penalized to ac- count for environmental impact. For example, CO2 emitted from biogas combustion is categorized as biotic CO2, which is considered as a part of natural carbon cycle. There- fore, biogas combustion is a carbon-neutral process. Similarly, CO2 emitted during the storage process is biotic. We visualize the carbon conversion process in Figure 2.2, where sources outside circles (total carbon emissions) need to be penalized to mitigate environ- ment impact.

Another important observation is that energy is obtained during biogas combustion, which displaces the use of other fossil fuels. Consequently, an indirect carbon benefit also appears because of displaced fossil fuels used in electricity generation (replaced by bio- gas). In our analysis, we also capture the saved carbon emissions from this displacement.

To summarize, there are two major driving forces associated with carbon emissions: (a) installation of biogas recovery technologies to reduce direct methane emissions from organic waste management and (b) replacement of fossil fuels with biogas for electricity generation.

Considering economic and environmental impact simultaneously leads to a conflict resolution problem. In this work, we use the social cost of carbon (SCC) to monetize environmental impact. SCC is a monetary measure of the long-term economic damage inflicted by a tonne of CO2 emissions in a given year IAWG, US (2010). The U.S. Envi- ronmental Protection Agency (USEPA) and other federal agencies use SCC to evaluate impacts of policies and incentives. Nordhaus (2017) and government studies (IAWG, US, 2010) estimate consistent SCC values of $35-$40 /tonne CO2 during 2015-2020. In this work, we use a nominal SCC value of $38/tonne CO2 in base cases. Sensitivity analysis on the impact of SCC is conducted in our studies.
