ENVIRONMENTAL METRIC. Supply chain models reported in the literature often seek to mitigate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (You and Wang, 2011; Zandi Atashbar et al., 2018). In this work, we also consider GHG emissions because this is a major driving force behind waste processing (Ghafoori et al., 2007a). The emissions analyzed in this study include methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2). N2O emissions, although relevant, are not considered here because the technologies considered will have small influence

on them. In the case studies provided we make a simplified analysis on their potential impact. We also note that methane emissions arising from waste collection, land appli- cation, and disposal stages are not considered because these are orders of magnitude smaller than those generated from waste storage (where anaerobic decomposition takes place). For instance, Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2014) estimate methane emissions from cow manure during collection and land application to be 25.3 g. tonne and 7.6 g/ tonne, while methane emissions during six-month storage reach 2500 g/tonne. Limited emissions during collection and land application are due to exposure of the waste to oxygen, which limits anaerobic production of methane. We make the blanket assumption that biogas recovery technologies do not leak methane into the environment but in some cases we analyze the impact of potential leaks. In summary, we only consider direct carbon emis- sions from storage of organic waste and from transportation, and indirect emissions from technologies (resulting from the need of external energy to be operated).

Among the considered carbon emissions, not all of them should be penalized to ac- count for environmental impact. For example, CO2 emitted from biogas combustion is categorized as biotic CO2, which is considered as a part of natural carbon cycle. There- fore, biogas combustion is a carbon-neutral process. Similarly, CO2 emitted during the storage process is biotic. We visualize the carbon conversion process in Figure 2.2, where sources outside circles (total carbon emissions) need to be penalized to mitigate environ- ment impact.

Another important observation is that energy is obtained during biogas combustion, which displaces the use of other fossil fuels. Consequently, an indirect carbon benefit also appears because of displaced fossil fuels used in electricity generation (replaced by bio- gas). In our analysis, we also capture the saved carbon emissions from this displacement.

To summarize, there are two major driving forces associated with carbon emissions: (a) installation of biogas recovery technologies to reduce direct methane emissions from organic waste management and (b) replacement of fossil fuels with biogas for electricity generation.

Considering economic and environmental impact simultaneously leads to a conflict resolution problem. In this work, we use the social cost of carbon (SCC) to monetize environmental impact. SCC is a monetary measure of the long-term economic damage inflicted by a tonne of CO2 emissions in a given year IAWG, US (2010). The U.S. Envi- ronmental Protection Agency (USEPA) and other federal agencies use SCC to evaluate impacts of policies and incentives. Nordhaus (2017) and government studies (IAWG, US, 2010) estimate consistent SCC values of $35-$40 /tonne CO2 during 2015-2020. In this work, we use a nominal SCC value of $38/tonne CO2 in base cases. Sensitivity analysis on the impact of SCC is conducted in our studies.
Figure 2.2: Carbon conversion relationships in the supply chain

Equations (2.2.15)-(2.2.17) give expressions for estimating the total carbon dioxide TCO2 emitted by technologies and by transportation. Equation (2.2.18) captures the total amount of methane TCH4. The (per unit) social cost of carbon (SCC) for CH4 is set to be 28 times of the SCC of CO2. The total social cost of carbon (TSCC) is computed in equation (2.2.19). The saved social cost of carbon (SSCC) can be calculated using (2.2.20) - (2.2.21). In (2.2.20), the coefficient CO2,fuel/biogas represents the average CO2 emissions by fuels when they generate the same amount of electricity as one tonne of biogas.
The net social cost of carbon (NSCC) for the supply chain is computed using equa-

tion (2.2.22). We use NSCC as our final environmental metric, which takes into account the non-biotic CO2 and CH4 emissions and carbon benefits during organic waste man- agement. It is monetized using the SCC, which can help achieve coordination between economic and environmental objectives. A supply chain is considered to be more sus- tainable when it has lower TSCC and NSCC, and higher SSCC.

SOCIAL WELFARE The supply chain problem seeks to maximize the social welfare func- tion:

Maximizing the social welfare seeks to maximize the collective well-being of all stakehold- ers affected by the system (e.g., technology providers, waste suppliers, and consumers of products). In other words, it is assumed that stakeholders cooperate to achieve a com- mon goal. The solution obtained under this approach contrasts with that obtained in a competitive market setting, in which every stakeholder maximizes their own individual objective. The social welfare function used in this work includes economic (the capital in-

vestment cost Cinvi operational and maintenance cost Com, transportation cost Ctrans, and income from recovered products) and environmental factors (GHG emissions weighted by the social cost of carbon). Maximizing the social welfare thus achieves a compromise solution between economic and environmental benefits (the SCC acts as a weight for the environmental metric). The SCC value can be changed in order to capture the trade-off between economic and environmental factors. This will be explored in the case studies presented next.

We point out that other types of socio-economic impacts generated by organic waste can also be counted for in the proposed framework. These include odor and pathogen re- duction and their economic impacts on property values and health. Trading-off multiple objectives simultaneously requires advanced optimization techniques for conflict resolu- tion (Sampat et al., 2017; Dowling et al., 2016). We will explore these issues in future work.
16: The nomenclature of the supply chain model is summarized in the Appendix. We define a total set of nodes N, which are split into five types of sub-nodes (subsets): Ncounty,

NCAFO, NWWTP, NLF, and Ncs (product collection sites). We also define a set of products P that include CM, S, FW, ID, biogas, C6, C8, and FD. Figure 2.1 presents a high-level view of the interactions between the different types nodes and products in the supply chain.

The network comprises a set of transportation links (arcs) F=\(i,j,p)i,jEN,pe P}. Every link (i,j, p) E F has an associated sending node i, receiving node j, and product type p. The flow associated with link (i,j,p) E F is given by fi,j,p E R+. The network also comprises of a set of out-of-network sources S = {(i,p) E N, pep} (waste supplies) and a set of out-of-network sinks D = {(i,p)|i E N,PEP} (product demands). The supply flow Si,p E R+ associated with a source (i,p) E S has the following attributes: product type p and sending node i. Similarly, the demand flow di,p E R+ associated with a sink (i, p) E D has the following attributes: product type p and receiving node i.

We also define a set of technologies T, which are split into two categories (subsets): Tbiogas and TC6/C8. Not all technologies are allowed to be installed in all nodes (e.g., CM can only be processed in CAFOs). Consequently, we define subsets Tbiogas,i and TC6/C8,i for each node i E N and note that Tbiogas = UienThiogas,in TC6/C8 = UiENTC6/C8,i and T = Tbiogas TC6/C8. Hybrid technologies are constructed in a modular manner by assembling individual biogas and C6/C8 technologies and connecting them through product flows.
PRODUCT BALANCES. The product balances in the network are given by:
Equation (3.2.1) is a balance for each product at each node. The left-hand side contains

supply Si,p, net generation amount Pi,p, and total inflow EjEN fj,i,p. The right-hand side contains demand di,p and total outflow EjEN fi,j,p. The net generation term Pi,p can be negative (which represents consumption in a technology) or positive (which represents production). Equation (5.2.2) describes the net generation term, which is given by the cumulative net generation in all technologies (xi,p,t) installed at a node. Equation (2.2.3) describes the generation term for each product in each technology (relative to the refer- ence product rt E P) in the form of yield factors At,P (a positive yield factor indicates production while a negative yield indicates consumption).

TECHNOLOGY CAPACITY CONSTRAINTS. Equation (2.2.4) imposes bounds for each tech- nology. If a technology is not installed in a node (Yi,t = 0), the generation amounts are set to zero; otherwise (Yi,t = 1), the generation amounts are bounded by the technology capacity St.

LOGICAL CONSTRAINTS. Equation (2.2.5) is used to enforce the logic that no technolo- gies are allowed to be installed in county nodes and collection sites. Equation (2.2.6) and (2.2.7) enforce the logic that, at CAFO, WWTP, and LF nodes, at most one biogas tech- nology and one C6/C8 technology can be installed. Equation (2.2.8) enforces technology options that are not allowed for node i E N, where T = TThiogas,iUTC6/C8,i.Similar log- ical constraints can be imposed to analyze diverse case studies with different placement restrictions. For example, a decision-maker might be interested in analyzing the impact of installing only certain types of technologies.
(2.2.5) tET E NCAFO U NWWTP UNLF (2.2.6) EThiogas,i

ECONOMIC METRIC Equations (3.2.9)-(2.2.12) provide expressions to compute the in- vestment cost Cinv, operational and maintenance cost Com, transportation cost Ctrans, and total cost Ctotal. We consider only a set of technologies with pre-defined capacities that the model can choose from. This allows us to capture nonlinear economies of scale with a linear model (because investment and operating costs Cinv,t, Cop,t are pre-calculated at the different capacities considered). Moreover, from a practical stand-point, we note that technology vendors often only provide certain equipment sizes. Equation (2.2.13) computes income obtained from product sales. The total profit is obtained from (2.2.14).

TECHNOLOGY CAPACITY CONSTRAINTS. Equation (2.2.4) imposes bounds for each tech- nology. If a technology is not installed in a node (Yi,t = 0), the generation amounts are set to zero; otherwise (Yi,t = 1), the generation amounts are bounded by the technology capacity St.

LOGICAL CONSTRAINTS. Equation (2.2.5) is used to enforce the logic that no technolo- gies are allowed to be installed in county nodes and collection sites. Equation (2.2.6) and (2.2.7) enforce the logic that, at CAFO, WWTP, and LF nodes, at most one biogas tech- nology and one C6/C8 technology can be installed. Equation (2.2.8) enforces technology options that are not allowed for node i E N, where T = TThiogas,iUTC6/C8,i.Similar log- ical constraints can be imposed to analyze diverse case studies with different placement restrictions. For example, a decision-maker might be interested in analyzing the impact of installing only certain types of technologies.
(2.2.5) tET E NCAFO U NWWTP UNLF (2.2.6) EThiogas,i

ECONOMIC METRIC Equations (3.2.9)-(2.2.12) provide expressions to compute the in- vestment cost Cinv, operational and maintenance cost Com, transportation cost Ctrans, and total cost Ctotal. We consider only a set of technologies with pre-defined capacities that the model can choose from. This allows us to capture nonlinear economies of scale with a linear model (because investment and operating costs Cinv,t, Cop,t are pre-calculated at the different capacities considered). Moreover, from a practical stand-point, we note that technology vendors often only provide certain equipment sizes. Equation (2.2.13) computes income obtained from product sales. The total profit is obtained from (2.2.14).

ENVIRONMENTAL METRIC. Supply chain models reported in the literature often seek to mitigate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (You and Wang, 2011; Zandi Atashbar et al., 2018). In this work, we also consider GHG emissions because this is a major driving force behind waste processing (Ghafoori et al., 2007a). The emissions analyzed in this study include methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2). N2O emissions, although relevant, are not considered here because the technologies considered will have small influence

on them. In the case studies provided we make a simplified analysis on their potential impact. We also note that methane emissions arising from waste collection, land appli- cation, and disposal stages are not considered because these are orders of magnitude smaller than those generated from waste storage (where anaerobic decomposition takes place). For instance, Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2014) estimate methane emissions from cow manure during collection and land application to be 25.3 g. tonne and 7.6 g/ tonne, while methane emissions during six-month storage reach 2500 g/tonne. Limited emissions during collection and land application are due to exposure of the waste to oxygen, which limits anaerobic production of methane. We make the blanket assumption that biogas recovery technologies do not leak methane into the environment but in some cases we analyze the impact of potential leaks. In summary, we only consider direct carbon emis- sions from storage of organic waste and from transportation, and indirect emissions from technologies (resulting from the need of external energy to be operated).

Among the considered carbon emissions, not all of them should be penalized to ac- count for environmental impact. For example, CO2 emitted from biogas combustion is categorized as biotic CO2, which is considered as a part of natural carbon cycle. There- fore, biogas combustion is a carbon-neutral process. Similarly, CO2 emitted during the storage process is biotic. We visualize the carbon conversion process in Figure 2.2, where sources outside circles (total carbon emissions) need to be penalized to mitigate environ- ment impact.

Another important observation is that energy is obtained during biogas combustion, which displaces the use of other fossil fuels. Consequently, an indirect carbon benefit also appears because of displaced fossil fuels used in electricity generation (replaced by bio- gas). In our analysis, we also capture the saved carbon emissions from this displacement.

To summarize, there are two major driving forces associated with carbon emissions: (a) installation of biogas recovery technologies to reduce direct methane emissions from organic waste management and (b) replacement of fossil fuels with biogas for electricity generation.

Considering economic and environmental impact simultaneously leads to a conflict resolution problem. In this work, we use the social cost of carbon (SCC) to monetize environmental impact. SCC is a monetary measure of the long-term economic damage inflicted by a tonne of CO2 emissions in a given year IAWG, US (2010). The U.S. Envi- ronmental Protection Agency (USEPA) and other federal agencies use SCC to evaluate impacts of policies and incentives. Nordhaus (2017) and government studies (IAWG, US, 2010) estimate consistent SCC values of $35-$40 /tonne CO2 during 2015-2020. In this work, we use a nominal SCC value of $38/tonne CO2 in base cases. Sensitivity analysis on the impact of SCC is conducted in our studies.
Figure 2.2: Carbon conversion relationships in the supply chain

Equations (2.2.15)-(2.2.17) give expressions for estimating the total carbon dioxide TCO2 emitted by technologies and by transportation. Equation (2.2.18) captures the total amount of methane TCH4. The (per unit) social cost of carbon (SCC) for CH4 is set to be 28 times of the SCC of CO2. The total social cost of carbon (TSCC) is computed in equation (2.2.19). The saved social cost of carbon (SSCC) can be calculated using (2.2.20) - (2.2.21). In (2.2.20), the coefficient CO2,fuel/biogas represents the average CO2 emissions by fuels when they generate the same amount of electricity as one tonne of biogas.
The net social cost of carbon (NSCC) for the supply chain is computed using equa-

tion (2.2.22). We use NSCC as our final environmental metric, which takes into account the non-biotic CO2 and CH4 emissions and carbon benefits during organic waste man- agement. It is monetized using the SCC, which can help achieve coordination between economic and environmental objectives. A supply chain is considered to be more sus- tainable when it has lower TSCC and NSCC, and higher SSCC.
|(2.2.15) = iENjENPEP|(2.2.16) TCO2 =|(2.2.17) PEPiEN|(2.2.18) TSCC=SCC.TCO2+SCCCH4TCH4|(2.2.19) TCO2,fuel =|(2.2.20) SSCC=SCC TTO2,fuel|(2.2.21) NSCC = TSCC-SSCC.|(2.2.22)
SOCIAL WELFARE The supply chain problem seeks to maximize the social welfare func- tion:

Maximizing the social welfare seeks to maximize the collective well-being of all stakehold- ers affected by the system (e.g., technology providers, waste suppliers, and consumers of products). In other words, it is assumed that stakeholders cooperate to achieve a com- mon goal. The solution obtained under this approach contrasts with that obtained in a competitive market setting, in which every stakeholder maximizes their own individual objective. The social welfare function used in this work includes economic (the capital in-

vestment cost Cinvi operational and maintenance cost Com, transportation cost Ctrans, and income from recovered products) and environmental factors (GHG emissions weighted by the social cost of carbon). Maximizing the social welfare thus achieves a compromise solution between economic and environmental benefits (the SCC acts as a weight for the environmental metric). The SCC value can be changed in order to capture the trade-off between economic and environmental factors. This will be explored in the case studies presented next.

We point out that other types of socio-economic impacts generated by organic waste can also be counted for in the proposed framework. These include odor and pathogen re- duction and their economic impacts on property values and health. Trading-off multiple objectives simultaneously requires advanced optimization techniques for conflict resolu- tion (Sampat et al., 2017; Dowling et al., 2016). We will explore these issues in future work.
19: The nomenclature of the supply chain model is summarized in the Appendix. We define a total set of nodes N, which are split into five types of sub-nodes (subsets): Ncounty,

NCAFO, NWWTP, NLF, and Ncs (product collection sites). We also define a set of products P that include CM, S, FW, ID, biogas, C6, C8, and FD. Figure 2.1 presents a high-level view of the interactions between the different types nodes and products in the supply chain.

The network comprises a set of transportation links (arcs) F=\(i,j,p)i,jEN,pe P}. Every link (i,j, p) E F has an associated sending node i, receiving node j, and product type p. The flow associated with link (i,j,p) E F is given by fi,j,p E R+. The network also comprises of a set of out-of-network sources S = {(i,p) E N, pep} (waste supplies) and a set of out-of-network sinks D = {(i,p)|i E N,PEP} (product demands). The supply flow Si,p E R+ associated with a source (i,p) E S has the following attributes: product type p and sending node i. Similarly, the demand flow di,p E R+ associated with a sink (i, p) E D has the following attributes: product type p and receiving node i.

We also define a set of technologies T, which are split into two categories (subsets): Tbiogas and TC6/C8. Not all technologies are allowed to be installed in all nodes (e.g., CM can only be processed in CAFOs). Consequently, we define subsets Tbiogas,i and TC6/C8,i for each node i E N and note that Tbiogas = UienThiogas,in TC6/C8 = UiENTC6/C8,i and T = Tbiogas TC6/C8. Hybrid technologies are constructed in a modular manner by assembling individual biogas and C6/C8 technologies and connecting them through product flows.
PRODUCT BALANCES. The product balances in the network are given by:
Equation (3.2.1) is a balance for each product at each node. The left-hand side contains

supply Si,p, net generation amount Pi,p, and total inflow EjEN fj,i,p. The right-hand side contains demand di,p and total outflow EjEN fi,j,p. The net generation term Pi,p can be negative (which represents consumption in a technology) or positive (which represents production). Equation (5.2.2) describes the net generation term, which is given by the cumulative net generation in all technologies (xi,p,t) installed at a node. Equation (2.2.3) describes the generation term for each product in each technology (relative to the refer- ence product rt E P) in the form of yield factors At,P (a positive yield factor indicates production while a negative yield indicates consumption).

TECHNOLOGY CAPACITY CONSTRAINTS. Equation (2.2.4) imposes bounds for each tech- nology. If a technology is not installed in a node (Yi,t = 0), the generation amounts are set to zero; otherwise (Yi,t = 1), the generation amounts are bounded by the technology capacity St.

LOGICAL CONSTRAINTS. Equation (2.2.5) is used to enforce the logic that no technolo- gies are allowed to be installed in county nodes and collection sites. Equation (2.2.6) and (2.2.7) enforce the logic that, at CAFO, WWTP, and LF nodes, at most one biogas tech- nology and one C6/C8 technology can be installed. Equation (2.2.8) enforces technology options that are not allowed for node i E N, where T = TThiogas,iUTC6/C8,i.Similar log- ical constraints can be imposed to analyze diverse case studies with different placement restrictions. For example, a decision-maker might be interested in analyzing the impact of installing only certain types of technologies.

21: <header>2.3 Case Studies for the State of Wisconsin </header>
We consider diverse studies to analyze the impact of technologies on the social welfare:

<list>Do nothing case: All waste remains unprocessed and is directly transported to end sites (landfills and farms). The model in this case is a simple optimization problem that aims to minimize transportation cost and associated emissions (finds the best route for the waste streams). 
Biogas only case: C6/C8 technologies are not allowed to be installed. The model will determine optimal locations and sizes for biogas recovery systems along with optimal transportation routes for waste and product streams. 
C6/C8 only case: biogas technologies are not allowed to be installed. The model will determine optimal locations and sizes for C6/C8 recovery systems along with optimal transportation routes for waste and product streams.</list>

Hybrid biogas/C6/C8 case: all technologies are allowed to be installed. The model will determine optimal locations and capacities for all single and hybrid technolo- gies as well as waste and product transportation flows.
All scripts and data needed to reproduce the results are available at https :://github. com/zavalab/JuliaBox/tree/master/C6C8Recovery.
22: Locations for CAFOs and LFs are obtained from the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources (Wisconsin DNR). The collection point for C6 and C8 is assumed to be at the center of the state. We make this choice because C6/C8 markets are not well developed in the state. This is a conservative choice because multiple collection points can help reduce transportation costs. However, our results obtained are not sensitive to the location of the collection point, because the amount of C6/C8 is small compared to that of waste. We only consider WWTPs reported by the American Biogas Council.

WASTE SOURCE DATA: For CM, the supply values are estimated based on the average manure produced per cow per day (Lorimor et al., 2008), the total animal units of cows in each CAFO, and the average breakdown of different types of cows located at CAFOs (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017). For S, the supply values are estimated based on the sludge generated per human per day and the population in each county. Such values are based on a case study performed by the Madison Metropolitan Sewerage District (personal communication). For FW, the supply values are estimated using the average FW produced per human per day (Gustavsson et al., 2011) (excluding production and retail stages) and the population in each county.

TECHNOLOGY DATA: It is assumed that each candidate location has waste collection storage systems. The biogas recovery technologies comprise of an AD, a gas purification system, an electricity generator, and a separation system (screw press) (see Figure 2.3 (a)).

A power law expression regressed from 23 case studies reported in Beddoes et al. (2007) is used to compute AD costs, electricity generator costs, and operational costs for biogas recovery technologies of different capacities (Table S9). The power law captures effects of economies of scale. For the purification operation, it is reported that the biogas cleaning cost is in the range of 0.03 - 0.14 - USD/m³ (Beddoes et al., 2007). We use an average value of 0.08 USD/m³ and consider this an operational cost.

For C6/C8 recovery technologies, we consider laboratory-scale data reported in the literature (to our knowledge, there is no performance data available on full-scale installa- tions). Existing experimental systems include the following processing steps: acid-phase digestion system, filtration, liquid-liquid extraction, and refining (Kucek et al., 2016; Agler et al., 2012). It is assumed that the full scale system will follow the same steps (see Figure 2.3(b)). We account for uncertainties in technology performance by conducting sensitivity

Equipment costs are scaled from the values reported in Scarborough et al. (2017) by using a six-tenths power law. The costs for the reactor and related pumps are scaled directly by using the feed flow. Costs for separation units are scaled by using the product amount. We have found that the total equipment costs for C6/C8 technologies is lower than that of biogas technologies. This is because the acid-phase digester system needed is smaller than an AD system, thus requiring a shorter residence time than AD. The operational costs of C6/C8 recovery include routine maintenance costs, utility costs, base costs, and solvent costs. Maintenance costs are taken as 10% of the total equipment costs (Ulrich and Vasudevan, 2004). The utility costs are calculated based on the energy demand of the entire technology. A basic solution is used to adjust the pH in the reactor. The basic solution and solvent costs are also scaled from Scarborough et al. (2017). The total investment and operational costs of C6/C8 technologies are summarized in Table S10.

The creation of hybrid biogas/C6/C8 technologies follows naturally from the cou- pling C6/C8 and biogas technologies via the intermediate digestate flows. The supply chain framework automatically decides if C6/C8 and biogas technologies should be co- located at a given node (this does not require transportation of intermediate digestate) or should be placed in different nodes (this requires transportation). This illustrates that our supply chain framework is general and can accommodate interconnections between multiple technologies.

TRANSPORTATION DATA: The per unit transportation cost Ctrans is set to 0.16 USD/- tonne/km. This value is calculated based on the diesel consumed per tonne of sludge per kilometer by trucks (Yoshida et al., 2013), the average diesel price in USA, and the fraction of fuel cost in the total transportation cost (Trego and Murray, 2010). We use standard carbon emissions from combustion of diesel reported by the U.S. EPA (22.4 lb CO2/gallon diesel) to estimate the transportation emission constant CO2,trans, which is set

PRODUCT YIELD DATA: The yield factors for biogas from CM and FW are calculated using equation S1. Studies reported in the literature show that waste mixing can result in higher biogas yields (El-Mashad and Zhang, 2010; Zhang et al., 2013a; Dai et al., 2013; Neves et al., 2009). The yield factors reported, however, vary significantly (depending on the experimental setting). In this study we use a linear combination to calculate the yields. In addition, in order to reduce the number of products in our model, we only consider two mixture types: one composed of 90% CM and 10% FW (denoted as MX1) and one composed of 90% S and 10% FW (denoted as MX2) based on the total amount of each waste.

The yield factors for C6/C8 from organic waste are a major source of uncertainty. Scarborough et al. (2017) report that the average conversion to C6 and C8 is 16.3% COD and 1.7% COD, respectively in long-term experiments using biomass as substrate (i.e., the COD of C6 produced is 16.3% of the COD in the loaded biomass). As a first estimate, we assume that organic waste can achieve a similar COD conversion. A COD value for each waste stream is estimated based on the biogas yield factor and the COD conversion to biogas (Davis et al., 2013), which is 238 g-CH4/kg COD removed. The yields for C6 and C8 from waste mixtures are calculated by using a linear com- bination because the COD of the mixture is calculated by a linear combination (e.g., YieldC6,MX1 = 0.9Yieldc6,CM + 0.1Yieldc6,FW). When using the intermediate digestate (ID) from the C6/C8 technology to produce biogas, it is assumed that the remaining COD content can achieve the corresponding biogas yield (e.g. if 20% COD is removed during C6/C8 process, the biogas yield of ID will be 80% of the original number). The yield factors used in our studies are summarized in Table S11.
PRODUCT VALUES: The biogas market value is uncertain and depends on the electricity generation efficiency and the local electricity price. We set the biogas value to be 100

USD/tonne (0.087 USD/m³), which is calculated based on the energy value of biogas, an electricity conversion efficiency of 30%, and an average electricity price of 0.08 USD/kWh in Wisconsin. The values for C6 and C8 are set to 2,800 USD/tonne and 5,000 USD/tonne respectively (Scarborough et al., 2017).

CARBON EMISSIONS DATA: It is assumed that all untreated waste (CM, S, FW, and ID) will release CO2 and methane to the atmosphere during storage. Although C6 and C8 are not used as fuel, it is assumed that they will emit CO2 in the long-term due to degradation. The methane emission coefficient is calculated using equations S2 - S3. The emission factors in both summer and winter periods are estimated and an average value is used.
For the C6/C8 technologies, the CO2 emission factor is estimated from energy con- sumption. Data on emission factors for each product is also summarized in Table S11.

The carbon benefit obtained from biogas production varies in the range of 1.8 - 4.2 kg-CO/kg biogas (Row and Neabel, 2005; Yiridoe et al., 2009). In this work, this value is calculated by using the heating value of biogas (Murphy et al., 2004), the efficiency of electricity generation (Lantz, 2012a), and the average CO2 emissions associated with electricity generation using fossil fuels (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). The carbon benefit in our studies is set to 1.07 kg-CO2/kg-biogas.
Figure 2.4: Economic and environmental metrics (Unit: Million USD/yr)

In this work, this value is calculated by using the heating value of biogas (Murphy et al., 2004), the efficiency of electricity generation (Lantz, 2012a), and the average CO2 emissions associated with electricity generation using fossil fuels (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). The carbon benefit in our studies is set to 1.07 kg-CO2/kg-biogas.
Figure 2.4: Economic and environmental metrics (Unit: Million USD/yr)
26: Locations for CAFOs and LFs are obtained from the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources (Wisconsin DNR). The collection point for C6 and C8 is assumed to be at the center of the state. We make this choice because C6/C8 markets are not well developed in the state. This is a conservative choice because multiple collection points can help reduce transportation costs. However, our results obtained are not sensitive to the location of the collection point, because the amount of C6/C8 is small compared to that of waste. We only consider WWTPs reported by the American Biogas Council.

WASTE SOURCE DATA: For CM, the supply values are estimated based on the average manure produced per cow per day (Lorimor et al., 2008), the total animal units of cows in each CAFO, and the average breakdown of different types of cows located at CAFOs (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017). For S, the supply values are estimated based on the sludge generated per human per day and the population in each county. Such values are based on a case study performed by the Madison Metropolitan Sewerage District (personal communication). For FW, the supply values are estimated using the average FW produced per human per day (Gustavsson et al., 2011) (excluding production and retail stages) and the population in each county.

TECHNOLOGY DATA: It is assumed that each candidate location has waste collection storage systems. The biogas recovery technologies comprise of an AD, a gas purification system, an electricity generator, and a separation system (screw press) (see Figure 2.3 (a)).

A power law expression regressed from 23 case studies reported in Beddoes et al. (2007) is used to compute AD costs, electricity generator costs, and operational costs for biogas recovery technologies of different capacities (Table S9). The power law captures effects of economies of scale. For the purification operation, it is reported that the biogas cleaning cost is in the range of 0.03 - 0.14 - USD/m³ (Beddoes et al., 2007). We use an average value of 0.08 USD/m³ and consider this an operational cost.

For C6/C8 recovery technologies, we consider laboratory-scale data reported in the literature (to our knowledge, there is no performance data available on full-scale installa- tions). Existing experimental systems include the following processing steps: acid-phase digestion system, filtration, liquid-liquid extraction, and refining (Kucek et al., 2016; Agler et al., 2012). It is assumed that the full scale system will follow the same steps (see Figure 2.3(b)). We account for uncertainties in technology performance by conducting sensitivity

Equipment costs are scaled from the values reported in Scarborough et al. (2017) by using a six-tenths power law. The costs for the reactor and related pumps are scaled directly by using the feed flow. Costs for separation units are scaled by using the product amount. We have found that the total equipment costs for C6/C8 technologies is lower than that of biogas technologies. This is because the acid-phase digester system needed is smaller than an AD system, thus requiring a shorter residence time than AD. The operational costs of C6/C8 recovery include routine maintenance costs, utility costs, base costs, and solvent costs. Maintenance costs are taken as 10% of the total equipment costs (Ulrich and Vasudevan, 2004). The utility costs are calculated based on the energy demand of the entire technology. A basic solution is used to adjust the pH in the reactor. The basic solution and solvent costs are also scaled from Scarborough et al. (2017). The total investment and operational costs of C6/C8 technologies are summarized in Table S10.

The creation of hybrid biogas/C6/C8 technologies follows naturally from the cou- pling C6/C8 and biogas technologies via the intermediate digestate flows. The supply chain framework automatically decides if C6/C8 and biogas technologies should be co- located at a given node (this does not require transportation of intermediate digestate) or should be placed in different nodes (this requires transportation). This illustrates that our supply chain framework is general and can accommodate interconnections between multiple technologies.

TRANSPORTATION DATA: The per unit transportation cost Ctrans is set to 0.16 USD/- tonne/km. This value is calculated based on the diesel consumed per tonne of sludge per kilometer by trucks (Yoshida et al., 2013), the average diesel price in USA, and the fraction of fuel cost in the total transportation cost (Trego and Murray, 2010). We use standard carbon emissions from combustion of diesel reported by the U.S. EPA (22.4 lb CO2/gallon diesel) to estimate the transportation emission constant CO2,trans, which is set

PRODUCT YIELD DATA: The yield factors for biogas from CM and FW are calculated using equation S1. Studies reported in the literature show that waste mixing can result in higher biogas yields (El-Mashad and Zhang, 2010; Zhang et al., 2013a; Dai et al., 2013; Neves et al., 2009). The yield factors reported, however, vary significantly (depending on the experimental setting). In this study we use a linear combination to calculate the yields. In addition, in order to reduce the number of products in our model, we only consider two mixture types: one composed of 90% CM and 10% FW (denoted as MX1) and one composed of 90% S and 10% FW (denoted as MX2) based on the total amount of each waste.

The yield factors for C6/C8 from organic waste are a major source of uncertainty. Scarborough et al. (2017) report that the average conversion to C6 and C8 is 16.3% COD and 1.7% COD, respectively in long-term experiments using biomass as substrate (i.e., the COD of C6 produced is 16.3% of the COD in the loaded biomass). As a first estimate, we assume that organic waste can achieve a similar COD conversion. A COD value for each waste stream is estimated based on the biogas yield factor and the COD conversion to biogas (Davis et al., 2013), which is 238 g-CH4/kg COD removed. The yields for C6 and C8 from waste mixtures are calculated by using a linear com- bination because the COD of the mixture is calculated by a linear combination (e.g., YieldC6,MX1 = 0.9Yieldc6,CM + 0.1Yieldc6,FW). When using the intermediate digestate (ID) from the C6/C8 technology to produce biogas, it is assumed that the remaining COD content can achieve the corresponding biogas yield (e.g. if 20% COD is removed during C6/C8 process, the biogas yield of ID will be 80% of the original number). The yield factors used in our studies are summarized in Table S11.
PRODUCT VALUES: The biogas market value is uncertain and depends on the electricity generation efficiency and the local electricity price. We set the biogas value to be 100

USD/tonne (0.087 USD/m³), which is calculated based on the energy value of biogas, an electricity conversion efficiency of 30%, and an average electricity price of 0.08 USD/kWh in Wisconsin. The values for C6 and C8 are set to 2,800 USD/tonne and 5,000 USD/tonne respectively (Scarborough et al., 2017).

CARBON EMISSIONS DATA: It is assumed that all untreated waste (CM, S, FW, and ID) will release CO2 and methane to the atmosphere during storage. Although C6 and C8 are not used as fuel, it is assumed that they will emit CO2 in the long-term due to degradation. The methane emission coefficient is calculated using equations S2 - S3. The emission factors in both summer and winter periods are estimated and an average value is used.
For the C6/C8 technologies, the CO2 emission factor is estimated from energy con- sumption. Data on emission factors for each product is also summarized in Table S11.

The carbon benefit obtained from biogas production varies in the range of 1.8 - 4.2 kg-CO/kg biogas (Row and Neabel, 2005; Yiridoe et al., 2009). In this work, this value is calculated by using the heating value of biogas (Murphy et al., 2004), the efficiency of electricity generation (Lantz, 2012a), and the average CO2 emissions associated with electricity generation using fossil fuels (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). The carbon benefit in our studies is set to 1.07 kg-CO2/kg-biogas.
Figure 2.4: Economic and environmental metrics (Unit: Million USD/yr)

The carbon benefit obtained from biogas production varies in the range of 1.8 - 4.2 kg-CO/kg biogas (Row and Neabel, 2005; Yiridoe et al., 2009). In this work, this value is calculated by using the heating value of biogas (Murphy et al., 2004), the efficiency of electricity generation (Lantz, 2012a), and the average CO2 emissions associated with electricity generation using fossil fuels (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). The carbon benefit in our studies is set to 1.07 kg-CO2/kg-biogas.
Figure 2.4: Economic and environmental metrics (Unit: Million USD/yr)
30: Locations for CAFOs and LFs are obtained from the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources (Wisconsin DNR). The collection point for C6 and C8 is assumed to be at the center of the state. We make this choice because C6/C8 markets are not well developed in the state. This is a conservative choice because multiple collection points can help reduce transportation costs. However, our results obtained are not sensitive to the location of the collection point, because the amount of C6/C8 is small compared to that of waste. We only consider WWTPs reported by the American Biogas Council.

WASTE SOURCE DATA: For CM, the supply values are estimated based on the average manure produced per cow per day (Lorimor et al., 2008), the total animal units of cows in each CAFO, and the average breakdown of different types of cows located at CAFOs (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017). For S, the supply values are estimated based on the sludge generated per human per day and the population in each county. Such values are based on a case study performed by the Madison Metropolitan Sewerage District (personal communication). For FW, the supply values are estimated using the average FW produced per human per day (Gustavsson et al., 2011) (excluding production and retail stages) and the population in each county.

TECHNOLOGY DATA: It is assumed that each candidate location has waste collection storage systems. The biogas recovery technologies comprise of an AD, a gas purification system, an electricity generator, and a separation system (screw press) (see Figure 2.3 (a)).

A power law expression regressed from 23 case studies reported in Beddoes et al. (2007) is used to compute AD costs, electricity generator costs, and operational costs for biogas recovery technologies of different capacities (Table S9). The power law captures effects of economies of scale. For the purification operation, it is reported that the biogas cleaning cost is in the range of 0.03 - 0.14 - USD/m³ (Beddoes et al., 2007). We use an average value of 0.08 USD/m³ and consider this an operational cost.

For C6/C8 recovery technologies, we consider laboratory-scale data reported in the literature (to our knowledge, there is no performance data available on full-scale installa- tions). Existing experimental systems include the following processing steps: acid-phase digestion system, filtration, liquid-liquid extraction, and refining (Kucek et al., 2016; Agler et al., 2012). It is assumed that the full scale system will follow the same steps (see Figure 2.3(b)). We account for uncertainties in technology performance by conducting sensitivity

Equipment costs are scaled from the values reported in Scarborough et al. (2017) by using a six-tenths power law. The costs for the reactor and related pumps are scaled directly by using the feed flow. Costs for separation units are scaled by using the product amount. We have found that the total equipment costs for C6/C8 technologies is lower than that of biogas technologies. This is because the acid-phase digester system needed is smaller than an AD system, thus requiring a shorter residence time than AD. The operational costs of C6/C8 recovery include routine maintenance costs, utility costs, base costs, and solvent costs. Maintenance costs are taken as 10% of the total equipment costs (Ulrich and Vasudevan, 2004). The utility costs are calculated based on the energy demand of the entire technology. A basic solution is used to adjust the pH in the reactor. The basic solution and solvent costs are also scaled from Scarborough et al. (2017). The total investment and operational costs of C6/C8 technologies are summarized in Table S10.

The creation of hybrid biogas/C6/C8 technologies follows naturally from the cou- pling C6/C8 and biogas technologies via the intermediate digestate flows. The supply chain framework automatically decides if C6/C8 and biogas technologies should be co- located at a given node (this does not require transportation of intermediate digestate) or should be placed in different nodes (this requires transportation). This illustrates that our supply chain framework is general and can accommodate interconnections between multiple technologies.

TRANSPORTATION DATA: The per unit transportation cost Ctrans is set to 0.16 USD/- tonne/km. This value is calculated based on the diesel consumed per tonne of sludge per kilometer by trucks (Yoshida et al., 2013), the average diesel price in USA, and the fraction of fuel cost in the total transportation cost (Trego and Murray, 2010). We use standard carbon emissions from combustion of diesel reported by the U.S. EPA (22.4 lb CO2/gallon diesel) to estimate the transportation emission constant CO2,trans, which is set

PRODUCT YIELD DATA: The yield factors for biogas from CM and FW are calculated using equation S1. Studies reported in the literature show that waste mixing can result in higher biogas yields (El-Mashad and Zhang, 2010; Zhang et al., 2013a; Dai et al., 2013; Neves et al., 2009). The yield factors reported, however, vary significantly (depending on the experimental setting). In this study we use a linear combination to calculate the yields. In addition, in order to reduce the number of products in our model, we only consider two mixture types: one composed of 90% CM and 10% FW (denoted as MX1) and one composed of 90% S and 10% FW (denoted as MX2) based on the total amount of each waste.

The yield factors for C6/C8 from organic waste are a major source of uncertainty. Scarborough et al. (2017) report that the average conversion to C6 and C8 is 16.3% COD and 1.7% COD, respectively in long-term experiments using biomass as substrate (i.e., the COD of C6 produced is 16.3% of the COD in the loaded biomass). As a first estimate, we assume that organic waste can achieve a similar COD conversion. A COD value for each waste stream is estimated based on the biogas yield factor and the COD conversion to biogas (Davis et al., 2013), which is 238 g-CH4/kg COD removed. The yields for C6 and C8 from waste mixtures are calculated by using a linear com- bination because the COD of the mixture is calculated by a linear combination (e.g., YieldC6,MX1 = 0.9Yieldc6,CM + 0.1Yieldc6,FW). When using the intermediate digestate (ID) from the C6/C8 technology to produce biogas, it is assumed that the remaining COD content can achieve the corresponding biogas yield (e.g. if 20% COD is removed during C6/C8 process, the biogas yield of ID will be 80% of the original number). The yield factors used in our studies are summarized in Table S11.
PRODUCT VALUES: The biogas market value is uncertain and depends on the electricity generation efficiency and the local electricity price. We set the biogas value to be 100

USD/tonne (0.087 USD/m³), which is calculated based on the energy value of biogas, an electricity conversion efficiency of 30%, and an average electricity price of 0.08 USD/kWh in Wisconsin. The values for C6 and C8 are set to 2,800 USD/tonne and 5,000 USD/tonne respectively (Scarborough et al., 2017).

CARBON EMISSIONS DATA: It is assumed that all untreated waste (CM, S, FW, and ID) will release CO2 and methane to the atmosphere during storage. Although C6 and C8 are not used as fuel, it is assumed that they will emit CO2 in the long-term due to degradation. The methane emission coefficient is calculated using equations S2 - S3. The emission factors in both summer and winter periods are estimated and an average value is used.
For the C6/C8 technologies, the CO2 emission factor is estimated from energy con- sumption. Data on emission factors for each product is also summarized in Table S11.

The carbon benefit obtained from biogas production varies in the range of 1.8 - 4.2 kg-CO/kg biogas (Row and Neabel, 2005; Yiridoe et al., 2009). In this work, this value is calculated by using the heating value of biogas (Murphy et al., 2004), the efficiency of electricity generation (Lantz, 2012a), and the average CO2 emissions associated with electricity generation using fossil fuels (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). The carbon benefit in our studies is set to 1.07 kg-CO2/kg-biogas.
Figure 2.4: Economic and environmental metrics (Unit: Million USD/yr)

32: The economic and environmental metrics for the case studies are summarized in Figure 2.4. For the do nothing case, we find that the social welfare is negative because waste streams need to be transported to the end sites and because CO2 will be emitted due to waste degradation (thus incurring a large TSCC). In this case, TSCC reaches 40.06 million USD per year.

We have found that, if only biogas technologies are installed, the total profit is neg- ative. This indicates that the value of biogas is not high enough to make installations economically viable. On the other hand, because biogas is recovered from waste, CO2 emissions are reduced significantly. In particular, TSCC is reduced by 87% relative to the do nothing case. Moreover, since biogas is used to generate electricity, there is a consider- able emissions benefit from the replacement of fuels in the power grid. The social welfare in this case is negative because the carbon benefits cannot offset revenue. Interestingly, if we factor in biogas leaks (5% of produced biogas) in our analysis, the total profit will de- crease and the NSCC will increase, which can make the overall social welfare to decrease by 17.68 million USD/yr. We can thus see that preventing leaks from biogas production systems is important.

If only C6/C8 technologies are installed, we have found that the total profit is positive and thus installations are economically viable. This is the result of high C6 and C8 market values and moderate technology costs, which offset low yields. On the other hand, the carbon cost is substantial in this case because low yields imply that the the intermediate digestate obtained from C6/C8 recovery still contains a large amount of organic material that will emit CO2 after disposal. Interestingly, we have found that the social welfare in this case becomes positive (92.81 million USD/yr) because the profit (119.71 million USD/yr) can offset the social cost of carbon (26.90 million USD/yr). This provides room to invest in research in C6/C8 technologies or to penalize environmental impact caused by the incomplete degradation of organic waste. For example, if we also
account for economic penalties associated with N2O emissions during waste storage and land application, the overall social welfare is still positive.

It can be concluded that the biogas recovery route is environmentally beneficial but not profitable while C6/C8 recovery is profitable but not as environmentally beneficial. When the technologies are combined, both benefits can be achieved and the social welfare becomes higher and reaches 114.28 million USD/yr (an improvement of 23%). This indi- cates that biogas and C6/C8 technologies can be used in a synergistic (cooperative) manner to maximize the social welfare and to achieve economic viability. The number of biogas and C6/C8 technologies installed are 96 and 117, respectively, and the total investment cost is 439 million USD, which translates to an return on investment (ROI = Profit/Investment) of 22.3%. We also note that, in terms of tonnage, the production of C6/C8 is only 14% of that of biogas. This highlights that revenue in C6/C8 is much higher on a per mass basis. Interestingly, in the hybrid case we also observe that the profit is reduced by 17% compared to the C6/C8 case because the system seeks to reduce carbon costs to achieve a higher social welfare. Moreover, we observe that the NSCC only represents 14% of the so- cial welfare, which implies that technology deployments are mostly driven by economics and not by environmental performance. The emissions from untreated waste are also improved over those achieved by the do nothing and biogas only cases. In particular, the TSCC is 74.8% lower relative to that of the do nothing case.

36: The optimal supply chain networks for the do nothing case, biogas only case, C6/C8 only case, and hybrid case are illustrated in Figure 2.5. We have found that, in the hybrid case, most biogas recovery occurs in technologies that are collocated with C6/C8 technologies. In particular, all biogas technologies installed use ID even if the yield factor is lower compared to that obtained with the original organic waste. We have also found that food waste is a key asset used as a flexible supplement (due to its high organic content)

and transporting CM over long distances is not optimal. In particular, FW can be used to generate C6 and C8 by itself, or mixed with CM or S to generate C6, C8 and biogas. As for the transportation of CM, the results in the biogas only case indicate a maximum viable transportation distance of 50.88 km and an average distance of 13.29 km. The average distance is consistent with the analysis reported in Poliafico and Murphy (2007) (which reports a viable distance in the range of 15 to 25 km). Interestingly, in the hybrid case, the maximum and average transportation distances for CM are 74.03 km and 16.84 km, respectively, while the total flow of CM transported is 75% higher (relative to the biogas only case). This implies that long-distance transportation of waste is justified because of the high potential economic gains. Our results thus suggest that ad-hoc transportation distances currently recommended in the literature can be restrictive.
37: The optimal supply chain networks for the do nothing case, biogas only case, C6/C8 only case, and hybrid case are illustrated in Figure 2.5. We have found that, in the hybrid case, most biogas recovery occurs in technologies that are collocated with C6/C8 technologies. In particular, all biogas technologies installed use ID even if the yield factor is lower compared to that obtained with the original organic waste. We have also found that food waste is a key asset used as a flexible supplement (due to its high organic content)

and transporting CM over long distances is not optimal. In particular, FW can be used to generate C6 and C8 by itself, or mixed with CM or S to generate C6, C8 and biogas. As for the transportation of CM, the results in the biogas only case indicate a maximum viable transportation distance of 50.88 km and an average distance of 13.29 km. The average distance is consistent with the analysis reported in Poliafico and Murphy (2007) (which reports a viable distance in the range of 15 to 25 km). Interestingly, in the hybrid case, the maximum and average transportation distances for CM are 74.03 km and 16.84 km, respectively, while the total flow of CM transported is 75% higher (relative to the biogas only case). This implies that long-distance transportation of waste is justified because of the high potential economic gains. Our results thus suggest that ad-hoc transportation distances currently recommended in the literature can be restrictive.
38: We also analyze the impact of waste mixing on economic and environmental perfor- mance (Figure 2.6). In general, waste mixing provides flexibility to the supply chain and decreases the transportation cost because waste streams can be transported to a closer site to be mixed and treated with other streams. When mixing is not allowed, the supply chain is rigid and each waste stream needs to be transported to a specific site (e.g. FW must be moved to LFs and cannot go to WWTPs). Mixing also provides investment flexi- bility, resulting in decreased capital costs. For example, the total amount of FW produced in the state is small compared to that of CM and S. If mixing is not allowed, technologies with small capacities need to be built to process FW (such technologies do not benefit from economies of scale). On the other hand, if mixing is allowed, FW can be mixed with CM and/or S SO that a larger facility can be built to exploit economies of scale. We also note that, since waste mixing has synergistic effects in yield factors, more flexible mixing ratios can be used to improve performance.

40: INFLUENCE OF BIOGAS PRICE: The biogas price is mainly influenced by the electricity price and the energy conversion efficiency. The electricity price can vary between 0.053 and 0.18 USD/kWh (reported by U.S. Energy Information Administration). The electricity generation efficiency from biogas varies in the range of 30 to 40% (Budzianowski and Budzianowska, 2015). Using these ranges, we obtain a biogas value range of 70 to 310 USD/tonne, which we covered in our sensitivity studies (Figure 2.7(a) and 2.7(b)).

It can be observed that the profit and social welfare increase as the biogas value in- creases. We also find that, within the probable range of the biogas value, the profit and the social welfare are in the range of -50 to 140 million USD/yr and -30 to 170 million USD/yr, respectively. This means that the economic viability of biogas deployments is not robust (profitability is easily influenced by external factors). When both biogas and C6/C8 technologies are deployed in a coordinated manner, the social welfare remains
(b) Total (TSCC) and saved (SSCC) social cost of
positive over the entire probable range of biogas values. This indicates that the hybrid supply chain is more flexible and can better absorb market fluctuations.

INFLUENCE OF SOCIAL COST OF CARBON: Another important source of uncertain- ty /ambiguity is the (per unit) SCC, which reflects the value that society/government puts on environmental impact (emissions). Stern (2008) pointed out that SCC can reach values of up to 300 USD/tonne C (85 USD/tonne CO2). Moore and Diaz (2015) obtained a SCC value of 220 USD/tonne CO2 in their study and Ackerman and Stanton (2012) re- ported values of 900 USD/tonne CO2 in 2010 and projected 1500 USD/tonne CO2 in 2050 in extreme cases. We note that, because SCC performs as the weight of the environmental objective, adjusting the value of SCC is useful to analyze the trade-off between economic and environmental objectives.
We conduct a sensitivity analysis on SCC within the range of 38 to 228 USD/tonne

CO2 (Figure 2.7(c) and 2.7(d)). We observe that the profit is not sensitive to the SCC val- ues. However, increasing SCC leads to a dramatic decrease of the social welfare when only C6/C8 technologies are installed, and results in an increasing social welfare for bio- gas systems. Notably, when both biogas and C6/C8 technologies are deployed, the social welfare dominates. This suggests that hybrid supply chain configurations can withstand market and SCC uncertainties, and can thus survive under various economic and envi- ronmental policy conditions.

INFLUENCE OF c6/c8 YIELD FACTORS: Our work is based on the observation that valuable chemicals can be recovered before final stabilization of organic waste in bio- gas recovery systems. Such chemicals (which are not necessarily limited to C6 and C8) should have a sufficiently high market value and yield to provide a competitive advan- tage. C6/C8 recovery technologies, however, have not been tested at an industrial level and their performance is uncertain. We use the optimization framework to provide in- sights to experimental researchers on what technology performance targets are needed to make C6/C8 technologies viable. Specifically, we conducted sensitivity analysis on the C6/C8 yield factors in order to find a feasible region for social welfare and rates of return. The yield factors for C6 and C8 are calculated based on COD conversion. In this analysis, we used 16.3% and 1.7% COD conversions for C6 and C8, respectively. A higher overall COD conversion for C6 and C8 and a higher selectivity on C8 will make the system more profitable because C8 has much higher value. Laboratory experiments described in An- dersen et al. (2017) have shown that high COD conversions (of 17.6% for C6 and 7.5% for C8) are achievable. We set this performance as an ideal upper bound.

Figure 2.8(a) shows the optimal social welfare as a function of the yield factors. It can be observed that, when the yield factors of C6 and C8 are low, no C6/C8 technology will be installed (this is equivalent to the biogas only case), and a near-zero social welfare and negative ROI are obtained. As the yield factors increase, more C6/C8 technologies are deployed and higher social welfare and ROI are obtained. The figure also highlights ROI
Figure 2.8: Impact of C6/C8 yield factors on social welfare and rates of return.
regions for different yield factor combinations.

Figure 2.8(b) shows a projection of the social welfare surface in the form of contour lines. The solid line indicates the minimum feasible combination of yield factors that drive C6/C8 installations. If the yields are below this line, then no C6/C8 technology will be installed (the supply chain will only produce biogas). The rest of the contour lines are the line of zero profit, the line of 10% ROI, and the line of 20% ROI. We highlight that a 20% ROI can be achieved by obtaining either a high C6 yield (of 18%) and a low C8 yield (of 0.5%) or by obtaining a low C6 yield (of 6%) and a high C8 yield of C8 (of 8%). Similarly, any yield combinations that are on this contour will achieve the ROI of 20%. These results indicate that there is a significant flexibility to make the supply chain viable. This analysis highlights that our framework can provide useful insights on specific technology performance targets that can achieve high social impacts.

A higher overall COD conversion for C6 and C8 and a higher selectivity on C8 will make the system more profitable because C8 has much higher value. Laboratory experiments described in An- dersen et al. (2017) have shown that high COD conversions (of 17.6% for C6 and 7.5% for C8) are achievable. We set this performance as an ideal upper bound.

Figure 2.8(a) shows the optimal social welfare as a function of the yield factors. It can be observed that, when the yield factors of C6 and C8 are low, no C6/C8 technology will be installed (this is equivalent to the biogas only case), and a near-zero social welfare and negative ROI are obtained. As the yield factors increase, more C6/C8 technologies are deployed and higher social welfare and ROI are obtained. The figure also highlights ROI

Figure 2.8(b) shows a projection of the social welfare surface in the form of contour lines. The solid line indicates the minimum feasible combination of yield factors that drive C6/C8 installations. If the yields are below this line, then no C6/C8 technology will be installed (the supply chain will only produce biogas). The rest of the contour lines are the line of zero profit, the line of 10% ROI, and the line of 20% ROI. We highlight that a 20% ROI can be achieved by obtaining either a high C6 yield (of 18%) and a low C8 yield (of 0.5%) or by obtaining a low C6 yield (of 6%) and a high C8 yield of C8 (of 8%). Similarly, any yield combinations that are on this contour will achieve the ROI of 20%. These results indicate that there is a significant flexibility to make the supply chain viable. This analysis highlights that our framework can provide useful insights on specific technology performance targets that can achieve high social impacts.
45: <header>2.3.5 Impact of Externalities </header>

INFLUENCE OF BIOGAS PRICE: The biogas price is mainly influenced by the electricity price and the energy conversion efficiency. The electricity price can vary between 0.053 and 0.18 USD/kWh (reported by U.S. Energy Information Administration). The electricity generation efficiency from biogas varies in the range of 30 to 40% (Budzianowski and Budzianowska, 2015). Using these ranges, we obtain a biogas value range of 70 to 310 USD/tonne, which we covered in our sensitivity studies (Figure 2.7(a) and 2.7(b)).

It can be observed that the profit and social welfare increase as the biogas value in- creases. We also find that, within the probable range of the biogas value, the profit and the social welfare are in the range of -50 to 140 million USD/yr and -30 to 170 million USD/yr, respectively. This means that the economic viability of biogas deployments is not robust (profitability is easily influenced by external factors). When both biogas and C6/C8 technologies are deployed in a coordinated manner, the social welfare remains

INFLUENCE OF SOCIAL COST OF CARBON: Another important source of uncertain- ty /ambiguity is the (per unit) SCC, which reflects the value that society/government puts on environmental impact (emissions). Stern (2008) pointed out that SCC can reach values of up to 300 USD/tonne C (85 USD/tonne CO2). Moore and Diaz (2015) obtained a SCC value of 220 USD/tonne CO2 in their study and Ackerman and Stanton (2012) re- ported values of 900 USD/tonne CO2 in 2010 and projected 1500 USD/tonne CO2 in 2050 in extreme cases. We note that, because SCC performs as the weight of the environmental objective, adjusting the value of SCC is useful to analyze the trade-off between economic and environmental objectives.
We conduct a sensitivity analysis on SCC within the range of 38 to 228 USD/tonne

CO2 (Figure 2.7(c) and 2.7(d)). We observe that the profit is not sensitive to the SCC val- ues. However, increasing SCC leads to a dramatic decrease of the social welfare when only C6/C8 technologies are installed, and results in an increasing social welfare for bio- gas systems. Notably, when both biogas and C6/C8 technologies are deployed, the social welfare dominates. This suggests that hybrid supply chain configurations can withstand market and SCC uncertainties, and can thus survive under various economic and envi- ronmental policy conditions.

INFLUENCE OF c6/c8 YIELD FACTORS: Our work is based on the observation that valuable chemicals can be recovered before final stabilization of organic waste in bio- gas recovery systems. Such chemicals (which are not necessarily limited to C6 and C8) should have a sufficiently high market value and yield to provide a competitive advan- tage. C6/C8 recovery technologies, however, have not been tested at an industrial level and their performance is uncertain. We use the optimization framework to provide in- sights to experimental researchers on what technology performance targets are needed to make C6/C8 technologies viable. Laboratory experiments described in An- dersen et al. (2017) have shown that high COD conversions (of 17.6% for C6 and 7.5% for C8) are achievable. We set this performance as an ideal upper bound.

It can be observed that, when the yield factors of C6 and C8 are low, no C6/C8 technology will be installed (this is equivalent to the biogas only case), and a near-zero social welfare and negative ROI are obtained. 47: <header>2.4 Conclusions </header>

The results and conclusions provided by our framework provide some interesting trends and conclusions but need to be refined by using additional experimental data and details on techno-economic performance. The analysis capabilities of our framework are also

hindered by the computational complexity of the model, which already reaches the limits of state-of-the-art optimization solvers. We are currently investigating advanced algo- rithms that will help us explore the use of stochastic and multi-period optimization for- mulations to capture uncertainties directly in the design process and to capture seasonal effects (e.g., changes in waste supply values). We will also investigate the use of multi- objective optimization techniques to identify marginal prices for carbon that achieve an optimal compromise between economic and environmental performance. Extended ca- pabilities will also help us incorporate many other technologies and products, such as nutrient (N,P,K) recovery technologies and derived products that can be used to generate additional revenue streams. Here, it will be interesting to explore how to optimally coor- dinate nutrient recovery technologies with C6/C8 and biogas technologies. Many other socio-economic benefits can also be considered as well in our proposed framework (such as odor and pathogen reduction, N2O emissions, jobs created, and impacts on property values). Another interesting direction to address computational tractability would be to use piece-wise linear approximations for capacity costs and to allow for continuous tech- nology capacities. This can reduce the number of technologies considered and capture a larger decision space but the formulations become nonlinear. Tighter energy integration in the proposed hybrid technologies can further improve costs. For instance, the heat required for C6/C8 separation can be supplied by the biogas system, and a preliminary energy balance shows that the rest of the heat from biogas is sufficient to run separation systems. Heat recovery, however, will require collocation of technologies, which can limit supply chain flexibility (a trade-off exists). We will also explore opportunities to improve environmental performance by improving transportation logistics and/or by using biogas as transportation fuel. Moreover, it is necessary to capture logistics associated with (local) waste collection, which will require higher resolution data. Our results also indicate that there is a need for coordinated markets that can better exploit waste streams.
LOGISTICS NETWORK AND HARMFUL ALGAL BLOOM CONTROL

50: <header>3.1 Introduction </header>

Anthropogenic nutrient pollution, primarily consisting of nitrogen and phosphorus, is one of the most widespread water quality problems facing the U.S., with far-ranging repercussions for environmental quality, human health, and economic success. Sources of nutrient pollution are classified as either non-point or point sources. Non-point sources include nutrient releases from agricultural land, natural land, and stormwater, while point sources include nutrient releases from permitted facilities such as wastewater treat- ment plants (WWTPs)¹. Particularly, harmful algal blooms (HABs) represent a critical and challenging nutrient pollution impact. Excess nutrient releases act as a predictor of lowering water quality (Ruane et al., 2014). A number of studies have been conducted to identify the nutrient pollution hotspots either from a holistic material flow perspective (Cordell et al., 2012) or for a particular geographical location (Kovacs et al., 2012).
Algal species generate significant levels of toxins that threaten human and aquatic

life. Rapid algae growth is associated with subsequent decreases in dissolved oxygen content of the water body, resulting in widespread die-offs of aquatic life. HABs have economic impacts in the form of remediation costs for water treatment and reduction in real estate values of impacted areas. Specifically, algae create unpleasant odors and visual aesthetics that decrease the value of recreational water systems (Heisler et al., 2008). In 2009, it was estimated that 30% of the lakes from 36 states in the U.S. have reported toxic cyanobacterial bloom issues (Graham et al., 2009). The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (U.S. EPA) estimates that nutrient pollution and associated HABs lead to annual tourism losses of one billion dollars and to commercial fishing annual losses of tens of millions of dollars 2.

Multiple indicators have been proven to measure nutrient pollution and to estimate the risk and severity of HABs. One simple indicator is the concentration of nitrogen and phosphorus in a water body, which is used by many regulatory bodies for monitoring and controlling water quality. For example, the suggested phosphorus concentration for lake water in the State of Wisconsin is 15-40 mg P/m³ (Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources, 2017). In life cycle assessment (LCA) studies, the so-called eutrophication po- tential (EP) metric is usually calculated using nutrient release and fate factors (Goedkoop et al., 2009; Bare, 2012). The EP metric is a suitable metric of the long-term impact of nu- trient release but neglects short-term seasonal impacts. Diverse indicators have also been developed to measure the severeness of HABs such as the Secchi depth, algae cell den- sity, concentration of chlorophyll-a (chl-a), and concentration of toxins (e.g., microcystins) (Paul et al., 2017). Carlson (1977) proposed a comprehensive indicator, the trophic state index (TSI), which incorporates the Secchi depth, phosphorus concentration, and chl-a concentration. The TSI is used in water quality reports to classify the severity of eutroph- ication of a water body (Division of Environmental Management, 2016). Specifically, a water body is eutrophic when the TSI is larger than 50 and is hypereutrophic if the TSI is larger than 60.

Nutrient pollution and HAB issues have been widely studied from the perspectives of human health, economic analysis, prediction and monitoring, treatment, and remediation (Backer et al., 2015; Adams et al., 2018; Johansen et al., 2018; Dai et al., 2015). Interestingly, a dimension of the nutrient pollution problem that has not received as much attention is how this interacts with agricultural logistics network management practices. This is impor- tant because the transport of nutrients to water bodies is a spatiotemporal phenomenon that involves multiple scales and that is tightly related to the spatial layout and geogra- phy of agricultural lands surrounding the water bodies, to the timing of nutrient source application, and to regional nutrient imbalances. Specifically, by designing an appropri- ate logistics network that stores, mobilizes, and processes organic waste, it is possible to balance and recycle nutrients more effectively and with this control the timing and loca- tion of nutrient runoff to water bodies. The value of logistics network management on the mitigation of environmental impacts of livestock waste has been previously analyzed from the perspective of air quality (air emissions and energy efficiency) but not from the perspective of water quality (Zandi Atashbar et al., 2018). Akram et. al. provide a framework to optimize nutrient recovery using a transportation network in Pakistan and Sweden. (Akram et al., 2019) Other recent studies have focused on the prediction of nu- trient pollution and HABs by using statistical modeling and machine learning techniques (Scown et al., 2017). Such studies consider diverse factors that affect HAB development such as geography and in-excess nutrient flows but do not offer alternatives into how to influence and control such flows.

In this work, we analyze how logistics networks can be used to enable spatiotem- poral control of in-excess nutrient flows and associated HABs. To do so, we propose a computational framework that incorporates a logistics network model that seeks to iden- tify optimal locations for waste storage and processing technologies as well as optimal strategies for mobilization of waste and derived products in a given region (Sampat et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2018). In addition, the framework incorporates a medium-fidelity nutrient transport model which captures dynamics of the transport process from agriculture lands

to water bodies. Moreover, the framework incorporates an algae bloom prediction model that tracks the nutrient concentration of a water body over time and relates this to the algae bloom level. The framework integrates these models to design a four-dimensional model for organic waste management that simultaneously mitigates investment, trans- portation, operational, and environmental costs. We present a series of case studies in the Upper Yahara watershed in the State of Wisconsin to illustrate the practicability of the framework.
