The liquid and solid fraction of manure can be used for land application separately, and the solid fraction can also be sold to the external customer.

The nutrient demand at agriculture lands is calculated using crop types reported in 2017 (Sharara et al., 2018), the land area, and the nutrient removal rate provided by USDA's crop nutrient tool (which automates and incorporates information in the Agri- cultural Waste Management Field Handbook (USA. Department of Agriculture (Wash- ington). Soil Conservation Service, 1992)). Based on the nutrient uptake curves of several crops (corn, wheat) (Jones et al., 2011), we observe that nutrient accumulation in the crops is almost linear in time. Therefore, we assume that nutrient demand is distributed evenly during the growing season. We note that, if there are rigorous fertilizing models available, we can incorporate those models into the framework by modifying the nutrient demand at different times and tracking surplus nutrient at a given time. The growing time for different crops is obtained from Agricultural Handbook provided by the National Agri- cultural Statistics Service of the United States Department of Agriculture (NASS, USDA, 1997). For legumes (such as soybean), we currently set the nitrogen demand as zero because of their capability of nitrogen fixation. In reality, however, farmers do apply ni- trogen fertilzers on these crops (although the quantity is relatively small compared with other crops such as corn). We will relax this assumption in future work. By solving this model, we provide decision-making information (e.g. transportation and storage plan- ning) and values regarding nutrient release from manure, fertilizers, and nutrient uptake by crops, which can be used in the nutrient transport model.

The logistics network models solved in our studies are large-scale linear program- ming problems. The models were formulated using the Julia-based modeling framework JuMP and were solved with Gurobi (Version 7.5.2). The model instances solved are com-
putationally challenging. The largest model contains 360,935 linear constraints and over 20,743,603 variables before presolving and requires around six hours to solve.

SETTING AND DATA FOR NUTRIENT TRANSPORT MODEL. The nutrient transport model requires also significant input data corresponding to the nutrient release from point and non-point sources, and the average precipitation within the study area, as shown in Fig- ure 3.5(b). For point-source release, we observe that there is no effluent directly to the Lake Mendota from WWTPs according to the Madison Metropolitan Sewerage District 5. Nutrient release corresponding to the non-point sources, specifically the agricultural sector (fertilizer, animal waste, and crop uptake), is extracted from the results of the logis- tics network model as explained in the previous Section (equation (3.2.25)-(3.2.27)). For the nutrient fixation data, we use global parameters reported by Bouwman et al. (2009). Specifically, the P fixation and deposition amount are o; the N fixation rate is 5 kg ex- cess N/(ha-year) for nonleguminous crops and 25 kg excess N/ (ha-year) for leguminous crops. The N deposition amount is 1.5 g N/(m2. year) (Dentener et al., 2006). For the nutrient release from the natural area, we simply assume that it is 1/6 of the nutrient release from the agricultural area (Montgomery, 2014). Using this nutrient release data along with the data on precipitation, time of concentration (which is 3.3 days estimated using equations in National Engineering Handbook (NRCS, USDA, 2004)), and the curve number (which is set as an average value of 70 (Dane County, 2002)), the model calculates the runoff value. The nutrient transport delay in the water body (e.g., from the river to lake) is ignored because this occurs at a faster timescale than the one considered here. By running this model, we can obtain the nutrient flows into the water body and runoff volume, which are used to update the nutrient environment of the nutrient environment in the algae growth model.
SETTING AND DATA FOR ALGAE GROWTH MODEL. The algae growth model as de- scribed in Figure 3.5(c) requires input data to capture algal cell information (surface area,

volume, maximum dissension, and movement under light conditions), amount of nu- trients entering the system (from nutrient transport model using equation (3.2.46) and (3.2.47)), lake volume, average depth, and weather data. In this study, we consider eight algae species that represent the typical species found in lakes with certain properties such as edibility, nitrogen fixation, and fast movement. The algae cell information is obtained from (Elliott et al., 2002). The lake information is obtained from the Wisconsin DNR. The temperature profile and mixed layer depth throughout the year are generated using the FLAKE model (Mironov et al., 2010). The weather conditions, including sunlight intensity and duration of days, are calculated using the SWAT model (Neitsch et al., 2011).

TIMESCALES. In this modeling framework, there are several physical processes with different time scales. However, assuming uniform time distribution of events as described in the previous sections allows us to combine, run, and solve the models within the same time scale. The growth of crops usually takes months. We capture the aggregate nutrient requirements during this growth phase on a bi-weekly time scale. In the logistics model, we make decisions every two weeks about the transportation routes, the storage planning, and technology processing. The nutrient balance is calculated every two weeks, and by assuming the nutrient runoff is proportional to daily runoff, we test its impact of nutrient pollution on HAB development on a daily basis.

NOTES ON UNCERTAINTIES. The uncertainty in this framework can be mainly attributed to the data inputs for the modeling components. In the logistics design model, the crop yields and nutrient demand is the main source of uncertainty. Although we use the standard values reported by USDA as the nutrient requirements for all the crops, the real value can be influenced by multiple external seasonal factors such as soil conditions, climate and precipitation, cultivation activities, etc. These factors can be incorporated into the model when there is rigorous fertilizing/crop growing model available. Addi- tionally, these uncertainties can be considered independent to decisions and therefore, one can reformulate the model as exogenous stochastic optimization problem. However,

this will remarkably increase the size of the problem and the computational intensity in solving procedure. For the nutrient transport model, some global parameters applied can cause errors in the prediction specific to the Upper Yahara Watershed. This error can be reduced by considering more rigorous soil-water assimilation (this is part of our future work). Also, the use of historical datasets to predict future system performance introduces more uncertainty in the results.

age systems and fix the associated binary variables (Sharara et al., 2018). Yield factors, the separation efficiency of nutrients, investment and operational costs are obtained from previous studies (Sampat et al., 2018a). We also assume that only those farms with more than 500 animal units are equipped with separation technologies. The liquid and solid fraction of manure can be used for land application separately, and the solid fraction can also be sold to the external customer.

The nutrient demand at agriculture lands is calculated using crop types reported in 2017 (Sharara et al., 2018), the land area, and the nutrient removal rate provided by USDA's crop nutrient tool (which automates and incorporates information in the Agri- cultural Waste Management Field Handbook (USA. Department of Agriculture (Wash- ington). Soil Conservation Service, 1992)). Based on the nutrient uptake curves of several crops (corn, wheat) (Jones et al., 2011), we observe that nutrient accumulation in the crops is almost linear in time. Therefore, we assume that nutrient demand is distributed evenly during the growing season. We note that, if there are rigorous fertilizing models available, we can incorporate those models into the framework by modifying the nutrient demand at different times and tracking surplus nutrient at a given time. The growing time for different crops is obtained from Agricultural Handbook provided by the National Agri- cultural Statistics Service of the United States Department of Agriculture (NASS, USDA, 1997). For legumes (such as soybean), we currently set the nitrogen demand as zero because of their capability of nitrogen fixation. In reality, however, farmers do apply ni- trogen fertilzers on these crops (although the quantity is relatively small compared with other crops such as corn). We will relax this assumption in future work. By solving this model, we provide decision-making information (e.g. transportation and storage plan- ning) and values regarding nutrient release from manure, fertilizers, and nutrient uptake by crops, which can be used in the nutrient transport model.

The logistics network models solved in our studies are large-scale linear program- ming problems. The models were formulated using the Julia-based modeling framework JuMP and were solved with Gurobi (Version 7.5.2). The model instances solved are com-
putationally challenging. The largest model contains 360,935 linear constraints and over 20,743,603 variables before presolving and requires around six hours to solve.

SETTING AND DATA FOR NUTRIENT TRANSPORT MODEL. The nutrient transport model requires also significant input data corresponding to the nutrient release from point and non-point sources, and the average precipitation within the study area, as shown in Fig- ure 3.5(b). For point-source release, we observe that there is no effluent directly to the Lake Mendota from WWTPs according to the Madison Metropolitan Sewerage District 5. Nutrient release corresponding to the non-point sources, specifically the agricultural sector (fertilizer, animal waste, and crop uptake), is extracted from the results of the logis- tics network model as explained in the previous Section (equation (3.2.25)-(3.2.27)). For the nutrient fixation data, we use global parameters reported by Bouwman et al. (2009). Specifically, the P fixation and deposition amount are o; the N fixation rate is 5 kg ex- cess N/(ha-year) for nonleguminous crops and 25 kg excess N/ (ha-year) for leguminous crops. The N deposition amount is 1.5 g N/(m2. year) (Dentener et al., 2006). For the nutrient release from the natural area, we simply assume that it is 1/6 of the nutrient release from the agricultural area (Montgomery, 2014). Using this nutrient release data along with the data on precipitation, time of concentration (which is 3.3 days estimated using equations in National Engineering Handbook (NRCS, USDA, 2004)), and the curve number (which is set as an average value of 70 (Dane County, 2002)), the model calculates the runoff value. The nutrient transport delay in the water body (e.g., from the river to lake) is ignored because this occurs at a faster timescale than the one considered here. By running this model, we can obtain the nutrient flows into the water body and runoff volume, which are used to update the nutrient environment of the nutrient environment in the algae growth model.
SETTING AND DATA FOR ALGAE GROWTH MODEL. The algae growth model as de- scribed in Figure 3.5(c) requires input data to capture algal cell information (surface area,

volume, maximum dissension, and movement under light conditions), amount of nu- trients entering the system (from nutrient transport model using equation (3.2.46) and (3.2.47)), lake volume, average depth, and weather data. In this study, we consider eight algae species that represent the typical species found in lakes with certain properties such as edibility, nitrogen fixation, and fast movement. The algae cell information is obtained from (Elliott et al., 2002). The lake information is obtained from the Wisconsin DNR. The temperature profile and mixed layer depth throughout the year are generated using the FLAKE model (Mironov et al., 2010). The weather conditions, including sunlight intensity and duration of days, are calculated using the SWAT model (Neitsch et al., 2011).

TIMESCALES. In this modeling framework, there are several physical processes with different time scales. However, assuming uniform time distribution of events as described in the previous sections allows us to combine, run, and solve the models within the same time scale. The growth of crops usually takes months. We capture the aggregate nutrient requirements during this growth phase on a bi-weekly time scale. In the logistics model, we make decisions every two weeks about the transportation routes, the storage planning, and technology processing. The nutrient balance is calculated every two weeks, and by assuming the nutrient runoff is proportional to daily runoff, we test its impact of nutrient pollution on HAB development on a daily basis.

NOTES ON UNCERTAINTIES. The uncertainty in this framework can be mainly attributed to the data inputs for the modeling components. In the logistics design model, the crop yields and nutrient demand is the main source of uncertainty. Although we use the standard values reported by USDA as the nutrient requirements for all the crops, the real value can be influenced by multiple external seasonal factors such as soil conditions, climate and precipitation, cultivation activities, etc. These factors can be incorporated into the model when there is rigorous fertilizing/crop growing model available. Addi- tionally, these uncertainties can be considered independent to decisions and therefore, one can reformulate the model as exogenous stochastic optimization problem. However,

this will remarkably increase the size of the problem and the computational intensity in solving procedure. For the nutrient transport model, some global parameters applied can cause errors in the prediction specific to the Upper Yahara Watershed. This error can be reduced by considering more rigorous soil-water assimilation (this is part of our future work). Also, the use of historical datasets to predict future system performance introduces more uncertainty in the results.
96: We study the time period spanning April to the end of October since HABs are more likely to occur during this period. We begin the analysis by specifying manure storage levels. This helps capture the effects of winter manure application strategy because, if the initial storage level is low, then this indicates that farmers spread manure during the winter. We also specify the initial phosphorus and nitrogen concentrations in the lake to capture its starting nutrient status. In addition to the initial conditions, technology availability needs to be specified to define the scope of manure treatment. We also specify how strict the NMP is in the logistics network by adjusting the penalty coefficients AP and AN The logic of this decision-making procedure is summarized in Figure 3.6. If the algae prediction indicates high risk, we change the setting of the logistics network to adjust the management actions (e.g., increase the use of technologies and increase the environmental cost). We also use the framework to study the impacts of the changes in the logistics network on final HAB results.

101: In this section, we analyze the results obtained for the WI case study for different sce- narios. We also propose some suggestions from perspectives of logistics network waste management strategies for spatiotemporal HAB control and prevention.

The HAB prediction results for all the scenarios obtained from the PROTECH model are presented in Figure 3.7. Here, the green, yellow, and red dashed lines represent the low, moderate, and high probability HAB onset levels associated with different adverse health effects. The health effects are estimated by World Health Organization (2003). The blue solid line is the average chl-a concentration near the lake surface (<0.5 m), where most recreational activities occur.

We observe that, in all scenarios explored, HABs are more likely to occur in summer months (July, August, and early September) due to the higher temperature and favorable sunlight conditions. We also note that the occurrence and severity of HABs depend on the initial concentration of algae in the lake. Calculations confirm that environmental conditions (temperature and sunlight) and phosphorus concentration are the key limiting

Figure 3.7: Average chlorophyll-a concentration near the lake surface (<0.5m) for scenarios considered. The green, yellow, and red dashed lines represent the low, moderate, and high probability levels of acute health effects due to HABs. Blue solid line is the temporal profile achieved by implementing the designed organic waste management logistics network.
Figure 3.8: Chlorophyll-a concentration in the lake at different depths and times.

factors of algal growth. This observation is consistent with the assumption commonly made in many life-cycle analysis methods (Morelli et al., 2018). Specifically, it is often assumed that phosphorus is the limiting factor for algal growth in inland surface water while nitrogen is the limiting factor in marine water. Our results show that reducing the chl-a concentration below high-risk levels (Scenario VII) requires a combination of initiatives. Specifically, this can only be achieved by having high levels of winter store, reducing the base nutrient concentration level of the lake, enforcing strict NMP policies, and implementing separation technologies to mobilize waste outside the region. Main- taining chl-a levels at the high-risk levels (Scenarios I, III, VI) requires enforcing strict NMP policies (regardless of the other factors). We thus see that potential NMP policies play a key role in HABs control.

The concentration of chl-a at different depth and time for each scenario is shown in Figure 3.8. Here, red indicates a high chl-a concentration and blue indicates low chl-a concentration. We can see that algae cells are mainly aggregated at the surface of the lake during the summer time (due to favorable light and temperature conditions). For Scenario II, the surface concentration of chl-a reaches over 300 mg/m3 (corresponding to a cell density of 600,000 cells/ml). Together with the impact of sunlight, slow-moving water, and other natural forces, there is a risk of forming algal scum on the lake surface. For the best scenario (Scenario VII), the surface concentration of chl-a can be reduced by a factor of three (to 100 mg/m3.

From the above analysis, we find that incorporating appropriate logistics network management strategies in storage planning, nutrient application planning, and with the use of nutrient recovery technology, we can reduce the HAB level from a high-risk level to a moderate-risk level within one year. To further reduce the risk of HABs, mid-term efforts (e.g., perennial nutrient management) are necessary. Specifically, when the initial conditions are not favorable for the lake health (representing a poor manure application planning in winter), the predicted HAB level is always higher. In Scenario II, the HAB level can reach the warning level of a high probability of causing acute health impacts to

humans. Even with strict NMPs and the use of technologies, the chl-a concentration is still around the warning line (Scenario V). The model does confirm that it is not recommended to apply manure during the winter since the nutrient demand from crops is low and the nutrients accumulated in the soil will travel to the surface water when the spring runoff occurs. Thus, the winter application activity can impact the HAB situation in the following summer and investing storage systems to manage winter manure is critical to displace the timing of nutrient flows and HABs.

Under the current fertilizer market prices and their nutrient content (Schnitkey et al., 2015), farmers prefer to buy fertilizers from retailers instead of storing, transporting, and applying existing animal manure. Therefore, there should be an external driving force to make the system avoid this economically feasible but environmentally unsound solution. One strategy is to achieve this driving force through NMPs. In particular, our results reveal that a strict NMP can reduce the HAB level regardless of technology usage and initial condition (Figure 3.7). At present, only large CAFOs (concentrated animal feeding operations) and their associated agriculture lands are required to follow NMPs. Small farms are not mandated to follow NMPs and are more likely to use external fertilizers and spread animal waste during winter months.

By using manure separation technologies, we can reduce the HAB level by reducing the amount of excess nutrient in the study region (when there is external demand for products). We also observe that the use of technologies can have a larger impact on HAB reduction when combined with strict NMP policies and proper storage planning (Scenario VII).

The concentration of chl-a at different depth and time for each scenario is shown in Figure 3.8. Here, red indicates a high chl-a concentration and blue indicates low chl-a concentration. We can see that algae cells are mainly aggregated at the surface of the lake during the summer time (due to favorable light and temperature conditions). For Scenario II, the surface concentration of chl-a reaches over 300 mg/m3 (corresponding to a cell density of 600,000 cells/ml). Together with the impact of sunlight, slow-moving water, and other natural forces, there is a risk of forming algal scum on the lake surface. For the best scenario (Scenario VII), the surface concentration of chl-a can be reduced by a factor of three (to 100 mg/m3.

From the above analysis, we find that incorporating appropriate logistics network management strategies in storage planning, nutrient application planning, and with the use of nutrient recovery technology, we can reduce the HAB level from a high-risk level to a moderate-risk level within one year. To further reduce the risk of HABs, mid-term efforts (e.g., perennial nutrient management) are necessary. Specifically, when the initial conditions are not favorable for the lake health (representing a poor manure application planning in winter), the predicted HAB level is always higher. In Scenario II, the HAB level can reach the warning level of a high probability of causing acute health impacts to

humans. Even with strict NMPs and the use of technologies, the chl-a concentration is still around the warning line (Scenario V). The model does confirm that it is not recommended to apply manure during the winter since the nutrient demand from crops is low and the nutrients accumulated in the soil will travel to the surface water when the spring runoff occurs. Thus, the winter application activity can impact the HAB situation in the following summer and investing storage systems to manage winter manure is critical to displace the timing of nutrient flows and HABs.

Under the current fertilizer market prices and their nutrient content (Schnitkey et al., 2015), farmers prefer to buy fertilizers from retailers instead of storing, transporting, and applying existing animal manure. Therefore, there should be an external driving force to make the system avoid this economically feasible but environmentally unsound solution. One strategy is to achieve this driving force through NMPs. In particular, our results reveal that a strict NMP can reduce the HAB level regardless of technology usage and initial condition (Figure 3.7). At present, only large CAFOs (concentrated animal feeding operations) and their associated agriculture lands are required to follow NMPs. Small farms are not mandated to follow NMPs and are more likely to use external fertilizers and spread animal waste during winter months.

By using manure separation technologies, we can reduce the HAB level by reducing the amount of excess nutrient in the study region (when there is external demand for products). We also observe that the use of technologies can have a larger impact on HAB reduction when combined with strict NMP policies and proper storage planning (Scenario VII).

109: <header>3.4.1 Logistics Network Design and Nutrient Pollution </header>

In addition to the results obtained with the PROTECH model, we can gain insights from the results of the logistics network model. In Figure 3.9, we present the mobilization (transportation) routes for manure for Scenario V and Scenario VI at different times. The

overall inventory levels for manure are presented in Figure 3.10. We note that in the Figure 3.10, two clusters for starting and ending trajectory points is because we have assumed (for the case study) two winter storage percentage values as shown in Table 3.1. For all scenarios, the inventory levels of manure have a similar trend. At the beginning of April, the inventory levels increase because most crops have not been planted and the system needs to store the waste. After the short increase in inventory level, there is a drop because the land nodes need nutrients to support crop growth, and also because the logistics network needs to clear storage space for the coming inventory in the subsequent winter. This trend is also evident from the transportation maps (Figure 3.9). For both scenarios, the majority of farms transfer waste to storage systems in early April and then haul the waste agricultural lands in the following months. We also observe that the NMP influences the nutrient output of the logistics network system by adjusting spatial mobilization (transportation) instead of temporal mobilization (inventory levels). For the transportation in Scenario V where the NMP is strict, the transportation routes are longer with an average transportation distance of 2.28 km in August for livestock waste (more mobilization is needed). This is because the logistics network is monitoring the locations where nutrients are in demand and the farmers send the animal waste to the exact agricultural land to achieve high efficiency in nutrient reuse. On the other hand, in Scenario VI (where the NMP is not enforced strictly), the farmers only send the waste to nearest lands (with an average transportation distance of 1.62 km in August for livestock waste) to achieve a lower transportation cost. The transportation routes for other scenarios follow similar trends. By comparing Figure 3.9 and Figure 3.10 we observe that, although all scenarios seem to have used up the livestock waste, the spatial distribution is different. In some scenarios (e.g. Scenario V), the manure is used at lands where nutrient is needed; while for other scenarios (e.g. Scenario VI), the manure distribution is uneven resulting nutrient surplus at specific hotspots while other lands face nutrient deficiency.
In Figure 3.11 we present the nutrient loading maps for Scenario II, VI, VII at different time periods. For Scenario II and VI, since the NMP enforcement is loose and fertilizers

are demanded by farmers, the phosphorus is highly imbalanced in the area. Scenario II presents a more severe situation since no nutrient recovery technology is installed. 114: <header>3.4.2 Trade-off Analysis Between Economic and Environmental Objectives </header>

Finally, we perform a trade-off analysis between the economic costs (transportation, tech- nology, and fertilizer costs) and the HAB levels by varying the environmental costs (en- forcing NMP more strictly) in the logistics network under two settings, presented in Figure 3.12. This problem is a trade-off problem in the sense that the unit environmental cost acts as a weight (e.g., price guarantee, feed-in tariff) of the environmental objective and by varying this parameter, we are varying the relative importance of the economic and environmental objectives. The modeling framework seeks to find pareto solutions that coordinate the two objectives on the pareto front. The red plane represents the high probability levels of acute health effects due to HABs. Figure 3.12(a) shows the trade-off with 70% initial storage, high initial nutrient concentration (120 mg P/m³), and the use of separation technology. The environmental costs of excess phosphorus are o, 6.6, 13.2, and 19.8 USD/kg excess P, respectively (Scenario VI has the same initial condition with an environmental cost of o USD/kg excess P). The ratio of the environmental costs of ex-

Figure 3.11: Phosphorus loadings in Scenarios II, VI, VII and at different times: (a)Scenario II, April, (b) Scenario II, August, (c) Scenario II, October, (d) Scenario VI, April, (e) Scenario VI, August, (f) Scenario VI, October, (g) Scenario VII, April, (h) Sce- nario VII, August, and (i) Scenario VII, October.

cess phosphorus and nitrogen remain unchanged. From the graph, we find that the cost necessary to keep the curve near the red plane is about 1.25 million USD. To be specific, this cost only includes transportation cost and pure operational cost of technology; no additional investment is included. If we further increase the environmental costs, we can- not decrease the HAB level further. In this case, the cost associated with HAB prevention reaches 0.36 million USD given the worst-case cost is 0.89 million USD.

Figure 3.12(b) shows the trade-off with 90% initial storage, low initial nutrient con- centration (45 mg P/m³, and the use of separation technology. The environmental costs of excess phosphorus are o, 6.6, 13.2, 33, 46.2, and 66 USD/kg excess P, respectively (Sce- nario VII and Scenario VIII have the same initial condition with an environmental cost of 33 and o USD/kg excess P respectively). The cost to keep the curve under the red plane is still around 1.40 million USD. We note that our estimate of prevention cost is conservative because it does not factor in storage cost, cost related to policy implementation, and cost of technology development and investment. Additionally, if we compare the two cases we studied, we find that when the starting condition is relatively better (high winter storage and low nutrient concentration), we have the option to spend more money in the logistics and inventory planning to avoid nutrient runoff and reduce the HAB levels to a relatively large extent compared to present conditions. On the other hand, if the starting condition is not favorable (initial storage is low and the nutrient has already entered the lake), a small reduction in the HAB levels can be achieved. Since the current situation in the study area is closer to the worse case, mid and long-term prevention efforts and remedi- ation actions (e.g., direct lake cleaning, HAB scum removal) will be needed to control the nutrient runoff from agricultural lands and to accelerate the recovery of the lake.

This problem is a trade-off problem in the sense that the unit environmental cost acts as a weight (e.g., price guarantee, feed-in tariff) of the environmental objective and by varying this parameter, we are varying the relative importance of the economic and environmental objectives. The modeling framework seeks to find pareto solutions that coordinate the two objectives on the pareto front. The red plane represents the high probability levels of acute health effects due to HABs. Figure 3.12(a) shows the trade-off with 70% initial storage, high initial nutrient concentration (120 mg P/m³), and the use of separation technology. The environmental costs of excess phosphorus are o, 6.6, 13.2, and 19.8 USD/kg excess P, respectively (Scenario VI has the same initial condition with an environmental cost of o USD/kg excess P). The ratio of the environmental costs of ex-

Figure 3.11: Phosphorus loadings in Scenarios II, VI, VII and at different times: (a)Scenario II, April, (b) Scenario II, August, (c) Scenario II, October, (d) Scenario VI, April, (e) Scenario VI, August, (f) Scenario VI, October, (g) Scenario VII, April, (h) Sce- nario VII, August, and (i) Scenario VII, October.

cess phosphorus and nitrogen remain unchanged. From the graph, we find that the cost necessary to keep the curve near the red plane is about 1.25 million USD. To be specific, this cost only includes transportation cost and pure operational cost of technology; no additional investment is included. If we further increase the environmental costs, we can- not decrease the HAB level further. In this case, the cost associated with HAB prevention reaches 0.36 million USD given the worst-case cost is 0.89 million USD.

Figure 3.12(b) shows the trade-off with 90% initial storage, low initial nutrient con- centration (45 mg P/m³, and the use of separation technology. The environmental costs of excess phosphorus are o, 6.6, 13.2, 33, 46.2, and 66 USD/kg excess P, respectively (Sce- nario VII and Scenario VIII have the same initial condition with an environmental cost of 33 and o USD/kg excess P respectively). The cost to keep the curve under the red plane is still around 1.40 million USD. We note that our estimate of prevention cost is conservative because it does not factor in storage cost, cost related to policy implementation, and cost of technology development and investment. Additionally, if we compare the two cases we studied, we find that when the starting condition is relatively better (high winter storage and low nutrient concentration), we have the option to spend more money in the logistics and inventory planning to avoid nutrient runoff and reduce the HAB levels to a relatively large extent compared to present conditions. On the other hand, if the starting condition is not favorable (initial storage is low and the nutrient has already entered the lake), a small reduction in the HAB levels can be achieved. Since the current situation in the study area is closer to the worse case, mid and long-term prevention efforts and remedi- ation actions (e.g., direct lake cleaning, HAB scum removal) will be needed to control the nutrient runoff from agricultural lands and to accelerate the recovery of the lake.
119: In this work, we presented a computational framework to manage organic waste from agriculture activities for reducing and preventing nutrient pollution and HABs. We em-
(a) Trade-off analysis with 70% initial storage, high initial nutrient concentration, and use of separation technologies
(b) Trade-off analysis with 90% initial storage, low initial nutrient concentration, and use of separation technologes

Figure 3.12: Trade-off analysis between economic costs and nutrient pollution levels: (a) Trade-off analysis with 70% initial storage, high initial nutrient concentration, and use of separation technologies, and (b) Trade-off analysis with 90% initial storage, low initial nutrient concentration, and use of separation technologes.

ployed an existing nutrient fate and transport model NEWS2, and an algae growth model PROTECH, to study the impact of the decisions made in the logistics network manage- ment on the environment. This modeling framework can be used to predict if a HAB will occur in the worst case and if offhand preventive actions are enough to prohibit the occurrence by controlling the related environmental conditions. We also provided a case study for the Upper Yahara Watershed in the state of Wisconsin to show the practicabil- ity of the modeling framework. We have found that the logistics network management for waste and nutrients can reduce the incidence rates of HABs effectively but reducing to a non-harmful level may require long-term efforts such as installing advanced ma- nure treatment technologies and storage systems. In addition, we studied the impact of different nutrient pollution control strategies including coordinated manure storage and application, nutrient management incentive plans, and nutrient recovery technologies. We found that all three strategies can reduce nutrient release by a certain amount, and by extension reduce the level of HABs. Among them, using strict nutrient management plans can achieve the best immediate effects, and coordinated manure application planning in winter has a significant influence on the occurrence of HABs in the following summer. The three strategies have synergistic effects in the sense that, when applied together and considering a unit environmental cost incentive for nutrient recovery (price guarantee), they can reduce the HAB levels by the greatest extent. In addition, this contribution aims to offer more realistic nutrient pollution prevention and control costs and employ this framework for the design and evaluation of feasible and efficient nutrient management incentives and policies.
CONFLICT RESOLUTION IN LIVESTOCK MANURE MANAGEMENT
Article to be submitted to ACS Sustainable Chemistry and Engineering (Hu et al., 2021a).

122: The dairy sector in the United States (U.S.) is a multi-billion enterprise that fosters di- verse economic and social activities. The total dairy exports of the State of Wisconsin (WI) reached nearly 800 million US dollars in 2018. While there are various types of valu- able products generated from the dairy industry, such as milk and cheese, a large amount of byproduct, cow manure, is also generated. It has been estimated that an average lac- tating dairy cow can produces 9,500 liters of milk and 19,000 liters of manure in a year (López-Díaz et al., 2019). The land application of manure can improve soil health and pro- vide valuable nutrients for crop production. However, manure can also lead to negative environmental impacts if not managed properly. Manure is the second largest source of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions after enteric fermentation in cattle operations (Veltman et al., 2018). Ammonia (NH3) emissions from manure can reach 70% of excreted nitrogen (N) and deposit into water ecosystems or transform into nitrous oxide (N20) emissions

further contributing to climate change (Hristov et al., 2009). Manure management from livestock operations contribute 71% of total NH3 emissions in the U.S., surpassing by far the 14% contribution of mineral fertilizers, with dairy alone representing 23% of these livestock emissions (Roe et al., 2004). NH3 emissions from dairy manure in WI are par- ticularly important given the nearly 1.3 million cows in the state. There is also evidence showing that NH3 emissions have negative impacts on biodiversity, and are crucial in the formation of particulate matter in the atmosphere (Warner et al., 2017). Besides affecting air and water quality, these N losses reduce nutrients for crop production, increasing the fertilizer costs for farmers. Manure processing technologies, such as anaerobic digestion (AD) and solid-liquid separation (SLS), can increase operational flexibility and reduce environmental impacts from manure. However, adoption of these manure processing systems is still limited by the high capital, operational, and maintenance costs that may not be feasible for small or even some large operations. Agricultural AD systems can cost millions of dollars (Galinato et al., 2015) and SLS systems require additional equipment to handle both manure solids and liquids. Strategies that reduce costs and increase potential revenues are needed to promote the adoption of these systems by cattle operations.

AD produces biogas that has traditionally been used to produce electricity in the U.S. (U.S. Environmental Protection Agency) but more recently there is an increasing shift to renewable compressed natural gas (rCNG). The digestion system can reduce GHG emissions related to manure management by more than 50%, mostly in the form of CH4 generated during storage (Amon et al., 2006). Renewable energy from biogas can further offset the embedded emissions from grid electricity Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2015). This is especially true for fossil based electric grids. AD also increases nutrient mineralization, making N more available to crops. However, mineralized N is more susceptible to be lost through volatilization as NH3 during manure storage and land application if mitiga- tion steps are not taken (Rotz et al., 2018). SLS separates manure into liquid and solid fractions. The liquid fraction has reduced total solids (TS), is more easily transported via pumps for onsite use, and reduces NH3 emissions when land applied due to a rapid

infiltration (Guilayn et al., 2019). The solid fraction is suitable for long-distance trans- portation and can help mitigate nutrient imbalance over spatial areas. SLS effectively removes nutrients, particularly phosphorus (P), along with the manure solids that can be used as fertilizer or bedding (Hjorth et al., 2011). This is particularly important in places with P excess, which is the case of concentrated animal agriculture operations. Given the reduction of moisture in the solid fraction when compared to the incoming manure, overall farm hauling costs can be reduced by SLS if managed effectively. More recently, the implementation of ultrafiltration and reverse osmosis systems has been explored to further treat the liquid fraction streams to a clean water quality level. The objective of these systems is to obtain the highest separation efficiency possible of solids, nutrients, and minerals. This is achieved by running multiple separation systems in series, namely, mechanical SLS, ultrafiltration, and reverse osmosis. Sand separation is another common processing technology that affects manure characteristics (mainly moisture content) and thus emissions during manure storage and land application. It is important to under- stand how these changes in manure characteristics affect the environmental impacts of manure from excretion to land application.

The environmental impacts of manure systems can be quantified using life cycle as- sessment (LCA) methods. Manure management has usually been a subsection of more broad studies that have focused primarily on dairy products (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2015; Rotz et al., 2010; Thomassen et al., 2008). Other studies evaluated biogas production from manure and a mix of agricultural feedstocks to assess GHG emissions and fossil fuel consumption. De Vries et al. (2012) evaluated the environmental impacts of manure sep- aration by reverse osmosis. The authors found higher overall impacts when compared to management of raw manure, but reduced GHG emissions and depletion of fossil fuels with the addition of an AD system. Poeschl et al. (2012a) compared different AD systems processing single and mixed feedstocks (Poeschl et al., 2012b). The authors concluded that straw and corn silage produced the most biogas when evaluating single-feedstock technologies and combining municipal solid waste with agricultural and food industry

residues produced the most biogas within co-digestion technologies. Ebner et al. (2015) conducted a life cycle analysis of AD co-digesting dairy manure and industrial food waste. The study concluded that GHG emissions can be reduced by 70% when compared to conventional treatment of manure and food waste. Most LCA studies did not include an evaluation of environmental and economic trade-offs of these different management and processing steps. There is a need to identify a path that maximizes both their en- vironmental and economic impacts. This study will adopt an LCA approach to account for GHG emissions, NH3 emissions and depletion of fossil fuels from different manure management and processing technologies.

The environmental metrics quantified by LCA can be used in an optimization model to maximize environmental and economic benefits and inform decision-makers. However, environmental metrics often have conflicts due to the limitation of current technologies and the nature of the manure management problem. In addition, environmental met- rics conflict with economic metrics in general because of the relatively high costs and immature market of derived products. These metrics need to be balanced to achieve an overall satisfaction of related stakeholders. In this context, decision-making methodolo- gies are needed to uncover the overall conflicts and synergies of both environmental and economic metrics for a large system. These methodologies need to leverage the use of LCA to capture diverse impacts and the use of supply chain optimization techniques to determine suitable transportation routes for manure and derived products, as well as suitable processing technologies to mitigate environmental impacts (Sampat et al., 2017; You and Wang, 2011). Previous LCA optimization work have targeted up to three metrics and thus can either describe the Pareto front and trade-offs in an efficient way, or in- volve a decision-maker to quantify the relative importance of those metrics (Zhang et al., 2013b; Gong and You, 2014; Hu et al., 2018). These methods can become challenging as the number of metrics increase due to the curse of dimensionality. This study analyzes seven metrics and further integrates a utopia-tracking method (Kim and De Weck, 2006) to provide decisions that can resolve conflicts in metrics without the construction of the

There is also evidence showing that NH3 emissions have negative impacts on biodiversity, and are crucial in the formation of particulate matter in the atmosphere (Warner et al., 2017). Besides affecting air and water quality, these N losses reduce nutrients for crop production, increasing the fertilizer costs for farmers. Manure processing technologies, such as anaerobic digestion (AD) and solid-liquid separation (SLS), can increase operational flexibility and reduce environmental impacts from manure. However, adoption of these manure processing systems is still limited by the high capital, operational, and maintenance costs that may not be feasible for small or even some large operations. Agricultural AD systems can cost millions of dollars (Galinato et al., 2015) and SLS systems require additional equipment to handle both manure solids and liquids. Strategies that reduce costs and increase potential revenues are needed to promote the adoption of these systems by cattle operations.

AD produces biogas that has traditionally been used to produce electricity in the U.S. (U.S. Environmental Protection Agency) but more recently there is an increasing shift to renewable compressed natural gas (rCNG). The digestion system can reduce GHG emissions related to manure management by more than 50%, mostly in the form of CH4 generated during storage (Amon et al., 2006). Renewable energy from biogas can further offset the embedded emissions from grid electricity Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2015). This is especially true for fossil based electric grids. AD also increases nutrient mineralization, making N more available to crops. However, mineralized N is more susceptible to be lost through volatilization as NH3 during manure storage and land application if mitiga- tion steps are not taken (Rotz et al., 2018). SLS separates manure into liquid and solid fractions. The liquid fraction has reduced total solids (TS), is more easily transported via pumps for onsite use, and reduces NH3 emissions when land applied due to a rapid

infiltration (Guilayn et al., 2019). The solid fraction is suitable for long-distance trans- portation and can help mitigate nutrient imbalance over spatial areas. SLS effectively removes nutrients, particularly phosphorus (P), along with the manure solids that can be used as fertilizer or bedding (Hjorth et al., 2011). This is particularly important in places with P excess, which is the case of concentrated animal agriculture operations. Given the reduction of moisture in the solid fraction when compared to the incoming manure, overall farm hauling costs can be reduced by SLS if managed effectively. More recently, the implementation of ultrafiltration and reverse osmosis systems has been explored to further treat the liquid fraction streams to a clean water quality level. The objective of these systems is to obtain the highest separation efficiency possible of solids, nutrients, and minerals. This is achieved by running multiple separation systems in series, namely, mechanical SLS, ultrafiltration, and reverse osmosis. Sand separation is another common processing technology that affects manure characteristics (mainly moisture content) and thus emissions during manure storage and land application. It is important to under- stand how these changes in manure characteristics affect the environmental impacts of manure from excretion to land application.

The environmental impacts of manure systems can be quantified using life cycle as- sessment (LCA) methods. Manure management has usually been a subsection of more broad studies that have focused primarily on dairy products (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2015; Rotz et al., 2010; Thomassen et al., 2008). Other studies evaluated biogas production from manure and a mix of agricultural feedstocks to assess GHG emissions and fossil fuel consumption. De Vries et al. (2012) evaluated the environmental impacts of manure sep- aration by reverse osmosis. The authors found higher overall impacts when compared to management of raw manure, but reduced GHG emissions and depletion of fossil fuels with the addition of an AD system. Poeschl et al. (2012a) compared different AD systems processing single and mixed feedstocks (Poeschl et al., 2012b). The authors concluded that straw and corn silage produced the most biogas when evaluating single-feedstock technologies and combining municipal solid waste with agricultural and food industry

residues produced the most biogas within co-digestion technologies. Ebner et al. (2015) conducted a life cycle analysis of AD co-digesting dairy manure and industrial food waste. The study concluded that GHG emissions can be reduced by 70% when compared to conventional treatment of manure and food waste. Most LCA studies did not include an evaluation of environmental and economic trade-offs of these different management and processing steps. There is a need to identify a path that maximizes both their en- vironmental and economic impacts. This study will adopt an LCA approach to account for GHG emissions, NH3 emissions and depletion of fossil fuels from different manure management and processing technologies.

The environmental metrics quantified by LCA can be used in an optimization model to maximize environmental and economic benefits and inform decision-makers. However, environmental metrics often have conflicts due to the limitation of current technologies and the nature of the manure management problem. In addition, environmental met- rics conflict with economic metrics in general because of the relatively high costs and immature market of derived products. These metrics need to be balanced to achieve an overall satisfaction of related stakeholders. In this context, decision-making methodolo- gies are needed to uncover the overall conflicts and synergies of both environmental and economic metrics for a large system. These methodologies need to leverage the use of LCA to capture diverse impacts and the use of supply chain optimization techniques to determine suitable transportation routes for manure and derived products, as well as suitable processing technologies to mitigate environmental impacts (Sampat et al., 2017; You and Wang, 2011). Previous LCA optimization work have targeted up to three metrics and thus can either describe the Pareto front and trade-offs in an efficient way, or in- volve a decision-maker to quantify the relative importance of those metrics (Zhang et al., 2013b; Gong and You, 2014; Hu et al., 2018). These methods can become challenging as the number of metrics increase due to the curse of dimensionality. This study analyzes seven metrics and further integrates a utopia-tracking method (Kim and De Weck, 2006) to provide decisions that can resolve conflicts in metrics without the construction of the

129: The computational framework includes three components: LCA, supply chain optimiza- tion model, and utopia-tracking decision-making procedure. The environmental and eco- nomic impacts of cattle manure processing technologies from a modeled dairy and beef farm are first evaluated using an LCA approach. This data is then used by a supply chain optimization model for technology selection and placement, and transportation routing and production planning. The systematic performance of economic and environmental metrics is also evaluated. The supply chain model is solved repeatedly with different objectives, and then a utopia-tracking decision-making procedure is applied to provide an optimal solution of conflict resolution.
130: SCOPE AND SYSTEM BOUNDARIES A partial LCA model (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014) was used to estimate i) GHG emissions, ii) NH3 emissions, and iii) depletion of fossil fuels (DFF) during dairy and beef manure management. These three environmental indicators were quantified during manure collection, storage, processing, and land application. In addition, nutrients (N, P and K) reaching the land were tracked. A 2,306 animal unit (1 AU = 1,000 pound of animal) dairy farm (1,000 lactating cows, 605 growing heifers and 286 mature heifers and dry cows) and a 2,478 AU beef farm (1,000 bulls and cows, 210 replacement cattle, 750 stocker cattle and 750 finishing cattle) were modeled. Manure characteristics were estimated for each animal type for dairy (Nennich et al., 2005) and beef (Lorimor et al., 2008) and aggregated for the herd (Table 4.1).

A total of eleven technologies were modeled for both dairy and beef farms with dif- ferent manure management practices (Figure 4.1). After excretion, manure is collected from the barn in all technologies and then either directly land applied, stored prior to
Table 4.1: Aggregated herd manure characteristics for dairy and beef herds

land application, or processed via sand recovery, AD, mechanical SLS, and liquid manure processing to clean water (SLS + ultrafiltration + reverse osmosis) and then land applied. Manure storage is modeled with and without a liner cover and manure is land applied by surface broadcast and injection. Table 4.2 presents a summary of each technology, where in this context, a technology is defined as a process containing different manure handling operation units.
Table 4.2: Summary of the operations included in each technology

DETERMINATION OF ENVIRONMENTAL METRICS Accounted GHG emissions (expressed in kg CO2-eq) include methane (CH4), direct and indirect nitrous oxide (N2O) from ma- nure, and fossil carbon dioxide (CO2) from the combustion of fossil fuels used on-farm. Characterization factors for CH4 and N2O are 28 and 265 for a 100-year horizon (Myhre et al., 2014). NH3 (expressed in kg) is emitted from manure during collection, storage

Figure 4.1: System boundaries for all eleven modeled technologies. Green arrows represent products of the system, and black arrows represent processes where AD: anaerobic digestion, SLS: solid-liquid separation, UF: ultrafiltration, RO: reverse os- mosis.
and land application and DFF (MJ) comes from the use of grid electricity and diesel for machinery operation.

General manure handling operations include collection, storage and land application. Manure is collected daily with a skid steer that operates with diesel. Manure, digestate, and separated manure/digestate liquids and solids are in an open storage for six months before land application in April and October. A mineralization rate of 16% is assumed given the storage duration (Rotz et al., 2018). An organic crust is modeled in technology scenarios without manure processing due to higher TS in manure which decreases CH4 and NH3 emissions but increases N2O emissions (Rotz et al., 2018). Liner covers on top of the storage are also modeled to mitigate emissions. Manure is agitated then land applied by surface broadcast with diesel applicators or with manure injection to mitigate NH3 emissions.
Mechanical SLS is modeled using a screw press that runs on electricity with separa- tion efficiencies reported in (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2019). A plug-flow digester is used

to produce biogas considering a 28-day retention time. Electricity is generated from bio- gas (65% CH4 with a LHV of 36 MJ/m3) at 35% and 50% electric and thermal efficiency, respectively. The digester is heated using 20% of the thermal energy generated during electricity production. It is assumed that the remaining thermal energy is lost. In addi- tion, the digester demands 17% of the daily produced electricity (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). Total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) is increased by 35% due to mineralization dur- ing the digestion process (Rotz et al., 2018). It is assumed that the produced electricity is used on farm with surpluses injected into the electricity grid. This produced renewable electricity offsets both the lifecycle GHG emissions and DFF embedded in the production and use of grid electricity and are expressed as benefits (negative GHG and DFF). The manure treatment to clean water process runs after AD and consists of three separation systems set up in series: a centrifuge, an ultrafiltration system and a reverse osmosis sys- tem. Centrifugation achieves higher separation efficiencies than screw pressing (Guilayn et al., 2019). After centrifugation, the solid fraction is stored for six months before land application and the liquid digestate is further separated into an untrafiltration concentrate (UFC) stream (containing most of the P) and an untrafiltration permeate (UFP) stream. The UFC is stored for six months before land application and the UFP is further sep- arated by reverse osmosis into a purified water stream (which can be re-used or safely discharged) and a reverse osmosis concentrate (ROC, containing the remaining nutrients and solids) that is stored before land application.
131: SCOPE AND SYSTEM BOUNDARIES A partial LCA model (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014) was used to estimate i) GHG emissions, ii) NH3 emissions, and iii) depletion of fossil fuels (DFF) during dairy and beef manure management. These three environmental indicators were quantified during manure collection, storage, processing, and land application. In addition, nutrients (N, P and K) reaching the land were tracked. A 2,306 animal unit (1 AU = 1,000 pound of animal) dairy farm (1,000 lactating cows, 605 growing heifers and 286 mature heifers and dry cows) and a 2,478 AU beef farm (1,000 bulls and cows, 210 replacement cattle, 750 stocker cattle and 750 finishing cattle) were modeled. Manure characteristics were estimated for each animal type for dairy (Nennich et al., 2005) and beef (Lorimor et al., 2008) and aggregated for the herd (Table 4.1).

A total of eleven technologies were modeled for both dairy and beef farms with dif- ferent manure management practices (Figure 4.1). After excretion, manure is collected from the barn in all technologies and then either directly land applied, stored prior to
Table 4.1: Aggregated herd manure characteristics for dairy and beef herds

land application, or processed via sand recovery, AD, mechanical SLS, and liquid manure processing to clean water (SLS + ultrafiltration + reverse osmosis) and then land applied. Manure storage is modeled with and without a liner cover and manure is land applied by surface broadcast and injection. Table 4.2 presents a summary of each technology, where in this context, a technology is defined as a process containing different manure handling operation units.
Table 4.2: Summary of the operations included in each technology

DETERMINATION OF ENVIRONMENTAL METRICS Accounted GHG emissions (expressed in kg CO2-eq) include methane (CH4), direct and indirect nitrous oxide (N2O) from ma- nure, and fossil carbon dioxide (CO2) from the combustion of fossil fuels used on-farm. Characterization factors for CH4 and N2O are 28 and 265 for a 100-year horizon (Myhre et al., 2014). NH3 (expressed in kg) is emitted from manure during collection, storage

Figure 4.1: System boundaries for all eleven modeled technologies. Green arrows represent products of the system, and black arrows represent processes where AD: anaerobic digestion, SLS: solid-liquid separation, UF: ultrafiltration, RO: reverse os- mosis.
and land application and DFF (MJ) comes from the use of grid electricity and diesel for machinery operation.

General manure handling operations include collection, storage and land application. Manure is collected daily with a skid steer that operates with diesel. Manure, digestate, and separated manure/digestate liquids and solids are in an open storage for six months before land application in April and October. A mineralization rate of 16% is assumed given the storage duration (Rotz et al., 2018). An organic crust is modeled in technology scenarios without manure processing due to higher TS in manure which decreases CH4 and NH3 emissions but increases N2O emissions (Rotz et al., 2018). Liner covers on top of the storage are also modeled to mitigate emissions. Manure is agitated then land applied by surface broadcast with diesel applicators or with manure injection to mitigate NH3 emissions.
Mechanical SLS is modeled using a screw press that runs on electricity with separa- tion efficiencies reported in (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2019). A plug-flow digester is used

to produce biogas considering a 28-day retention time. Electricity is generated from bio- gas (65% CH4 with a LHV of 36 MJ/m3) at 35% and 50% electric and thermal efficiency, respectively. The digester is heated using 20% of the thermal energy generated during electricity production. It is assumed that the remaining thermal energy is lost. In addi- tion, the digester demands 17% of the daily produced electricity (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). Total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) is increased by 35% due to mineralization dur- ing the digestion process (Rotz et al., 2018). It is assumed that the produced electricity is used on farm with surpluses injected into the electricity grid. This produced renewable electricity offsets both the lifecycle GHG emissions and DFF embedded in the production and use of grid electricity and are expressed as benefits (negative GHG and DFF). The manure treatment to clean water process runs after AD and consists of three separation systems set up in series: a centrifuge, an ultrafiltration system and a reverse osmosis sys- tem. Centrifugation achieves higher separation efficiencies than screw pressing (Guilayn et al., 2019). After centrifugation, the solid fraction is stored for six months before land application and the liquid digestate is further separated into an untrafiltration concentrate (UFC) stream (containing most of the P) and an untrafiltration permeate (UFP) stream. The UFC is stored for six months before land application and the UFP is further sep- arated by reverse osmosis into a purified water stream (which can be re-used or safely discharged) and a reverse osmosis concentrate (ROC, containing the remaining nutrients and solids) that is stored before land application.

136: The supply chain optimization model is extended from (Sampat et al., 2017) by integrat- ing multiple environmental metrics, which allows us to evaluate the performance of the supply chain from both economic and environmental dimensions.
In the supply chain model, we use N to represent the set of geographical nodes (e.g., dairy and beef farms, croplands, processing sites, etc.), and P to represent the set of

materials (e.g. raw dairy manure, solid and liquid fractions of separated manure, etc.). The transformation of products occurs in nodes which are equipped with technologies (any technology from Table 4.2), and we define the set of technologies as T. And finally, we use I to represent the set of environmental metrics tracked in this study (e.g. metrics for GHG emissions and nutrient pollution). The constraints and objective functions are introduced below.

MATERIAL BALANCE AND CONVERSION Equation (5.2.1) captures the material bal- ance at each node and for each material. For a node i and material p, the left-hand side represents material accumulation, containing the supply amount Si,p, total generation/- consumption amount t 8i,p,t from technologies installed at node i, and total transporta- tion inflow , Note that the term gi,p,t can be positive or negative, representing a generation or consumption of material p in technology t, respectively. And the right-hand side of the equation represents material consumption, including the demand amount di,p and total transportation outflow j ifi,jp

Equation (5.2.2) captures the transformation relationships of materials in technolo- gies. The generation or consumption amount of product p in technology t installed at node i can be calculated using the consumption amount of the reference material of that technology gi,r(t),t, and the ratio between yield factors At,p. The reference material for a technology is by default the main feedstock. For example, at node i*, assuming that tech- nology t* is installed and can process waste p*, which is also the reference product of t* (p* = r(t*)). We further know that 0.05 unit of biogas (pb) can be generated when 1 unit of p* is consumed (At*,Pb = 0.05, Atx,r(t*) = -1), then for every 1000 unit input of p* (gi*,r(t*),t* = - 1000), the generated biogas amount is gi*,Pb,t* = 50 units.

CAPACITY CONSTRAINTS Equation (4.2.3) captures the possible range of demand term di,p, where di,p is the demand capacity in node i for material p. Equation (4.2.4) captures the possible range of transportation flow fi,j,p, where fi,j,p is the upper bound of trans- portation flow of material p from node i to j. And equation (4.2.5) captures the possible range of the consumption term of reference product of technology t, where Yi,t is a binary variable indicating if technology t is installed at node i, and Cmin,t, Cmax,t represent the minimum and maximum capacity of technology t, respectively. When technology t is in- stalled, its processing capability is bounded by the defined technology capacities; while if technology is not installed, then all generation/consumption terms related become zero.

LOGICAL CONSTRAINTS Equation (4.2.6) and (4.2.7) are two typycal logical constraints used in supply chain design. Equation (4.2.6) means that, some technologies (Tsk) are not allowed to install at some certain nodes (Nsk). For example, it is not practical to install a dairy manure collection system in a beef farm. Equation (4.2.7) indicates that, at most one of some technologies (Tck) is installed at certain nodes (Nck). Assuming that there are different options of anaerobic digestion for dairy manure with different available capacity ranges, then only one of them with specific capacity can be applied. Note that these constraints can be implemented with flexibility for specific case studies by explicitly defining the subset of N and T.
Yi,t Nck, Nck C N, Tck T, 1,2, (4.2.7)

ECONOMIC METRICS We use equations (5.2.6) - (5.2.10) - to calculate economic metrics, including the total investment cost, operation cost, transportation cost, supply cost, and revenue. Equation (5.2.6) divides the investment cost into two parts, fixed investment cost and proportional investment cost, where Yi,t is the binary variable indicating whether technology t is installed at node i and Binv,t is the fixed part of investment cost for technol- ogy t. The term -gi,r(t),tKino,t is the proportional part of investment cost, where -gi,r(t),t is the processing amount of reference material and Kinv,t is the investment cost per unit of processed reference material. Similarly, equation(5.2.7)divides the investment cost into two parts, fixed part Bop,t and proportional part - -gi,r(t),tKop,t. Di,j is the distance between node i and j, and Ktrans,p is the transportation cost per unit of material p per unit distance of movement (equation (5.2.8)). Equations (5.2.9) and (5.2.10) capture the supply cost and demand revenue in the system, respectively, where Pi,p is the supply price for material p at node i, and pap is the demand price for material p at node i.

ENVIRONMENTAL METRICS Multiple environmental metrics from LCA can be inco- porated for comparison and decision-making purposes. Equation (4.2.13) calculates the quantity of metric e at node i, represented by , where Ee,t is the quantity of met-

ric e when technology t processes a unit of its reference material, and is obtained from the LCA output. The quantity of metric e from transportation is calculated by equation (4.2.14), where Ee,p is the unit quantity of metric e for moving a unit of product p by a unit distance. Finally, the system level performance of metric e, is calculated by equation (4.2.15).

OBJECTIVE FUNCTIONS For a rational decision-maker, both economic and environ- mental metrics need to be considered, making this a multi-objective optimization prob- lem. Total cost was used as the economic metric, which is defined by equation (4.2.16).

It is challenging to aggregate multiple environmental metrics into a simple metric given that they have different physical units and impacts. Therefore, the objective func- tions are represented by equation (4.2.17), where the decision-maker aims to minimize all of the metrics simultaneously.

MATERIAL BALANCE AND CONVERSION Equation (5.2.1) captures the material bal- ance at each node and for each material. For a node i and material p, the left-hand side represents material accumulation, containing the supply amount Si,p, total generation/- consumption amount t 8i,p,t from technologies installed at node i, and total transporta- tion inflow , Note that the term gi,p,t can be positive or negative, representing a generation or consumption of material p in technology t, respectively. And the right-hand side of the equation represents material consumption, including the demand amount di,p and total transportation outflow j ifi,jp

Equation (5.2.2) captures the transformation relationships of materials in technolo- gies. The generation or consumption amount of product p in technology t installed at node i can be calculated using the consumption amount of the reference material of that technology gi,r(t),t, and the ratio between yield factors At,p. The reference material for a technology is by default the main feedstock. For example, at node i*, assuming that tech- nology t* is installed and can process waste p*, which is also the reference product of t* (p* = r(t*)). We further know that 0.05 unit of biogas (pb) can be generated when 1 unit of p* is consumed (At*,Pb = 0.05, Atx,r(t*) = -1), then for every 1000 unit input of p* (gi*,r(t*),t* = - 1000), the generated biogas amount is gi*,Pb,t* = 50 units.

CAPACITY CONSTRAINTS Equation (4.2.3) captures the possible range of demand term di,p, where di,p is the demand capacity in node i for material p. Equation (4.2.4) captures the possible range of transportation flow fi,j,p, where fi,j,p is the upper bound of trans- portation flow of material p from node i to j. And equation (4.2.5) captures the possible range of the consumption term of reference product of technology t, where Yi,t is a binary variable indicating if technology t is installed at node i, and Cmin,t, Cmax,t represent the minimum and maximum capacity of technology t, respectively. When technology t is in- stalled, its processing capability is bounded by the defined technology capacities; while if technology is not installed, then all generation/consumption terms related become zero.

LOGICAL CONSTRAINTS Equation (4.2.6) and (4.2.7) are two typycal logical constraints used in supply chain design. Equation (4.2.6) means that, some technologies (Tsk) are not allowed to install at some certain nodes (Nsk). For example, it is not practical to install a dairy manure collection system in a beef farm. Equation (4.2.7) indicates that, at most one of some technologies (Tck) is installed at certain nodes (Nck). Assuming that there are different options of anaerobic digestion for dairy manure with different available capacity ranges, then only one of them with specific capacity can be applied. Note that these constraints can be implemented with flexibility for specific case studies by explicitly defining the subset of N and T.
Yi,t Nck, Nck C N, Tck T, 1,2, (4.2.7)

ECONOMIC METRICS We use equations (5.2.6) - (5.2.10) - to calculate economic metrics, including the total investment cost, operation cost, transportation cost, supply cost, and revenue. Equation (5.2.6) divides the investment cost into two parts, fixed investment cost and proportional investment cost, where Yi,t is the binary variable indicating whether technology t is installed at node i and Binv,t is the fixed part of investment cost for technol- ogy t. The term -gi,r(t),tKino,t is the proportional part of investment cost, where -gi,r(t),t is the processing amount of reference material and Kinv,t is the investment cost per unit of processed reference material. Similarly, equation(5.2.7)divides the investment cost into two parts, fixed part Bop,t and proportional part - -gi,r(t),tKop,t. Di,j is the distance between node i and j, and Ktrans,p is the transportation cost per unit of material p per unit distance of movement (equation (5.2.8)). Equations (5.2.9) and (5.2.10) capture the supply cost and demand revenue in the system, respectively, where Pi,p is the supply price for material p at node i, and pap is the demand price for material p at node i.

ENVIRONMENTAL METRICS Multiple environmental metrics from LCA can be inco- porated for comparison and decision-making purposes. Equation (4.2.13) calculates the quantity of metric e at node i, represented by , where Ee,t is the quantity of met-

ric e when technology t processes a unit of its reference material, and is obtained from the LCA output. The quantity of metric e from transportation is calculated by equation (4.2.14), where Ee,p is the unit quantity of metric e for moving a unit of product p by a unit distance. Finally, the system level performance of metric e, is calculated by equation (4.2.15).

OBJECTIVE FUNCTIONS For a rational decision-maker, both economic and environ- mental metrics need to be considered, making this a multi-objective optimization prob- lem. Total cost was used as the economic metric, which is defined by equation (4.2.16).

It is challenging to aggregate multiple environmental metrics into a simple metric given that they have different physical units and impacts. Therefore, the objective func- tions are represented by equation (4.2.17), where the decision-maker aims to minimize all of the metrics simultaneously.

Equation (4.2.4) captures the possible range of transportation flow fi,j,p, where fi,j,p is the upper bound of trans- portation flow of material p from node i to j. And equation (4.2.5) captures the possible range of the consumption term of reference product of technology t, where Yi,t is a binary variable indicating if technology t is installed at node i, and Cmin,t, Cmax,t represent the minimum and maximum capacity of technology t, respectively. When technology t is in- stalled, its processing capability is bounded by the defined technology capacities; while if technology is not installed, then all generation/consumption terms related become zero.

LOGICAL CONSTRAINTS Equation (4.2.6) and (4.2.7) are two typycal logical constraints used in supply chain design. Equation (4.2.6) means that, some technologies (Tsk) are not allowed to install at some certain nodes (Nsk). For example, it is not practical to install a dairy manure collection system in a beef farm. Equation (4.2.7) indicates that, at most one of some technologies (Tck) is installed at certain nodes (Nck). Assuming that there are different options of anaerobic digestion for dairy manure with different available capacity ranges, then only one of them with specific capacity can be applied. Note that these constraints can be implemented with flexibility for specific case studies by explicitly defining the subset of N and T.
Yi,t Nck, Nck C N, Tck T, 1,2, (4.2.7)

ECONOMIC METRICS We use equations (5.2.6) - (5.2.10) - to calculate economic metrics, including the total investment cost, operation cost, transportation cost, supply cost, and revenue. Equation (5.2.6) divides the investment cost into two parts, fixed investment cost and proportional investment cost, where Yi,t is the binary variable indicating whether technology t is installed at node i and Binv,t is the fixed part of investment cost for technol- ogy t. The term -gi,r(t),tKino,t is the proportional part of investment cost, where -gi,r(t),t is the processing amount of reference material and Kinv,t is the investment cost per unit of processed reference material. Similarly, equation(5.2.7)divides the investment cost into two parts, fixed part Bop,t and proportional part - -gi,r(t),tKop,t. Di,j is the distance between node i and j, and Ktrans,p is the transportation cost per unit of material p per unit distance of movement (equation (5.2.8)). Equations (5.2.9) and (5.2.10) capture the supply cost and demand revenue in the system, respectively, where Pi,p is the supply price for material p at node i, and pap is the demand price for material p at node i.

ENVIRONMENTAL METRICS Multiple environmental metrics from LCA can be inco- porated for comparison and decision-making purposes. Equation (4.2.13) calculates the quantity of metric e at node i, represented by , where Ee,t is the quantity of met-

ric e when technology t processes a unit of its reference material, and is obtained from the LCA output. The quantity of metric e from transportation is calculated by equation (4.2.14), where Ee,p is the unit quantity of metric e for moving a unit of product p by a unit distance. Finally, the system level performance of metric e, is calculated by equation (4.2.15).

OBJECTIVE FUNCTIONS For a rational decision-maker, both economic and environ- mental metrics need to be considered, making this a multi-objective optimization prob- lem. Total cost was used as the economic metric, which is defined by equation (4.2.16).

It is challenging to aggregate multiple environmental metrics into a simple metric given that they have different physical units and impacts. Therefore, the objective func- tions are represented by equation (4.2.17), where the decision-maker aims to minimize all of the metrics simultaneously.

The constraints and objective functions are introduced below.
