144: For the convenience of discussion, we first denote the collection of decision variables as X, and rewrite the objective functions as f1(x), f2(x), fk(x), where k = :I+1. We further
denote the feasible set of the multi-product multi-objective supply chain model as X, which is defined by constraints (S15)-(S29).

Multiple (conflicting) objectives are commonly addressed by using optimization algo- rithms to obtain the whole Pareto front (PF). From there, the PF can be used to make a final decision based on expert knowledge of a decision-maker. To obtain the whole Pareto front, usually a large number of problems need to be solved (e.g., the multi-stage e-constraint method (Palli et al., 1998)) as shown in the following problem (4.2.18), where Ej is some pre-defined threshold for objective fi(x).
min fi(x) x E X fj(x)

This method is computationally expensive, since one point on the PF requires solving the whole problem once, and the number of points needed to describe the complete PF grows exponentially as the number of objective functions increases. Moreover, some sampling strategies are also necessary to balance points on different regions of the PF, which can take additional computational resources. Some evolutionary algorithms have been proposed to reduce the computational complexity, such as NSGA-II and MOPSO method (Deb et al., 2002; Coello and Lechuga, 2002). Those algorithms do not guarantee optimality in general, and they are not suitable for solving large-scale problems (Antonio and Coello, 2013).

An alternative method is to use a utopia-point-based conflict resolution framework, or utopia-tracking method, to help propose a balanced decision without the construction of the whole PF (Kim and De Weck, 2006; Fuentes-Cortés et al., 2016). The principle idea is to first identify the best possible performance of each objective function, and then denote the combination of them as the utopia point. In most cases, the utopia point is not achievable since there are conflicts between objective functions. However, in the
utopia-tracking method, a decision that achieves the closest distance to the utopia point is recommended, which is called the optimal compromised solution (OCS).

The first step in the utopia-tracking method is to determine the best possible per- formance of each metric by solving the k problems indicated by equation (4.2.19). The optimal objective values for those problems are denoted as and the combi- nation of them, i.e., is defined as the utopia point.

Objective functions have different scales, which makes it difficult to minimize the distance to the utopia point since those objective functions weight very differently in the distance calculation. To scale those objective functions properly, an additional step to find their maximum values on the PF (i.e., Nadir point) is necessary. For problems with more than two dimensions, the Nadir point can be approximated using a pay-off matrix (Benayoun et al., 1971), indicated by equation (4.2.20).

In the pay-off matrix P, the diagonal elements are the objective values from prob- lem (4.2.19), and the off-diagonal elements fij are the objective values of the following problems (4.2.21), i.e., to optimize over a secondary objective while keeping the primary objective at its optimal value. This procedure of constructing the pay-off matrix P is named as lexicographic optimization (Aghaei et al., 2011).

The worst possible values of objective fi(x) on PF, denoted as fN, are approximated by taking the largest value in the pay-off matrix, as indicated by equation (4.2.22), where [fN,f2,...,FN] denote the approximated Nadir points.

With this information, we can define scaled objective functions, denoted as o = with elements defined by equation (4.2.23). It is clear that in the scaled space, all scaled objective values are larger than (or equal to) o, and on the PF, the objective values are approximately smaller than 1. In addition, the corresponding utopia point in the scaled space is the origin. Therefore, the optimal compromised solution xoc minimizes the distance to the origin in the scaled objective space, as indicated by equation (4.2.24), where 11-11p represents the p-norm used in distance calculation. Specifically, if the Manhattan norm is applied (p = 1), problem defined by equation(4.2.24) is linear; while if the Euclidean norm is applied (p = 2), the problem becomes quadratic. We further denote the objective values of the optimal compromised solution as = 01-11-17 (4.2.23) xoc = arg min||o|p (4.2.24)

The overall decision-making procedure is presented in Figure 4.2, consisting of utopia point generation, pay-off matrix construction, and optimal compromised solution gener- ation. The procedure of a bi-objective problem is illustrated graphically in Figure 4.3. The

overall structure needs to solve the whole supply chain model k2+1 times, one of which is a mixed-integer linear/quadratic programming (MILP/MIQP) problem depending on the selection of norm, and others are MILP problems.

Figure 4.3: Utopia-tracking method for bi-objective problems

Objective functions have different scales, which makes it difficult to minimize the distance to the utopia point since those objective functions weight very differently in the distance calculation. To scale those objective functions properly, an additional step to find their maximum values on the PF (i.e., Nadir point) is necessary. For problems with more than two dimensions, the Nadir point can be approximated using a pay-off matrix (Benayoun et al., 1971), indicated by equation (4.2.20).

In the pay-off matrix P, the diagonal elements are the objective values from prob- lem (4.2.19), and the off-diagonal elements fij are the objective values of the following problems (4.2.21), i.e., to optimize over a secondary objective while keeping the primary objective at its optimal value. This procedure of constructing the pay-off matrix P is named as lexicographic optimization (Aghaei et al., 2011).

The worst possible values of objective fi(x) on PF, denoted as fN, are approximated by taking the largest value in the pay-off matrix, as indicated by equation (4.2.22), where [fN,f2,...,FN] denote the approximated Nadir points.

With this information, we can define scaled objective functions, denoted as o = with elements defined by equation (4.2.23). It is clear that in the scaled space, all scaled objective values are larger than (or equal to) o, and on the PF, the objective values are approximately smaller than 1. In addition, the corresponding utopia point in the scaled space is the origin. Therefore, the optimal compromised solution xoc minimizes the distance to the origin in the scaled objective space, as indicated by equation (4.2.24), where 11-11p represents the p-norm used in distance calculation. Specifically, if the Manhattan norm is applied (p = 1), problem defined by equation(4.2.24) is linear; while if the Euclidean norm is applied (p = 2), the problem becomes quadratic. We further denote the objective values of the optimal compromised solution as = 01-11-17 (4.2.23) xoc = arg min||o|p (4.2.24)

The overall decision-making procedure is presented in Figure 4.2, consisting of utopia point generation, pay-off matrix construction, and optimal compromised solution gener- ation. The procedure of a bi-objective problem is illustrated graphically in Figure 4.3. The

overall structure needs to solve the whole supply chain model k2+1 times, one of which is a mixed-integer linear/quadratic programming (MILP/MIQP) problem depending on the selection of norm, and others are MILP problems.

Figure 4.3: Utopia-tracking method for bi-objective problems
149: For the convenience of discussion, we first denote the collection of decision variables as X, and rewrite the objective functions as f1(x), f2(x), fk(x), where k = :I+1. We further
denote the feasible set of the multi-product multi-objective supply chain model as X, which is defined by constraints (S15)-(S29).

Multiple (conflicting) objectives are commonly addressed by using optimization algo- rithms to obtain the whole Pareto front (PF). From there, the PF can be used to make a final decision based on expert knowledge of a decision-maker. To obtain the whole Pareto front, usually a large number of problems need to be solved (e.g., the multi-stage e-constraint method (Palli et al., 1998)) as shown in the following problem (4.2.18), where Ej is some pre-defined threshold for objective fi(x).
min fi(x) x E X fj(x)

This method is computationally expensive, since one point on the PF requires solving the whole problem once, and the number of points needed to describe the complete PF grows exponentially as the number of objective functions increases. Moreover, some sampling strategies are also necessary to balance points on different regions of the PF, which can take additional computational resources. Some evolutionary algorithms have been proposed to reduce the computational complexity, such as NSGA-II and MOPSO method (Deb et al., 2002; Coello and Lechuga, 2002). Those algorithms do not guarantee optimality in general, and they are not suitable for solving large-scale problems (Antonio and Coello, 2013).

An alternative method is to use a utopia-point-based conflict resolution framework, or utopia-tracking method, to help propose a balanced decision without the construction of the whole PF (Kim and De Weck, 2006; Fuentes-Cortés et al., 2016). The principle idea is to first identify the best possible performance of each objective function, and then denote the combination of them as the utopia point. In most cases, the utopia point is not achievable since there are conflicts between objective functions. However, in the
utopia-tracking method, a decision that achieves the closest distance to the utopia point is recommended, which is called the optimal compromised solution (OCS).

The first step in the utopia-tracking method is to determine the best possible per- formance of each metric by solving the k problems indicated by equation (4.2.19). The optimal objective values for those problems are denoted as and the combi- nation of them, i.e., is defined as the utopia point.

151: In this section, we demonstrate the applicability of the proposed computatioanl frame- work by considering a case study in WI.
152: The map in Figure 4.4 shows the geographical location and boundary of the study region, as well as the cropland and farms (beef and dairy) in the region.

Figure 4.4: Geographical boundary and agricultural activities in Up- per Yahara Watershed: red circles are farms with the size representing amount of manure produced annually, and green areas represent crop- lands with dark green representing high cropland density

The Upper Yahara Watershed (lake Mendota basin) area has already been well stud- ied in terms of manure storage (Larson et al., 2016), manure pelletizing (Sharara et al., 2018), and nutrient pollution and harmful algal blooms (HABs) (Sampat et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2019). The study region is affected heavily by nutrient pollution due to intensified

agricultural activities, and Dane county has made continuous efforts to reduce nutrient pollution, especially P loading, to improve the quality of lakes (Madison Metropolitan Sewerage District Staff, 2016). We define the nutrient balance index (NBI) as the ratio of nutrient applied to land to the amount of nutrient that is removed by crops. This im- plies that when NBI is greater than one, the nutrient will accumulate in the region and potentially create nutrient pollution issues while, when NBI is less than one, it indicates that there is a nutrient deficiency in the soil (overall soil fertility may be declining and therefore is not sustainable in the long-run). The NBI value for P in the Upper Yahara Watershed in the year 2012 and 2013, was estimated to be 1.95 and 1.35, respectively (Hu et al., 2019).

From previous studies, it was found that to reduce P loading and HABs, the most effective strategies are manure storage planning (shift land application time), transporta- tion (change land application location), and simple technologies like solid-liquid sepa- ration (concentrate nutrients for improved distribution). However, it can be speculated that all three strategies have negative impact on some other environmental metrics. For example, fossil fuels are consumed directly to transport and process manure. In addition, it has been reported that manure storage significantly increases CH4 emissions (Aguirre- Villegas et al., 2019) due to the anaerobic environment created when liquid and slurry manure is stored. Additionally, the crops in this study regions have different nutrient demand levels. If we consider other key nutrients for crop growth, such as N and potas- sium (K), we estimated the NBI for N, P, and K to be 1.27, 1.65, and 1.47, respectively, in the year of 2017. Figure 4.5 further shows that the relative needs of N and P have different spatial distributions. This is estimated by the crop type cultivated in each field, and nutrient needs (annual removal rate) of those crops. Areas plotted in red have rel- atively large P needs and areas in blue have relatively large N needs. Although P is the main nutrient that causes nutrient pollution in inland surface waterbodies, it has been reported that N contributes to nutrient pollution in marine water (Morelli et al., 2018). An optimized solution will take all these metrics into account and facilitate a more com-
Figure 4.5: Relative nutrient needs in Upper Yahara Watershed

156: The supply chain optimization model requires extensive input data, including farm (sup- ply) data, technology data, transportation data, and cropland (consumer) data. In this study, we consider 290 nodes, consisting of 55 beef farms, 148 dairy farms, 88 aggre- gated cropland nodes for computational simplicity(Sharara et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2019), and an external customer node. The objective functions considered include total cost, systems-level GHG, NH3, DFF, net N, P, and K release.

In this supply chain system, farms collect and supply livestock manure (beef manure and dairy cow manure). The annual supply amount of each farm is estimated by consid- ering the size of the farm (number of animal units, 1 AU = 1,000 pounds) and average manure generation amount per animal type reported by Lorimor et al. (2008). Then, the livestock manure can either be fed into a technology to produce value-added products or be sent to croplands directly without any processing. The product transformation rela- tionships, and the environmental emission data of technology are obtained from the LCA modeling. The investment and operational costs of technologies are obtained from liter-

ature and technical reports (English and Fleming, 2006; Gooch et al., 2002; Bentley et al., 2016; Beddoes et al., 2007). Regarding technology placement in the system, we assume that they can only be installed at farms and not at croplands. The general structure of the supply chain system is illustrated in Figure 4.6.
|||Application|Customer External Manure Collection (Farms)||Manure Processing (Farms)|(Croplands)|Customer External ||||Customer External ||||Customer External
Figure 4.6: Supply chain setup for the case study

A large amount of livestock manure and derived products (e.g., digestate from biogas generation) are applied to croplands. To calculate the net nutrient release on croplands, the amount of nutrients taken by crops is estimated using crop types in each land, land area, and the nutrient removal rate of each crop reported by the USDA crop nutrient tool (USA. Department of Agriculture (Washington). Soil Conservation Service, 1992). The amount of nutrient release at each cropland is calculated by considering the amount of applied livestock manure or by-products from technologies (e.g., digestate), and the corresponding nutrient availability in that stream, where the latter part is obtained from the LCA modeling. The net nutrient release is then defined as the difference between overall nutrient release and nutrient uptake. In addition, we assume that all biogas will be used for producing electricity locally, which will then be sold into the grid at standard market price 0.1 USD/kWh. We also place a hypothetical external customer at the City of Madison, which will collect solid products with a fixed total capacity and remove

159: For the baseline scenario (Scenario 1), we only include prevalent technologies (technology 1-10 in Table 4.2) since UF and RO are usually not widely integrated. All products, such as electricity, are sold directly under market price without any subsidies from the government. The decision-making procedure described in Figure 4.2 is followed to get the OCS (norm 1 is applied due to computational complexity). Since we have 7 objective functions, we need to solve 7 MILP problems to find the utopia point, 42 MILP problems to construct the pay-off matrix, and one additional MILP for the OCS.

In Scenaio 2, we introduce the novel treatment of manure to clean water technology (technology 11 in Table 4.2) into the supply chain system and monitor the impact of its availability (personal communication, John Mylin, 2020). Scenario 3 considers prevalent technologies with subsidies introduced into the system in the form of energy credits, i.e., the system will obtain additional profits when it recovers renewable energy from livestock manure. For Scenarios 2 and 3, the same decision-making procedure is followed to analyze how external factors can manipulate the optimal compromised solution and provide insights to policy-makers and stakeholders.

We note that with the specified study area and corresponding data, each model to be solved becomes a large-scale MILP problem. For example, we solved 2,548,727 continuous variables, 9,280 binary variables, and 313,699 linear constraints for one typical model. The models were implemented using Julia-based modeling framework JuMP (Version 0.21.2) and were solved with Gurobi (Version 9.0.3). Each of the models is computationally challenging and and modeling the total 50 problems can take weeks to finish. Some of the problems were not solved to optimality within the imposed 35-hour time limit. In that case, the best solution found (usually with an optimality gap less than 10%) is used for analysis purposes.

1: Table 4.3 shows the pay-off matrix after solving all problems in Scenario 1. In the pay-off matrix, the utopia point consists of diagonal elements (highlighted by asterisk*), and the approximated Nadir point consists of maximum values of each column (highlighted by underscore). M1 - M2 denotes the problem with metric M1 as the primary objective and metric M2 as the secondary objective. For example, Cost - GHG represents the problem which keeps cost at minimum value and then minimizes GHG emissions (row 1 column 2). Specifically, Cost - Cost represent the problem with Cost as the only objective. In addition, we use the notation M1 - * to represent the set of problems whose primary objective is metric M1.

Metric|Cost|GHG|NH3|DFF|Net N|Net P|Net K Unit|million USD per year|tonne CO2-eq per year|tonne NH3-eq per year|GJ per year|tonne N per year|tonne P per year|tonne K per year (1) min Cost|10*|19389|2201|34|2297|262|2008 (2) min GHG|19|236*|1966|-705|2538|283|2146 (3) min NH3|12|37911|494|39|851|34|1537 (4) min DFF|20|537|2050|-706*|2153|285|2069 (5) min Net N|22|45669|1940|-112|149*|<0.1|1418 (6) min Net P|16|800|669|-697|150|o*|1400 (7) min Net K|19|9718|1253|-580|208|<0.1|1400
Table 4.3: Pay-off matrix and utopia point of Scenario 1

We note that the pay-off matrix and utopia point can provide useful insights to decision-makers, especially regarding the limiting behavior of each metric and the global conflicts between a pair of objectives. Generally, we can find that cost conflicts with all other environmental objectives. It is not profitable to reduce any of the analyzed environmental impacts in this study with traditional technologies. Within those environ- mental objectives, GHG shows strong conflicts with nutrients (especially N and K), but a weak conflict with DFF. Similarly, DFF shows conflicts with nutrient and costs, but it
has stronger conflicts with NH3. The three nutrient objectives, N, P, and K, show a weak conflict with one another, showing potential for simultaneous improvement.

When only the total cost is minimized (problem Cost - *), the supply chain is purely economic-driven and there is no technology installed. This is because the current market prices of electricity and nutrient products cannot cover the processing and investment cost. All farms send the livestock manure directly to the nearest croplands for application in order to reduce the transportation cost. Therefore, the 10 million USD/year is a baseline cost involving manure collection, transport, and application in the supply chain. At the minimum cost, the best achievable values of other objectives are listed in the first row of the pay-off matrix. GHG emissions reach 19389 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year, most of which is contributed by the manure collection and application process (19300 tonnes) and only a small fraction (89 tonnes) by the transportation process. The net nutrient release is also high because the farms transport manure to nearest land regardless of its nutrient condition, which can further intensify nutrient pollution in some areas. The average transportation distance of raw manure is 0.98 km. We note that all problems in row 1 lead to the same solution. The corresponding supply chain design is presented in Figure 4.7(a).

When the system minimizes GHG emissions (problem GHG - *), the minimum pos- sible value is 236 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year, which is order of magnitude smaller compared to the value when minimizing cost. This minimum value can be only achieved when the system reduces GHG emissions from livestock manure processing and trans- portation activity simultaneously. In the supply chain, all of the livestock manure is col- lected and processed by technology 9 (sand recovery, AD, storage with coverage), which is the most carbon-friendly technology from the LCA perspective. The AD system produces renewable energy and brings carbon credits from the displacement of grid electricity, and the covered storage can minimize CH4 emissions after the digestion process. The an- nual system cost increases to 19 million USD/year, where 50.5% of the cost come from operating technologies, 24.9% is from capital investment, and the rest is transportation

cost. In order to reduce the GHG emissions, transportation also needs to be minimized and it causes high net nutrient release since it neglects the nutrient conditions. For this problem, 172 tonnes of CO2-eq are from transportation, and only 65 tonnes of CO2-eq are contributed by technologies (due to the carbon offset). The supply chain design for this problem is presented in Figure 4.7(b). In the design, the total technology capacity is able to process all livestock manure in the study region, SO that the GHG emission from manure is minimized. The placement of those technologies is configured SO that the overall transportation activities are reduced. There are 62 technologies installed in total, with capacities ranging from 8000 to 58500 metric tonnes of raw manure per year. The average distance of transporting raw manure (2.06 km) is longer than digestate (0.72 km) because the digestate is diluted during sand recovery.
Figure 4.7: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem Cost - * and (b) GHG - *

For both of the previously discussed objectives, there is no degeneracy found in the solution (i.e., all problems in the same row of Table 4.3 obtain the same solution). This is not the case for the other five objectives. As an example, when minimizing NH3, as long as all the manure in the system is processed by the technology that has least NH3 emissions (i.e., technology 7, storage with coverage) and then applied by injection, then

the overall supply chain system presents the minimum NH3 emissions (494 tonne of NH3 per year). The NH3 emissions in transportation activity is negligible, therefore, the placement of those technologies can be arbitrary. As a result, the system has additional degrees of freedom to adjust other objectives while keeping the minimum NH3 emissions, by tuning technology placement and transportation activities. Results of two problems, NH3 - GHG and NH3 - N are presented in Figure 4.8(a) and 4.8(b). When we minimize GHG while keeping the minimum NH3 emissions, all farms are equipped with storage systems. This system only seeks to minimize GHG during transportation given that all manure is processed by technology 7 and GHG emissions during manure processing are already fixed. By installing a technology in each farm, the movement of raw manure is avoided, and the transportation of stored manure can be minimized. Therefore, the average transportation distances of raw manure and stored manure are o and 0.98 km, re- spectively (same as problem Cost - *). Alternatively, if net N is selected as the secondary objective, the system will first process all the manure, then activate transportation activi- ties to move processed (stored) manure streams according to the estimated N demand in cropland to balance it. The average transportation distances are 12.89 km and 13.28 km for raw manure and stored manure, respectively.
Figure 4.8: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem NH3 - GHG and (b) NH3 - N

The minimum DFF value is achieved when all the livestock manure is collected and then processed by technology 4 (sand recovery, AD, and storage without cover). The movement of materials is also reduced given that transportation contributes to DFF. The DFF minimization problems show similar behavior to the GHG ones, which conflict with cost, NH3 and nutrients. The main difference is that, for DFF the selected technology has an uncovered storage. The coverage does not impact DFF during the manure processing, but without the cover, more mass is emitted to the gas atmosphere and the weight of digestate, as well as its transportation cost, can be slightly reduced. Most problems in row 4 that minimizes DFF in the pay-off matrix solve to very similar designs, which means that although there are extra degrees of freedom to adjust secondary objectives, the system is already very constrained. Two of the supply chain designs are displayed in Figure 4.9(a) and 4.9(b), corresponding to problem DFF - Cost and DFF - N, respectively. As can be observed, except from different technology sizing, there are only some subtle differences in transportation of those two designs (highlighted by blue circles).
Figure 4.9: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem DFF - Cost and (b) DFF - N, where blue circles on the right highlights the differences between the two similar designs
The last three rows in the pay-off matrix represent system performance when net nutrient (N, P, K) releases are considered as the primary objective functions. We can

see that for the study region, only P can be perfectly balanced by applying solid-liquid separation technologies (potentially in combination AD and covered storage) and proper transportation management. Once the system achieves P balance, it can use its remaining resources to improve performance of other objectives (i.e., the solution of problem P - * has degeneracy). This can be inferred from row 6 of the pay-off matrix, where all objective function values are relatively close to their corresponding optimal values. The results of two problems, P - Cost and P - GHG are given below as an example in Figure 4.10(a) and 4.10(b), respectively. Both of them use solid-liquid separation technologies to produce solid products, which can be sold to the external customer to drive P outside of the study region. Transportation distance of raw livestock manure and applicable streams (liquid manure, digestate) is increased from previous cases that move P from the west area (with P surplus) to the east area (with P deficiency) to better balance nutrient. For problem P - Cost that focuses on cost, the system tends to install cheaper technologies (ony SLS) and transport extra solid streams to far croplands to reduce transportation cost. The average transportation distances for raw manure, solid products, and liquid products are 2.32 km, 18.13km, and 1.16 km, respectively. While for problem P - GHG, the GHG-friendly technologies, AD, will be installed more frequently. Similarly, for problem P - NH3, storage systems (with storage cover) are installed to reduce NH3 emissions. For problem P - N and P - K, the transportation activities are adjusted in order to better match the nutrient needs in croplands. Because some of these problems do not consider overall costs, there can be irrational economic decisions (e.g., loops in transportation routes and unnecessary technology installations).

For the other two nutrient objectives, N and K, the minimum net release values are 149 and 1400 tonnes per year, respectively. The general strategy is the same, i.e., to use SLS to separate manure and transport products based on nutrient need in croplands. While compared to P, the system now has more limited capability to tackle other non-nutrient targets, as can be seen in row 5 and 7 of the pay-off matrix which minimize net N and K, respectively. The results of two problems, N - Cost and N - GHG are given below

as an example in Figure 4.11(a) and 4.11(b), respectively. We can see the two problems are different in terms of technology placement and transportation activities, but not in technology types. The problem N - Cost has larger technology sizes due to economics of scale, while N - GHG has more distributed facilities in order to reduce GHG emissions in transportation.

Figure 4.10: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem P - Cost and (b) P - GHG, where pink dots represent pure SLS technology, grey dots represent AD technology with coverage, dark green lines are transportation flows of solid fraction of livestock raw ma- nure, purple lines are for liquid fraction of livestock manure, light green lines are for solid fraction of digestate, and magenta lines are for liquid fraction of digestate

The average distance of transporting raw manure (2.06 km) is longer than digestate (0.72 km) because the digestate is diluted during sand recovery.
Figure 4.7: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem Cost - * and (b) GHG - *

For both of the previously discussed objectives, there is no degeneracy found in the solution (i.e., all problems in the same row of Table 4.3 obtain the same solution). This is not the case for the other five objectives. As an example, when minimizing NH3, as long as all the manure in the system is processed by the technology that has least NH3 emissions (i.e., technology 7, storage with coverage) and then applied by injection, then

the overall supply chain system presents the minimum NH3 emissions (494 tonne of NH3 per year). The NH3 emissions in transportation activity is negligible, therefore, the placement of those technologies can be arbitrary. As a result, the system has additional degrees of freedom to adjust other objectives while keeping the minimum NH3 emissions, by tuning technology placement and transportation activities. Results of two problems, NH3 - GHG and NH3 - N are presented in Figure 4.8(a) and 4.8(b). When we minimize GHG while keeping the minimum NH3 emissions, all farms are equipped with storage systems. This system only seeks to minimize GHG during transportation given that all manure is processed by technology 7 and GHG emissions during manure processing are already fixed. By installing a technology in each farm, the movement of raw manure is avoided, and the transportation of stored manure can be minimized. Therefore, the average transportation distances of raw manure and stored manure are o and 0.98 km, re- spectively (same as problem Cost - *). Alternatively, if net N is selected as the secondary objective, the system will first process all the manure, then activate transportation activi- ties to move processed (stored) manure streams according to the estimated N demand in cropland to balance it. The average transportation distances are 12.89 km and 13.28 km for raw manure and stored manure, respectively.
Figure 4.8: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem NH3 - GHG and (b) NH3 - N

The minimum DFF value is achieved when all the livestock manure is collected and then processed by technology 4 (sand recovery, AD, and storage without cover). The movement of materials is also reduced given that transportation contributes to DFF. The DFF minimization problems show similar behavior to the GHG ones, which conflict with cost, NH3 and nutrients. The main difference is that, for DFF the selected technology has an uncovered storage. The coverage does not impact DFF during the manure processing, but without the cover, more mass is emitted to the gas atmosphere and the weight of digestate, as well as its transportation cost, can be slightly reduced. Most problems in row 4 that minimizes DFF in the pay-off matrix solve to very similar designs, which means that although there are extra degrees of freedom to adjust secondary objectives, the system is already very constrained. Two of the supply chain designs are displayed in Figure 4.9(a) and 4.9(b), corresponding to problem DFF - Cost and DFF - N, respectively. As can be observed, except from different technology sizing, there are only some subtle differences in transportation of those two designs (highlighted by blue circles).
Figure 4.9: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem DFF - Cost and (b) DFF - N, where blue circles on the right highlights the differences between the two similar designs
The last three rows in the pay-off matrix represent system performance when net nutrient (N, P, K) releases are considered as the primary objective functions. We can

see that for the study region, only P can be perfectly balanced by applying solid-liquid separation technologies (potentially in combination AD and covered storage) and proper transportation management. Once the system achieves P balance, it can use its remaining resources to improve performance of other objectives (i.e., the solution of problem P - * has degeneracy). This can be inferred from row 6 of the pay-off matrix, where all objective function values are relatively close to their corresponding optimal values. The results of two problems, P - Cost and P - GHG are given below as an example in Figure 4.10(a) and 4.10(b), respectively. Both of them use solid-liquid separation technologies to produce solid products, which can be sold to the external customer to drive P outside of the study region. Transportation distance of raw livestock manure and applicable streams (liquid manure, digestate) is increased from previous cases that move P from the west area (with P surplus) to the east area (with P deficiency) to better balance nutrient. For problem P - Cost that focuses on cost, the system tends to install cheaper technologies (ony SLS) and transport extra solid streams to far croplands to reduce transportation cost. The average transportation distances for raw manure, solid products, and liquid products are 2.32 km, 18.13km, and 1.16 km, respectively. While for problem P - GHG, the GHG-friendly technologies, AD, will be installed more frequently. Similarly, for problem P - NH3, storage systems (with storage cover) are installed to reduce NH3 emissions. For problem P - N and P - K, the transportation activities are adjusted in order to better match the nutrient needs in croplands. Because some of these problems do not consider overall costs, there can be irrational economic decisions (e.g., loops in transportation routes and unnecessary technology installations).

For the other two nutrient objectives, N and K, the minimum net release values are 149 and 1400 tonnes per year, respectively. The general strategy is the same, i.e., to use SLS to separate manure and transport products based on nutrient need in croplands. While compared to P, the system now has more limited capability to tackle other non-nutrient targets, as can be seen in row 5 and 7 of the pay-off matrix which minimize net N and K, respectively. The results of two problems, N - Cost and N - GHG are given below

as an example in Figure 4.11(a) and 4.11(b), respectively. We can see the two problems are different in terms of technology placement and transportation activities, but not in technology types. The problem N - Cost has larger technology sizes due to economics of scale, while N - GHG has more distributed facilities in order to reduce GHG emissions in transportation.

Figure 4.10: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem P - Cost and (b) P - GHG, where pink dots represent pure SLS technology, grey dots represent AD technology with coverage, dark green lines are transportation flows of solid fraction of livestock raw ma- nure, purple lines are for liquid fraction of livestock manure, light green lines are for solid fraction of digestate, and magenta lines are for liquid fraction of digestate
4: Table 4.3 shows the pay-off matrix after solving all problems in Scenario 1. In the pay-off matrix, the utopia point consists of diagonal elements (highlighted by asterisk*), and the approximated Nadir point consists of maximum values of each column (highlighted by underscore). M1 - M2 denotes the problem with metric M1 as the primary objective and metric M2 as the secondary objective. For example, Cost - GHG represents the problem which keeps cost at minimum value and then minimizes GHG emissions (row 1 column 2). Specifically, Cost - Cost represent the problem with Cost as the only objective. In addition, we use the notation M1 - * to represent the set of problems whose primary objective is metric M1.

Metric|Cost|GHG|NH3|DFF|Net N|Net P|Net K Unit|million USD per year|tonne CO2-eq per year|tonne NH3-eq per year|GJ per year|tonne N per year|tonne P per year|tonne K per year (1) min Cost|10*|19389|2201|34|2297|262|2008 (2) min GHG|19|236*|1966|-705|2538|283|2146 (3) min NH3|12|37911|494|39|851|34|1537 (4) min DFF|20|537|2050|-706*|2153|285|2069 (5) min Net N|22|45669|1940|-112|149*|<0.1|1418 (6) min Net P|16|800|669|-697|150|o*|1400 (7) min Net K|19|9718|1253|-580|208|<0.1|1400
Table 4.3: Pay-off matrix and utopia point of Scenario 1

We note that the pay-off matrix and utopia point can provide useful insights to decision-makers, especially regarding the limiting behavior of each metric and the global conflicts between a pair of objectives. Generally, we can find that cost conflicts with all other environmental objectives. It is not profitable to reduce any of the analyzed environmental impacts in this study with traditional technologies. Within those environ- mental objectives, GHG shows strong conflicts with nutrients (especially N and K), but a weak conflict with DFF. Similarly, DFF shows conflicts with nutrient and costs, but it
has stronger conflicts with NH3. The three nutrient objectives, N, P, and K, show a weak conflict with one another, showing potential for simultaneous improvement.

When only the total cost is minimized (problem Cost - *), the supply chain is purely economic-driven and there is no technology installed. This is because the current market prices of electricity and nutrient products cannot cover the processing and investment cost. All farms send the livestock manure directly to the nearest croplands for application in order to reduce the transportation cost. Therefore, the 10 million USD/year is a baseline cost involving manure collection, transport, and application in the supply chain. At the minimum cost, the best achievable values of other objectives are listed in the first row of the pay-off matrix. GHG emissions reach 19389 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year, most of which is contributed by the manure collection and application process (19300 tonnes) and only a small fraction (89 tonnes) by the transportation process. The net nutrient release is also high because the farms transport manure to nearest land regardless of its nutrient condition, which can further intensify nutrient pollution in some areas. The average transportation distance of raw manure is 0.98 km. We note that all problems in row 1 lead to the same solution. The corresponding supply chain design is presented in Figure 4.7(a).

When the system minimizes GHG emissions (problem GHG - *), the minimum pos- sible value is 236 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year, which is order of magnitude smaller compared to the value when minimizing cost. This minimum value can be only achieved when the system reduces GHG emissions from livestock manure processing and trans- portation activity simultaneously. In the supply chain, all of the livestock manure is col- lected and processed by technology 9 (sand recovery, AD, storage with coverage), which is the most carbon-friendly technology from the LCA perspective. The AD system produces renewable energy and brings carbon credits from the displacement of grid electricity, and the covered storage can minimize CH4 emissions after the digestion process. The an- nual system cost increases to 19 million USD/year, where 50.5% of the cost come from operating technologies, 24.9% is from capital investment, and the rest is transportation

cost. In order to reduce the GHG emissions, transportation also needs to be minimized and it causes high net nutrient release since it neglects the nutrient conditions. For this problem, 172 tonnes of CO2-eq are from transportation, and only 65 tonnes of CO2-eq are contributed by technologies (due to the carbon offset). The supply chain design for this problem is presented in Figure 4.7(b). In the design, the total technology capacity is able to process all livestock manure in the study region, SO that the GHG emission from manure is minimized. The placement of those technologies is configured SO that the overall transportation activities are reduced. There are 62 technologies installed in total, with capacities ranging from 8000 to 58500 metric tonnes of raw manure per year. The average distance of transporting raw manure (2.06 km) is longer than digestate (0.72 km) because the digestate is diluted during sand recovery.
Figure 4.7: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem Cost - * and (b) GHG - *

The AD system produces renewable energy and brings carbon credits from the displacement of grid electricity, and the covered storage can minimize CH4 emissions after the digestion process. The an- nual system cost increases to 19 million USD/year, where 50.5% of the cost come from operating technologies, 24.9% is from capital investment, and the rest is transportation

cost. In order to reduce the GHG emissions, transportation also needs to be minimized and it causes high net nutrient release since it neglects the nutrient conditions. For this problem, 172 tonnes of CO2-eq are from transportation, and only 65 tonnes of CO2-eq are contributed by technologies (due to the carbon offset). The supply chain design for this problem is presented in Figure 4.7(b). In the design, the total technology capacity is able to process all livestock manure in the study region, SO that the GHG emission from manure is minimized. The placement of those technologies is configured SO that the overall transportation activities are reduced. There are 62 technologies installed in total, with capacities ranging from 8000 to 58500 metric tonnes of raw manure per year. The average distance of transporting raw manure (2.06 km) is longer than digestate (0.72 km) because the digestate is diluted during sand recovery.
Figure 4.7: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem Cost - * and (b) GHG - *

For both of the previously discussed objectives, there is no degeneracy found in the solution (i.e., all problems in the same row of Table 4.3 obtain the same solution). This is not the case for the other five objectives. As an example, when minimizing NH3, as long as all the manure in the system is processed by the technology that has least NH3 emissions (i.e., technology 7, storage with coverage) and then applied by injection, then

the overall supply chain system presents the minimum NH3 emissions (494 tonne of NH3 per year). The NH3 emissions in transportation activity is negligible, therefore, the placement of those technologies can be arbitrary. As a result, the system has additional degrees of freedom to adjust other objectives while keeping the minimum NH3 emissions, by tuning technology placement and transportation activities. Results of two problems, NH3 - GHG and NH3 - N are presented in Figure 4.8(a) and 4.8(b). When we minimize GHG while keeping the minimum NH3 emissions, all farms are equipped with storage systems. This system only seeks to minimize GHG during transportation given that all manure is processed by technology 7 and GHG emissions during manure processing are already fixed. By installing a technology in each farm, the movement of raw manure is avoided, and the transportation of stored manure can be minimized. Therefore, the average transportation distances of raw manure and stored manure are o and 0.98 km, re- spectively (same as problem Cost - *). Alternatively, if net N is selected as the secondary objective, the system will first process all the manure, then activate transportation activi- ties to move processed (stored) manure streams according to the estimated N demand in cropland to balance it. The average transportation distances are 12.89 km and 13.28 km for raw manure and stored manure, respectively.
Figure 4.8: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem NH3 - GHG and (b) NH3 - N

The minimum DFF value is achieved when all the livestock manure is collected and then processed by technology 4 (sand recovery, AD, and storage without cover). The movement of materials is also reduced given that transportation contributes to DFF. The DFF minimization problems show similar behavior to the GHG ones, which conflict with cost, NH3 and nutrients. The main difference is that, for DFF the selected technology has an uncovered storage. The coverage does not impact DFF during the manure processing, but without the cover, more mass is emitted to the gas atmosphere and the weight of digestate, as well as its transportation cost, can be slightly reduced. Most problems in row 4 that minimizes DFF in the pay-off matrix solve to very similar designs, which means that although there are extra degrees of freedom to adjust secondary objectives, the system is already very constrained. Two of the supply chain designs are displayed in Figure 4.9(a) and 4.9(b), corresponding to problem DFF - Cost and DFF - N, respectively. As can be observed, except from different technology sizing, there are only some subtle differences in transportation of those two designs (highlighted by blue circles).
Figure 4.9: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem DFF - Cost and (b) DFF - N, where blue circles on the right highlights the differences between the two similar designs
The last three rows in the pay-off matrix represent system performance when net nutrient (N, P, K) releases are considered as the primary objective functions. We can

see that for the study region, only P can be perfectly balanced by applying solid-liquid separation technologies (potentially in combination AD and covered storage) and proper transportation management. Once the system achieves P balance, it can use its remaining resources to improve performance of other objectives (i.e., the solution of problem P - * has degeneracy). This can be inferred from row 6 of the pay-off matrix, where all objective function values are relatively close to their corresponding optimal values. The results of two problems, P - Cost and P - GHG are given below as an example in Figure 4.10(a) and 4.10(b), respectively. Both of them use solid-liquid separation technologies to produce solid products, which can be sold to the external customer to drive P outside of the study region. Transportation distance of raw livestock manure and applicable streams (liquid manure, digestate) is increased from previous cases that move P from the west area (with P surplus) to the east area (with P deficiency) to better balance nutrient. For problem P - Cost that focuses on cost, the system tends to install cheaper technologies (ony SLS) and transport extra solid streams to far croplands to reduce transportation cost. The average transportation distances for raw manure, solid products, and liquid products are 2.32 km, 18.13km, and 1.16 km, respectively. While for problem P - GHG, the GHG-friendly technologies, AD, will be installed more frequently. Similarly, for problem P - NH3, storage systems (with storage cover) are installed to reduce NH3 emissions. For problem P - N and P - K, the transportation activities are adjusted in order to better match the nutrient needs in croplands. Because some of these problems do not consider overall costs, there can be irrational economic decisions (e.g., loops in transportation routes and unnecessary technology installations).

For the other two nutrient objectives, N and K, the minimum net release values are 149 and 1400 tonnes per year, respectively. The general strategy is the same, i.e., to use SLS to separate manure and transport products based on nutrient need in croplands. While compared to P, the system now has more limited capability to tackle other non-nutrient targets, as can be seen in row 5 and 7 of the pay-off matrix which minimize net N and K, respectively. The results of two problems, N - Cost and N - GHG are given below

as an example in Figure 4.11(a) and 4.11(b), respectively. We can see the two problems are different in terms of technology placement and transportation activities, but not in technology types. The problem N - Cost has larger technology sizes due to economics of scale, while N - GHG has more distributed facilities in order to reduce GHG emissions in transportation.

Figure 4.10: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem P - Cost and (b) P - GHG, where pink dots represent pure SLS technology, grey dots represent AD technology with coverage, dark green lines are transportation flows of solid fraction of livestock raw ma- nure, purple lines are for liquid fraction of livestock manure, light green lines are for solid fraction of digestate, and magenta lines are for liquid fraction of digestate
7: Table 4.3 shows the pay-off matrix after solving all problems in Scenario 1. In the pay-off matrix, the utopia point consists of diagonal elements (highlighted by asterisk*), and the approximated Nadir point consists of maximum values of each column (highlighted by underscore). M1 - M2 denotes the problem with metric M1 as the primary objective and metric M2 as the secondary objective. For example, Cost - GHG represents the problem which keeps cost at minimum value and then minimizes GHG emissions (row 1 column 2). Specifically, Cost - Cost represent the problem with Cost as the only objective. In addition, we use the notation M1 - * to represent the set of problems whose primary objective is metric M1.

Table 4.3: Pay-off matrix and utopia point of Scenario 1

We note that the pay-off matrix and utopia point can provide useful insights to decision-makers, especially regarding the limiting behavior of each metric and the global conflicts between a pair of objectives. Generally, we can find that cost conflicts with all other environmental objectives. It is not profitable to reduce any of the analyzed environmental impacts in this study with traditional technologies. Within those environ- mental objectives, GHG shows strong conflicts with nutrients (especially N and K), but a weak conflict with DFF. Similarly, DFF shows conflicts with nutrient and costs, but it
has stronger conflicts with NH3. The three nutrient objectives, N, P, and K, show a weak conflict with one another, showing potential for simultaneous improvement.

When only the total cost is minimized (problem Cost - *), the supply chain is purely economic-driven and there is no technology installed. This is because the current market prices of electricity and nutrient products cannot cover the processing and investment cost. All farms send the livestock manure directly to the nearest croplands for application in order to reduce the transportation cost. Therefore, the 10 million USD/year is a baseline cost involving manure collection, transport, and application in the supply chain. At the minimum cost, the best achievable values of other objectives are listed in the first row of the pay-off matrix. GHG emissions reach 19389 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year, most of which is contributed by the manure collection and application process (19300 tonnes) and only a small fraction (89 tonnes) by the transportation process. The net nutrient release is also high because the farms transport manure to nearest land regardless of its nutrient condition, which can further intensify nutrient pollution in some areas. The average transportation distance of raw manure is 0.98 km. We note that all problems in row 1 lead to the same solution. The corresponding supply chain design is presented in Figure 4.7(a).

When the system minimizes GHG emissions (problem GHG - *), the minimum pos- sible value is 236 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year, which is order of magnitude smaller compared to the value when minimizing cost. This minimum value can be only achieved when the system reduces GHG emissions from livestock manure processing and trans- portation activity simultaneously. In the supply chain, all of the livestock manure is col- lected and processed by technology 9 (sand recovery, AD, storage with coverage), which is the most carbon-friendly technology from the LCA perspective. The AD system produces renewable energy and brings carbon credits from the displacement of grid electricity, and the covered storage can minimize CH4 emissions after the digestion process. The an- nual system cost increases to 19 million USD/year, where 50.5% of the cost come from operating technologies, 24.9% is from capital investment, and the rest is transportation

cost. In order to reduce the GHG emissions, transportation also needs to be minimized and it causes high net nutrient release since it neglects the nutrient conditions. For this problem, 172 tonnes of CO2-eq are from transportation, and only 65 tonnes of CO2-eq are contributed by technologies (due to the carbon offset). The supply chain design for this problem is presented in Figure 4.7(b). In the design, the total technology capacity is able to process all livestock manure in the study region, SO that the GHG emission from manure is minimized. The placement of those technologies is configured SO that the overall transportation activities are reduced. There are 62 technologies installed in total, with capacities ranging from 8000 to 58500 metric tonnes of raw manure per year. The average distance of transporting raw manure (2.06 km) is longer than digestate (0.72 km) because the digestate is diluted during sand recovery.
Figure 4.7: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem Cost - * and (b) GHG - *

At the minimum cost, the best achievable values of other objectives are listed in the first row of the pay-off matrix. GHG emissions reach 19389 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year, most of which is contributed by the manure collection and application process (19300 tonnes) and only a small fraction (89 tonnes) by the transportation process. The net nutrient release is also high because the farms transport manure to nearest land regardless of its nutrient condition, which can further intensify nutrient pollution in some areas. The average transportation distance of raw manure is 0.98 km. We note that all problems in row 1 lead to the same solution. The corresponding supply chain design is presented in Figure 4.7(a).

When the system minimizes GHG emissions (problem GHG - *), the minimum pos- sible value is 236 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year, which is order of magnitude smaller compared to the value when minimizing cost. This minimum value can be only achieved when the system reduces GHG emissions from livestock manure processing and trans- portation activity simultaneously. In the supply chain, all of the livestock manure is col- lected and processed by technology 9 (sand recovery, AD, storage with coverage), which is the most carbon-friendly technology from the LCA perspective. The AD system produces renewable energy and brings carbon credits from the displacement of grid electricity, and the covered storage can minimize CH4 emissions after the digestion process. The an- nual system cost increases to 19 million USD/year, where 50.5% of the cost come from operating technologies, 24.9% is from capital investment, and the rest is transportation

cost. In order to reduce the GHG emissions, transportation also needs to be minimized and it causes high net nutrient release since it neglects the nutrient conditions. For this problem, 172 tonnes of CO2-eq are from transportation, and only 65 tonnes of CO2-eq are contributed by technologies (due to the carbon offset). The supply chain design for this problem is presented in Figure 4.7(b). In the design, the total technology capacity is able to process all livestock manure in the study region, SO that the GHG emission from manure is minimized. The placement of those technologies is configured SO that the overall transportation activities are reduced. There are 62 technologies installed in total, with capacities ranging from 8000 to 58500 metric tonnes of raw manure per year. The average distance of transporting raw manure (2.06 km) is longer than digestate (0.72 km) because the digestate is diluted during sand recovery.
Figure 4.7: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem Cost - * and (b) GHG - *

For both of the previously discussed objectives, there is no degeneracy found in the solution (i.e., all problems in the same row of Table 4.3 obtain the same solution). This is not the case for the other five objectives. As an example, when minimizing NH3, as long as all the manure in the system is processed by the technology that has least NH3 emissions (i.e., technology 7, storage with coverage) and then applied by injection, then

the overall supply chain system presents the minimum NH3 emissions (494 tonne of NH3 per year). The NH3 emissions in transportation activity is negligible, therefore, the placement of those technologies can be arbitrary. As a result, the system has additional degrees of freedom to adjust other objectives while keeping the minimum NH3 emissions, by tuning technology placement and transportation activities. Results of two problems, NH3 - GHG and NH3 - N are presented in Figure 4.8(a) and 4.8(b). When we minimize GHG while keeping the minimum NH3 emissions, all farms are equipped with storage systems. This system only seeks to minimize GHG during transportation given that all manure is processed by technology 7 and GHG emissions during manure processing are already fixed. By installing a technology in each farm, the movement of raw manure is avoided, and the transportation of stored manure can be minimized. Therefore, the average transportation distances of raw manure and stored manure are o and 0.98 km, re- spectively (same as problem Cost - *). Alternatively, if net N is selected as the secondary objective, the system will first process all the manure, then activate transportation activi- ties to move processed (stored) manure streams according to the estimated N demand in cropland to balance it. The average transportation distances are 12.89 km and 13.28 km for raw manure and stored manure, respectively.
Figure 4.8: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem NH3 - GHG and (b) NH3 - N

The minimum DFF value is achieved when all the livestock manure is collected and then processed by technology 4 (sand recovery, AD, and storage without cover). The movement of materials is also reduced given that transportation contributes to DFF. The DFF minimization problems show similar behavior to the GHG ones, which conflict with cost, NH3 and nutrients. The main difference is that, for DFF the selected technology has an uncovered storage. The coverage does not impact DFF during the manure processing, but without the cover, more mass is emitted to the gas atmosphere and the weight of digestate, as well as its transportation cost, can be slightly reduced. Most problems in row 4 that minimizes DFF in the pay-off matrix solve to very similar designs, which means that although there are extra degrees of freedom to adjust secondary objectives, the system is already very constrained. Two of the supply chain designs are displayed in Figure 4.9(a) and 4.9(b), corresponding to problem DFF - Cost and DFF - N, respectively. As can be observed, except from different technology sizing, there are only some subtle differences in transportation of those two designs (highlighted by blue circles).
Figure 4.9: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem DFF - Cost and (b) DFF - N, where blue circles on the right highlights the differences between the two similar designs
The last three rows in the pay-off matrix represent system performance when net nutrient (N, P, K) releases are considered as the primary objective functions. We can

see that for the study region, only P can be perfectly balanced by applying solid-liquid separation technologies (potentially in combination AD and covered storage) and proper transportation management. Once the system achieves P balance, it can use its remaining resources to improve performance of other objectives (i.e., the solution of problem P - * has degeneracy). This can be inferred from row 6 of the pay-off matrix, where all objective function values are relatively close to their corresponding optimal values. The results of two problems, P - Cost and P - GHG are given below as an example in Figure 4.10(a) and 4.10(b), respectively. Both of them use solid-liquid separation technologies to produce solid products, which can be sold to the external customer to drive P outside of the study region. Transportation distance of raw livestock manure and applicable streams (liquid manure, digestate) is increased from previous cases that move P from the west area (with P surplus) to the east area (with P deficiency) to better balance nutrient. For problem P - Cost that focuses on cost, the system tends to install cheaper technologies (ony SLS) and transport extra solid streams to far croplands to reduce transportation cost. The average transportation distances for raw manure, solid products, and liquid products are 2.32 km, 18.13km, and 1.16 km, respectively. While for problem P - GHG, the GHG-friendly technologies, AD, will be installed more frequently. Similarly, for problem P - NH3, storage systems (with storage cover) are installed to reduce NH3 emissions. For problem P - N and P - K, the transportation activities are adjusted in order to better match the nutrient needs in croplands. Because some of these problems do not consider overall costs, there can be irrational economic decisions (e.g., loops in transportation routes and unnecessary technology installations).

For the other two nutrient objectives, N and K, the minimum net release values are 149 and 1400 tonnes per year, respectively. The general strategy is the same, i.e., to use SLS to separate manure and transport products based on nutrient need in croplands. While compared to P, the system now has more limited capability to tackle other non-nutrient targets, as can be seen in row 5 and 7 of the pay-off matrix which minimize net N and K, respectively. The results of two problems, N - Cost and N - GHG are given below

as an example in Figure 4.11(a) and 4.11(b), respectively. We can see the two problems are different in terms of technology placement and transportation activities, but not in technology types. The problem N - Cost has larger technology sizes due to economics of scale, while N - GHG has more distributed facilities in order to reduce GHG emissions in transportation.

Figure 4.10: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem P - Cost and (b) P - GHG, where pink dots represent pure SLS technology, grey dots represent AD technology with coverage, dark green lines are transportation flows of solid fraction of livestock raw ma- nure, purple lines are for liquid fraction of livestock manure, light green lines are for solid fraction of digestate, and magenta lines are for liquid fraction of digestate
10: Table 4.3 shows the pay-off matrix after solving all problems in Scenario 1. In the pay-off matrix, the utopia point consists of diagonal elements (highlighted by asterisk*), and the approximated Nadir point consists of maximum values of each column (highlighted by underscore). M1 - M2 denotes the problem with metric M1 as the primary objective and metric M2 as the secondary objective. For example, Cost - GHG represents the problem which keeps cost at minimum value and then minimizes GHG emissions (row 1 column 2). Specifically, Cost - Cost represent the problem with Cost as the only objective. In addition, we use the notation M1 - * to represent the set of problems whose primary objective is metric M1.

We note that the pay-off matrix and utopia point can provide useful insights to decision-makers, especially regarding the limiting behavior of each metric and the global conflicts between a pair of objectives. Generally, we can find that cost conflicts with all other environmental objectives. It is not profitable to reduce any of the analyzed environmental impacts in this study with traditional technologies. Within those environ- mental objectives, GHG shows strong conflicts with nutrients (especially N and K), but a weak conflict with DFF. Similarly, DFF shows conflicts with nutrient and costs, but it
has stronger conflicts with NH3. The three nutrient objectives, N, P, and K, show a weak conflict with one another, showing potential for simultaneous improvement.

We note that the pay-off matrix and utopia point can provide useful insights to decision-makers, especially regarding the limiting behavior of each metric and the global conflicts between a pair of objectives. Generally, we can find that cost conflicts with all other environmental objectives. It is not profitable to reduce any of the analyzed environmental impacts in this study with traditional technologies. Within those environ- mental objectives, GHG shows strong conflicts with nutrients (especially N and K), but a weak conflict with DFF. Similarly, DFF shows conflicts with nutrient and costs, but it
has stronger conflicts with NH3. The three nutrient objectives, N, P, and K, show a weak conflict with one another, showing potential for simultaneous improvement.

When only the total cost is minimized (problem Cost - *), the supply chain is purely economic-driven and there is no technology installed. This is because the current market prices of electricity and nutrient products cannot cover the processing and investment cost. All farms send the livestock manure directly to the nearest croplands for application in order to reduce the transportation cost. Therefore, the 10 million USD/year is a baseline cost involving manure collection, transport, and application in the supply chain. At the minimum cost, the best achievable values of other objectives are listed in the first row of the pay-off matrix. GHG emissions reach 19389 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year, most of which is contributed by the manure collection and application process (19300 tonnes) and only a small fraction (89 tonnes) by the transportation process. The net nutrient release is also high because the farms transport manure to nearest land regardless of its nutrient condition, which can further intensify nutrient pollution in some areas. The average transportation distance of raw manure is 0.98 km. We note that all problems in row 1 lead to the same solution. The corresponding supply chain design is presented in Figure 4.7(a).

When the system minimizes GHG emissions (problem GHG - *), the minimum pos- sible value is 236 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year, which is order of magnitude smaller compared to the value when minimizing cost. This minimum value can be only achieved when the system reduces GHG emissions from livestock manure processing and trans- portation activity simultaneously. In the supply chain, all of the livestock manure is col- lected and processed by technology 9 (sand recovery, AD, storage with coverage), which is the most carbon-friendly technology from the LCA perspective. The AD system produces renewable energy and brings carbon credits from the displacement of grid electricity, and the covered storage can minimize CH4 emissions after the digestion process. The an- nual system cost increases to 19 million USD/year, where 50.5% of the cost come from operating technologies, 24.9% is from capital investment, and the rest is transportation

cost. In order to reduce the GHG emissions, transportation also needs to be minimized and it causes high net nutrient release since it neglects the nutrient conditions. For this problem, 172 tonnes of CO2-eq are from transportation, and only 65 tonnes of CO2-eq are contributed by technologies (due to the carbon offset). The supply chain design for this problem is presented in Figure 4.7(b). In the design, the total technology capacity is able to process all livestock manure in the study region, SO that the GHG emission from manure is minimized. The placement of those technologies is configured SO that the overall transportation activities are reduced. There are 62 technologies installed in total, with capacities ranging from 8000 to 58500 metric tonnes of raw manure per year. The average distance of transporting raw manure (2.06 km) is longer than digestate (0.72 km) because the digestate is diluted during sand recovery.
Figure 4.7: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem Cost - * and (b) GHG - *

For both of the previously discussed objectives, there is no degeneracy found in the solution (i.e., all problems in the same row of Table 4.3 obtain the same solution). This is not the case for the other five objectives. As an example, when minimizing NH3, as long as all the manure in the system is processed by the technology that has least NH3 emissions (i.e., technology 7, storage with coverage) and then applied by injection, then

the overall supply chain system presents the minimum NH3 emissions (494 tonne of NH3 per year). The NH3 emissions in transportation activity is negligible, therefore, the placement of those technologies can be arbitrary. As a result, the system has additional degrees of freedom to adjust other objectives while keeping the minimum NH3 emissions, by tuning technology placement and transportation activities. Results of two problems, NH3 - GHG and NH3 - N are presented in Figure 4.8(a) and 4.8(b). When we minimize GHG while keeping the minimum NH3 emissions, all farms are equipped with storage systems. This system only seeks to minimize GHG during transportation given that all manure is processed by technology 7 and GHG emissions during manure processing are already fixed. By installing a technology in each farm, the movement of raw manure is avoided, and the transportation of stored manure can be minimized. Therefore, the average transportation distances of raw manure and stored manure are o and 0.98 km, re- spectively (same as problem Cost - *). Alternatively, if net N is selected as the secondary objective, the system will first process all the manure, then activate transportation activi- ties to move processed (stored) manure streams according to the estimated N demand in cropland to balance it. The average transportation distances are 12.89 km and 13.28 km for raw manure and stored manure, respectively.
Figure 4.8: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem NH3 - GHG and (b) NH3 - N

The minimum DFF value is achieved when all the livestock manure is collected and then processed by technology 4 (sand recovery, AD, and storage without cover). The movement of materials is also reduced given that transportation contributes to DFF. The DFF minimization problems show similar behavior to the GHG ones, which conflict with cost, NH3 and nutrients. The main difference is that, for DFF the selected technology has an uncovered storage. The coverage does not impact DFF during the manure processing, but without the cover, more mass is emitted to the gas atmosphere and the weight of digestate, as well as its transportation cost, can be slightly reduced. Most problems in row 4 that minimizes DFF in the pay-off matrix solve to very similar designs, which means that although there are extra degrees of freedom to adjust secondary objectives, the system is already very constrained. Two of the supply chain designs are displayed in Figure 4.9(a) and 4.9(b), corresponding to problem DFF - Cost and DFF - N, respectively. As can be observed, except from different technology sizing, there are only some subtle differences in transportation of those two designs (highlighted by blue circles).
Figure 4.9: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem DFF - Cost and (b) DFF - N, where blue circles on the right highlights the differences between the two similar designs
The last three rows in the pay-off matrix represent system performance when net nutrient (N, P, K) releases are considered as the primary objective functions. We can

see that for the study region, only P can be perfectly balanced by applying solid-liquid separation technologies (potentially in combination AD and covered storage) and proper transportation management. Once the system achieves P balance, it can use its remaining resources to improve performance of other objectives (i.e., the solution of problem P - * has degeneracy). This can be inferred from row 6 of the pay-off matrix, where all objective function values are relatively close to their corresponding optimal values. The results of two problems, P - Cost and P - GHG are given below as an example in Figure 4.10(a) and 4.10(b), respectively. Both of them use solid-liquid separation technologies to produce solid products, which can be sold to the external customer to drive P outside of the study region. Transportation distance of raw livestock manure and applicable streams (liquid manure, digestate) is increased from previous cases that move P from the west area (with P surplus) to the east area (with P deficiency) to better balance nutrient. For problem P - Cost that focuses on cost, the system tends to install cheaper technologies (ony SLS) and transport extra solid streams to far croplands to reduce transportation cost. The average transportation distances for raw manure, solid products, and liquid products are 2.32 km, 18.13km, and 1.16 km, respectively. While for problem P - GHG, the GHG-friendly technologies, AD, will be installed more frequently. Similarly, for problem P - NH3, storage systems (with storage cover) are installed to reduce NH3 emissions. For problem P - N and P - K, the transportation activities are adjusted in order to better match the nutrient needs in croplands. Because some of these problems do not consider overall costs, there can be irrational economic decisions (e.g., loops in transportation routes and unnecessary technology installations).

For the other two nutrient objectives, N and K, the minimum net release values are 149 and 1400 tonnes per year, respectively. The general strategy is the same, i.e., to use SLS to separate manure and transport products based on nutrient need in croplands. While compared to P, the system now has more limited capability to tackle other non-nutrient targets, as can be seen in row 5 and 7 of the pay-off matrix which minimize net N and K, respectively. The results of two problems, N - Cost and N - GHG are given below

as an example in Figure 4.11(a) and 4.11(b), respectively. We can see the two problems are different in terms of technology placement and transportation activities, but not in technology types. The problem N - Cost has larger technology sizes due to economics of scale, while N - GHG has more distributed facilities in order to reduce GHG emissions in transportation.

Figure 4.10: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem P - Cost and (b) P - GHG, where pink dots represent pure SLS technology, grey dots represent AD technology with coverage, dark green lines are transportation flows of solid fraction of livestock raw ma- nure, purple lines are for liquid fraction of livestock manure, light green lines are for solid fraction of digestate, and magenta lines are for liquid fraction of digestate
13: Table 4.3 shows the pay-off matrix after solving all problems in Scenario 1. In the pay-off matrix, the utopia point consists of diagonal elements (highlighted by asterisk*), and the approximated Nadir point consists of maximum values of each column (highlighted by underscore). M1 - M2 denotes the problem with metric M1 as the primary objective and metric M2 as the secondary objective. For example, Cost - GHG represents the problem which keeps cost at minimum value and then minimizes GHG emissions (row 1 column 2). Specifically, Cost - Cost represent the problem with Cost as the only objective. In addition, we use the notation M1 - * to represent the set of problems whose primary objective is metric M1.
