Figure 4.10: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem P - Cost and (b) P - GHG, where pink dots represent pure SLS technology, grey dots represent AD technology with coverage, dark green lines are transportation flows of solid fraction of livestock raw ma- nure, purple lines are for liquid fraction of livestock manure, light green lines are for solid fraction of digestate, and magenta lines are for liquid fraction of digestate
15: As shown by the previous analysis, the system can make irrational or trivial decisions when only one or two objectives are considered, and other metrics can be left out. For example, the minimization of GHG might lead to nutrient pollution. An optimal compro- mise solution (OCS) is established when all objectives proposed by the decision-maker are treated with self-adapted weights. The OCS for Scenario 1 is presented in Table 4.4. Here minimum and maximum values from the payoff matrix are listed, OCS values represent
Figure 4.11: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem N - Cost (b) and N - GHG

the performance of the corresponding objective in the original units, and the scaled values are calculated from equation (4.2.23), which have a unit of 1 and represent the relative distance along the certain direction.

Metric|Cost|GHG|NH3|DFF|Net N|Net P|Net K Unit|million USD per year|tonne CO2-eq per year|tonne NH3-eq per year|GJ per year|tonne N per year|tonne P per year|tonne K per year min value|10|236|494|-706|149|o|1400 max value|22|45669|2201|39|2538|285|2146 OCS value|22|3776|1322|-670|677|29|1449 scaled value|1.01|0.08|0.49|0.05|0.22|0.10|0.07

The overall cost obtained in OCS is 22 million USD per year, which is almost the maximum value in the pay-off matrix. The pay-off matrix is an approximated approach for the Nadir point estimation, and the obtained value does not necessarily match the real Nadir point projected from the Pareto front. This means that by increasing the cost, the system can improve all other environmental objectives simultaneously and therefore, can properly resolve the conflicts among objectives. All the environmental metrics are
Figure 4.12: OCS designs for Scenario 1, where left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure, and right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.

considerably close to the corresponding utopia value. The GHG emission value is 3776 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year. Although it is larger than the utopia value (236), it is also an order of magnitude smaller than several other cases (e.g., subproblems Cost - GHG, Cost - NH3, and Cost - N). The DFF value is -670 GJ per year, which is only 5% greater than the utopia value. A negative value indicates that the system produces renewable energy that can replace fossil based grid electricity. The net nutrient release values are 677, 29, and 1449 metric tonne per year for N, P, and K, respectively. All of them are close to the utopia values, but none of the nutrient is perfectly balanced.

In the scaled objective space, the distance between OCS and the utopia point is 2.01 (with optimality gap of 12% after 35 hours of solving). Half of the distance is contributed by the cost (1.01), and the rest 6 environmental objectives contribute the remaining dis- tances. This again shows that for the OCS, the cost needs to be sufficiently large SO that the environmental impact can be reduced and then achieves overall smallest distance to the utopia point. Specifically for GHG, DFF, P, and K, all distances contributed by them are less than 0.1.
The supply chain design of OCS is shown in Figure 4.12. To reduce various envi- ronmental impacts, the system tends to install different types of processing technologies,

including AD, SLS, and covered storages. There are 31 farms with AD and SLS installed (technology 5, blue dots) and 19 farms with AD and covered storage installed (technol- ogy 9, grey dots). There is 1 farm with AD installed only (brown dot) and 1 farm with covered storage only (purple dot). Most technologies are installed at medium or large farms, with an average farm size of 590 AU. There are also 23 farms directly sending the raw manure to nearby lands, most of which are small farms and the unprocessed manure only occupies 2.5% of all manure in the study region. The average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, and liquid products are 1.93 km, 2.12 km, 15.95 km, and 1.60 km, respectively. Additionally, there is a mix of surface application and in- jection application used in croplands. Around 41.4% of manure in the system is applied by injection. Due to the mineralization of organic N after the digestion process, using a cover during storage is not enough to effectively prevent NH3 emissions and needs to be coupled with injection as the method for land application. Manure injection can reduce the NH3 emissions from land application by 95%, and reduce the overall NH3 emissions by 45%. However, injection can increase GHG emissions by around 10% as it created anaerobic conditions, which promote the formation of N2O. Therefore, the mixed land application method is an outcome of coordinating the conflict between GHG and NH3 emissions.

To summarize, in Scenario 1, most cow manure is processed by technologies, where 96.9% is processed by AD, 52% is processed by SLS, and 45.4% is stored with cover- age. Among those technologies, AD can create benefits in GHG and DFF reduction, SLS can further provide flexibility in product transportation and reduce nutrient losses, and covered storage systems are preferred for decreasing NH3 and DFF. The average technol- ogy sizes for them are 600 AU, 530 AU, and 680 AU, respectively. These technologies contributes 65.5% to the system cost and 89.4% to the overall GHG emissions, and trans- portation contributes 34.5% and 10.6% to the two metrics, respectively.

Among those technologies, AD can create benefits in GHG and DFF reduction, SLS can further provide flexibility in product transportation and reduce nutrient losses, and covered storage systems are preferred for decreasing NH3 and DFF. The average technol- ogy sizes for them are 600 AU, 530 AU, and 680 AU, respectively. These technologies contributes 65.5% to the system cost and 89.4% to the overall GHG emissions, and trans- portation contributes 34.5% and 10.6% to the two metrics, respectively.
21: As shown by the previous analysis, the system can make irrational or trivial decisions when only one or two objectives are considered, and other metrics can be left out. For example, the minimization of GHG might lead to nutrient pollution. An optimal compro- mise solution (OCS) is established when all objectives proposed by the decision-maker are treated with self-adapted weights. The OCS for Scenario 1 is presented in Table 4.4. Here minimum and maximum values from the payoff matrix are listed, OCS values represent
Figure 4.11: Supply chain designs for Scenario 1 (a) problem N - Cost (b) and N - GHG

the performance of the corresponding objective in the original units, and the scaled values are calculated from equation (4.2.23), which have a unit of 1 and represent the relative distance along the certain direction.

Metric|Cost|GHG|NH3|DFF|Net N|Net P|Net K Unit|million USD per year|tonne CO2-eq per year|tonne NH3-eq per year|GJ per year|tonne N per year|tonne P per year|tonne K per year min value|10|236|494|-706|149|o|1400 max value|22|45669|2201|39|2538|285|2146 OCS value|22|3776|1322|-670|677|29|1449 scaled value|1.01|0.08|0.49|0.05|0.22|0.10|0.07

The overall cost obtained in OCS is 22 million USD per year, which is almost the maximum value in the pay-off matrix. The pay-off matrix is an approximated approach for the Nadir point estimation, and the obtained value does not necessarily match the real Nadir point projected from the Pareto front. This means that by increasing the cost, the system can improve all other environmental objectives simultaneously and therefore, can properly resolve the conflicts among objectives. All the environmental metrics are
Figure 4.12: OCS designs for Scenario 1, where left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure, and right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.

considerably close to the corresponding utopia value. The GHG emission value is 3776 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year. Although it is larger than the utopia value (236), it is also an order of magnitude smaller than several other cases (e.g., subproblems Cost - GHG, Cost - NH3, and Cost - N). The DFF value is -670 GJ per year, which is only 5% greater than the utopia value. A negative value indicates that the system produces renewable energy that can replace fossil based grid electricity. The net nutrient release values are 677, 29, and 1449 metric tonne per year for N, P, and K, respectively. All of them are close to the utopia values, but none of the nutrient is perfectly balanced.

In the scaled objective space, the distance between OCS and the utopia point is 2.01 (with optimality gap of 12% after 35 hours of solving). Half of the distance is contributed by the cost (1.01), and the rest 6 environmental objectives contribute the remaining dis- tances. This again shows that for the OCS, the cost needs to be sufficiently large SO that the environmental impact can be reduced and then achieves overall smallest distance to the utopia point. Specifically for GHG, DFF, P, and K, all distances contributed by them are less than 0.1.
The supply chain design of OCS is shown in Figure 4.12. To reduce various envi- ronmental impacts, the system tends to install different types of processing technologies,

including AD, SLS, and covered storages. There are 31 farms with AD and SLS installed (technology 5, blue dots) and 19 farms with AD and covered storage installed (technol- ogy 9, grey dots). There is 1 farm with AD installed only (brown dot) and 1 farm with covered storage only (purple dot). Most technologies are installed at medium or large farms, with an average farm size of 590 AU. There are also 23 farms directly sending the raw manure to nearby lands, most of which are small farms and the unprocessed manure only occupies 2.5% of all manure in the study region. The average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, and liquid products are 1.93 km, 2.12 km, 15.95 km, and 1.60 km, respectively. Additionally, there is a mix of surface application and in- jection application used in croplands. Around 41.4% of manure in the system is applied by injection. Due to the mineralization of organic N after the digestion process, using a cover during storage is not enough to effectively prevent NH3 emissions and needs to be coupled with injection as the method for land application. Manure injection can reduce the NH3 emissions from land application by 95%, and reduce the overall NH3 emissions by 45%. However, injection can increase GHG emissions by around 10% as it created anaerobic conditions, which promote the formation of N2O. Therefore, the mixed land application method is an outcome of coordinating the conflict between GHG and NH3 emissions.

To summarize, in Scenario 1, most cow manure is processed by technologies, where 96.9% is processed by AD, 52% is processed by SLS, and 45.4% is stored with cover- age. Among those technologies, AD can create benefits in GHG and DFF reduction, SLS can further provide flexibility in product transportation and reduce nutrient losses, and covered storage systems are preferred for decreasing NH3 and DFF. The average technol- ogy sizes for them are 600 AU, 530 AU, and 680 AU, respectively. These technologies contributes 65.5% to the system cost and 89.4% to the overall GHG emissions, and trans- portation contributes 34.5% and 10.6% to the two metrics, respectively.
22: In Scenario 2, the technology for treatment of manure to clean water (technology 11) is added into the system. It can provide high efficiency in nutrient separation, at the price of higher investment and operational costs. The centrifuge separator produces separated solids with higher nutrient content (around 30% more concentrated than screw press). The UFC and ROC are assumed to be used as liquid digestate in the supply chain, and can be directly applied on cropland. The UFC and ROC streams are typically more diluted in nutrient than liquid digestate, SO it is less economic to transport them for a long distance. Clean water is approximately one third of the initial digestate volume and can be directly discharged or consumed by animals reducing transportation costs. The pay-off matrix of Scenario 2 (Table 4.5) was estimated.

Metric|Cost|GHG|NH3|DFF|Net N|Net P|Net K Unit|million USD per year|tonne CO2-eq per year|tonne NH3-eq per year|GJ per year|tonne N per year|tonne P per year|tonne K per year (1) min Cost|10*|19389|2201|34|2297|262|2008 (2) min GHG|19|236*|1966|-705|2538|283|2146 (3) min NH3|12|37911|494|39|851|34|1537 (4) min DFF|21|537.4|2050|-706*|2087|274|1995 (5) min Net N|28|40669|2009|-163|3*|o|1182 (6) min Net P|13|800|577|-697|3|o*|1166 (7) min Net K|27|9318|1518|-474|54|o|1166*
Table 4.5: Pay-off matrix and utopia point of Scenario 2

The treatment of manure to clean water technology mainly influences they way nutri- ents are processed and does not interfere with other metrics during pay-off matrix gener- ation because the technologies in Scenario 1 are still selected. As a result, cost, GHG, NH3 and DFF remain similar as with Scenario 1. This also indicates that a specific technology reducing an environmental metric in Scenario 1 will also achieve such effect in Scenario 2. With that, even if we consider nutrient as a secondary objective, only transportation will be adjusted and the new technology will not be selected, resulting in similar values in the pay-off matrix. After the treatment of manure to clean water technology is installed,

N and K are still not balanced, but minimum nutrient release values are achieved due to higher nutrient concentration in the demanded solids, which increases the overall system cost by 28.9% and 43.5%, respectively. Introducing new technology only brings additional degrees of freedom to balance P, as it was already balanced in Scenario 1. Therefore, some of the problems corresponding to P minimization in Scenario 2 show better performance than Scenario 1. Both problem N - P and K - P are perfectly P balanced, and problems P - * also shows lower NH3 emissions and DFF consumption.

The results of problem N - Cost and N - GHG are shown in Figure 4.13. Both N - Cost and N - GHG install four large treatment of manure to clean water technologies which can handle 25.9% of all manure in the system, and the rest manure is processed by either AD and SLS or SLS only. The main difference between the two problems is the location of technologies and transportation activities, where problem N - Cost has less technologies (with larger capacities) to benefit from the economics of scale, while problem N - GHG has more distributed technologies, each with smaller capacity to reduce GHG during transportation. The design of P - Cost and P - GHG are given in Figure 4.14. Compared with N, they have simpler designs since P is more easily balanced. The prob- lem P - Cost installs SLS and treatment of manure to clean water technology to use the least cost to achieve P balance, where the treatment of manure to clean water technology replaced the AD and SLS pathway in Scenario 1. The problem P - GHG uses more AD units to gain GHG credits.

The OCS of Scenario 2 was estimated by repeating the decision making procedure described previously (Table 4.6). The cost of the OCS is increased by around 3 million USD/year compared with Scenario 1, mainly due to the installment of new technologies. The net N and K releases are reduced by 16.1% and 17.3%, respectively, and P can be perfectly balanced. On the other hand, the GHG and DFF values are increased slightly, mainly due to the decreased biogas production. Generally, the new technology makes nutrient recovery easier and endows the ability to achieve lower nutrient release. If we look at the scaled objective space, the new OCS shows a similar composition to Scenario 1,
Figure 4.13: Supply chain designs for Scenario 2 (a) problem N - Cost and (b) N - GHG
Figure 4.14: Supply chain designs for Scenario 2 (a) problem P - Cost and (b) P - GHG

where the cost contributes almost half of the total distance, and all environmental metrics are properly improved with increased investment. While some of the metrics have slightly worse performance, the new OCS moves toward ideal nutrient management, and it is also closer to the utopia point. The scaled distance to the new utopia point is 1.66 (with optimality gap of 14.9% after 35 hours of solving). If we use the minimum and maximum values in Scenario 1 for scaling, the distance is 1.73, which is decreased by 14.3%.

Metric|Cost|GHG|NH3|DFF|Net N|Net P|Net K Unit|million USD per year|tonne CO2-eq per year|tonne NH3-eq per year|GJ per year|tonne N per year|tonne P per year|tonne K per year min value|10|236|494|-706|3|o|1166 max value|28|40662|2278|39|2538|283|2146 OCS value|25|4935|1175|-657|563|o|1201 scaled value|0.83|0.12|0.40|0.07|0.22|o|0.33

The corresponding OCS supply chain design is shown in Figure 4.15. The system still tends to install different types of technologies to balance conflicts between environmental metrics. In total, 53 farms have AD installed and can process 99.2% of manure, 7 of which are accompanied by uncovered storage systems (technology 8), 33 farms have covered storage systems (technology 9), and 13 of which are equipped with SLS (technology 5). The average sizes of the three technologies are 350 AU, 510 AU, and 440 AU, respectively. There are 4 large clean water technologies (average size of 2050 AU) installed that can process 25.9% of manure in the region. Compared with Scenario 1, where 52% of manure is processed by AD and SLS, the new treatment of manure to clean water technology re- placed almost half of the nutrient recovery task. The total amount of unprocessed manure is also decreased from 2.5% to 0.8% under the new setting. The number of AD systems is increased in order to balance out the additional GHG emissions in transporting raw manure to centralized facilities. For this scenario, the average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, liquid products, and UFC/ROC are 2.50km, 1.62km, 11.83km, 0.98km, and 2.50km, respectively. For this scenario, the transportation, investment, and operational cost is increased by 12.6%, 14.4%, and 6.2%, respectively. The

increased technology cost is mainly introduced by the new technologies. The transporta- tion cost is increased because more manure in the system is processed, and also because the more concentrated solid is sold to external customers and the system needs to move diluted streams to balance nutrients.
Figure 4.15: OCS designs for Scenario 2, where left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure, and right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.

The cost of the OCS is increased by around 3 million USD/year compared with Scenario 1, mainly due to the installment of new technologies. The net N and K releases are reduced by 16.1% and 17.3%, respectively, and P can be perfectly balanced. On the other hand, the GHG and DFF values are increased slightly, mainly due to the decreased biogas production. Generally, the new technology makes nutrient recovery easier and endows the ability to achieve lower nutrient release. If we look at the scaled objective space, the new OCS shows a similar composition to Scenario 1,
Figure 4.13: Supply chain designs for Scenario 2 (a) problem N - Cost and (b) N - GHG
Figure 4.14: Supply chain designs for Scenario 2 (a) problem P - Cost and (b) P - GHG

where the cost contributes almost half of the total distance, and all environmental metrics are properly improved with increased investment. While some of the metrics have slightly worse performance, the new OCS moves toward ideal nutrient management, and it is also closer to the utopia point. The scaled distance to the new utopia point is 1.66 (with optimality gap of 14.9% after 35 hours of solving). If we use the minimum and maximum values in Scenario 1 for scaling, the distance is 1.73, which is decreased by 14.3%.

Metric|Cost|GHG|NH3|DFF|Net N|Net P|Net K Unit|million USD per year|tonne CO2-eq per year|tonne NH3-eq per year|GJ per year|tonne N per year|tonne P per year|tonne K per year min value|10|236|494|-706|3|o|1166 max value|28|40662|2278|39|2538|283|2146 OCS value|25|4935|1175|-657|563|o|1201 scaled value|0.83|0.12|0.40|0.07|0.22|o|0.33

The corresponding OCS supply chain design is shown in Figure 4.15. The system still tends to install different types of technologies to balance conflicts between environmental metrics. In total, 53 farms have AD installed and can process 99.2% of manure, 7 of which are accompanied by uncovered storage systems (technology 8), 33 farms have covered storage systems (technology 9), and 13 of which are equipped with SLS (technology 5). The average sizes of the three technologies are 350 AU, 510 AU, and 440 AU, respectively. There are 4 large clean water technologies (average size of 2050 AU) installed that can process 25.9% of manure in the region. Compared with Scenario 1, where 52% of manure is processed by AD and SLS, the new treatment of manure to clean water technology re- placed almost half of the nutrient recovery task. The total amount of unprocessed manure is also decreased from 2.5% to 0.8% under the new setting. The number of AD systems is increased in order to balance out the additional GHG emissions in transporting raw manure to centralized facilities. For this scenario, the average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, liquid products, and UFC/ROC are 2.50km, 1.62km, 11.83km, 0.98km, and 2.50km, respectively. For this scenario, the transportation, investment, and operational cost is increased by 12.6%, 14.4%, and 6.2%, respectively. The

increased technology cost is mainly introduced by the new technologies. The transporta- tion cost is increased because more manure in the system is processed, and also because the more concentrated solid is sold to external customers and the system needs to move diluted streams to balance nutrients.
Figure 4.15: OCS designs for Scenario 2, where left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure, and right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.
26: In Scenario 2, the technology for treatment of manure to clean water (technology 11) is added into the system. It can provide high efficiency in nutrient separation, at the price of higher investment and operational costs. The centrifuge separator produces separated solids with higher nutrient content (around 30% more concentrated than screw press). The UFC and ROC are assumed to be used as liquid digestate in the supply chain, and can be directly applied on cropland. The UFC and ROC streams are typically more diluted in nutrient than liquid digestate, SO it is less economic to transport them for a long distance. Clean water is approximately one third of the initial digestate volume and can be directly discharged or consumed by animals reducing transportation costs. The pay-off matrix of Scenario 2 (Table 4.5) was estimated.

The treatment of manure to clean water technology mainly influences they way nutri- ents are processed and does not interfere with other metrics during pay-off matrix gener- ation because the technologies in Scenario 1 are still selected. As a result, cost, GHG, NH3 and DFF remain similar as with Scenario 1. This also indicates that a specific technology reducing an environmental metric in Scenario 1 will also achieve such effect in Scenario 2. With that, even if we consider nutrient as a secondary objective, only transportation will be adjusted and the new technology will not be selected, resulting in similar values in the pay-off matrix. After the treatment of manure to clean water technology is installed,

N and K are still not balanced, but minimum nutrient release values are achieved due to higher nutrient concentration in the demanded solids, which increases the overall system cost by 28.9% and 43.5%, respectively. Introducing new technology only brings additional degrees of freedom to balance P, as it was already balanced in Scenario 1. Therefore, some of the problems corresponding to P minimization in Scenario 2 show better performance than Scenario 1. Both problem N - P and K - P are perfectly P balanced, and problems P - * also shows lower NH3 emissions and DFF consumption.

The results of problem N - Cost and N - GHG are shown in Figure 4.13. Both N - Cost and N - GHG install four large treatment of manure to clean water technologies which can handle 25.9% of all manure in the system, and the rest manure is processed by either AD and SLS or SLS only. The main difference between the two problems is the location of technologies and transportation activities, where problem N - Cost has less technologies (with larger capacities) to benefit from the economics of scale, while problem N - GHG has more distributed technologies, each with smaller capacity to reduce GHG during transportation. The design of P - Cost and P - GHG are given in Figure 4.14. Compared with N, they have simpler designs since P is more easily balanced. The prob- lem P - Cost installs SLS and treatment of manure to clean water technology to use the least cost to achieve P balance, where the treatment of manure to clean water technology replaced the AD and SLS pathway in Scenario 1. The problem P - GHG uses more AD units to gain GHG credits.

Generally, the new technology makes nutrient recovery easier and endows the ability to achieve lower nutrient release. If we look at the scaled objective space, the new OCS shows a similar composition to Scenario 1,

where the cost contributes almost half of the total distance, and all environmental metrics are properly improved with increased investment. There are 4 large clean water technologies (average size of 2050 AU) installed that can process 25.9% of manure in the region. Compared with Scenario 1, where 52% of manure is processed by AD and SLS, the new treatment of manure to clean water technology re- placed almost half of the nutrient recovery task. The total amount of unprocessed manure is also decreased from 2.5% to 0.8% under the new setting. The number of AD systems is increased in order to balance out the additional GHG emissions in transporting raw manure to centralized facilities. For this scenario, the average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, liquid products, and UFC/ROC are 2.50km, 1.62km, 11.83km, 0.98km, and 2.50km, respectively. For this scenario, the transportation, investment, and operational cost is increased by 12.6%, 14.4%, and 6.2%, respectively. The

increased technology cost is mainly introduced by the new technologies. The transporta- tion cost is increased because more manure in the system is processed, and also because the more concentrated solid is sold to external customers and the system needs to move diluted streams to balance nutrients.
Figure 4.15: OCS designs for Scenario 2, where left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure, and right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.
30: In Scenario 2, the technology for treatment of manure to clean water (technology 11) is added into the system. It can provide high efficiency in nutrient separation, at the price of higher investment and operational costs. The centrifuge separator produces separated solids with higher nutrient content (around 30% more concentrated than screw press). The UFC and ROC are assumed to be used as liquid digestate in the supply chain, and can be directly applied on cropland. The UFC and ROC streams are typically more diluted in nutrient than liquid digestate, SO it is less economic to transport them for a long distance. Clean water is approximately one third of the initial digestate volume and can be directly discharged or consumed by animals reducing transportation costs. The pay-off matrix of Scenario 2 (Table 4.5) was estimated.

Table 4.5: Pay-off matrix and utopia point of Scenario 2

The treatment of manure to clean water technology mainly influences they way nutri- ents are processed and does not interfere with other metrics during pay-off matrix gener- ation because the technologies in Scenario 1 are still selected. As a result, cost, GHG, NH3 and DFF remain similar as with Scenario 1. This also indicates that a specific technology reducing an environmental metric in Scenario 1 will also achieve such effect in Scenario 2. With that, even if we consider nutrient as a secondary objective, only transportation will be adjusted and the new technology will not be selected, resulting in similar values in the pay-off matrix. After the treatment of manure to clean water technology is installed,

N and K are still not balanced, but minimum nutrient release values are achieved due to higher nutrient concentration in the demanded solids, which increases the overall system cost by 28.9% and 43.5%, respectively. Introducing new technology only brings additional degrees of freedom to balance P, as it was already balanced in Scenario 1. Therefore, some of the problems corresponding to P minimization in Scenario 2 show better performance than Scenario 1. Both problem N - P and K - P are perfectly P balanced, and problems P - * also shows lower NH3 emissions and DFF consumption.

The results of problem N - Cost and N - GHG are shown in Figure 4.13. Both N - Cost and N - GHG install four large treatment of manure to clean water technologies which can handle 25.9% of all manure in the system, and the rest manure is processed by either AD and SLS or SLS only. The main difference between the two problems is the location of technologies and transportation activities, where problem N - Cost has less technologies (with larger capacities) to benefit from the economics of scale, while problem N - GHG has more distributed technologies, each with smaller capacity to reduce GHG during transportation. The design of P - Cost and P - GHG are given in Figure 4.14. Compared with N, they have simpler designs since P is more easily balanced. The prob- lem P - Cost installs SLS and treatment of manure to clean water technology to use the least cost to achieve P balance, where the treatment of manure to clean water technology replaced the AD and SLS pathway in Scenario 1. The problem P - GHG uses more AD units to gain GHG credits.

The OCS of Scenario 2 was estimated by repeating the decision making procedure described previously (Table 4.6). The cost of the OCS is increased by around 3 million USD/year compared with Scenario 1, mainly due to the installment of new technologies. The net N and K releases are reduced by 16.1% and 17.3%, respectively, and P can be perfectly balanced. On the other hand, the GHG and DFF values are increased slightly, mainly due to the decreased biogas production. Generally, the new technology makes nutrient recovery easier and endows the ability to achieve lower nutrient release. If we look at the scaled objective space, the new OCS shows a similar composition to Scenario 1,
Figure 4.13: Supply chain designs for Scenario 2 (a) problem N - Cost and (b) N - GHG
Figure 4.14: Supply chain designs for Scenario 2 (a) problem P - Cost and (b) P - GHG

where the cost contributes almost half of the total distance, and all environmental metrics are properly improved with increased investment. While some of the metrics have slightly worse performance, the new OCS moves toward ideal nutrient management, and it is also closer to the utopia point. The scaled distance to the new utopia point is 1.66 (with optimality gap of 14.9% after 35 hours of solving). If we use the minimum and maximum values in Scenario 1 for scaling, the distance is 1.73, which is decreased by 14.3%.

33: <header>4.4.4 Impact of Renewable Energy Incentives </header>

For this section, we discuss how the government could potentially manipulate the OCS within our decision-making framework by offering incentives to some of those technolo- gies and products (Scenario 3). We considered technologies available in Scenario 1, and based on previous research Sampat et al. (2018b), it was estimated that an incentive of 500 USD/MWh electricity generated by sustainable resource will be needed to reduce the payback period of technologies to less than 10 years. The pay-off matrix of Scenario 3 is shown in Table 4.7.

An incentive for sustainable energy generation is intrinsically mitigating the conflicts between GHG and cost, as well as DFF and cost given that these two metrics are directly related to each other. This trend can also be observed from the pay-off matrix, where the main change occurs in row 1 and column 1. For problems Cost - *, the system cost is now

negative (-13.3 millions USD / year), which means it is now making profit with the high incentive. The manure in the system is fully processed since it is now a valuable resource. The GHG value in line 1 is also decreased by 88.9% due to the production of biogas, which means the imposed incentive has largely mitigated the conflicts between cost and GHG. If we look at column 1, we can find that almost all problems (except NH3 - Cost) have better economic performances. From our previous analysis of Scenario 1, most of the problems will use AD to at least partially process the manure in the region, and storage systems with coverage are installed to minimize NH3 emissions (NH3 - Cost). Therefore, the cost is reduced as a result of installing profitable AD systems. This incentive, however, does not affect environmental metrics. By adding incentives to produce renewable energy, the limiting behavior of the relevant metrics (GHG and DFF) does not change, since the physical capability of available technologies are not changed. The incentives only improve the cost metric as a result of an external source of profit, and have some slight negative impact on nutrient metrics on problem Cost - *.

The supply chain designs for problem Cost - * and GHG - Cost are shown below in Figure 4.16. First we note that the design of GHG - Cost is exactly the same as in Scenario 1, since the addition of incentives will not change the physical GHG emissions of the system. The only difference is the value brought by biogas production which decreases the system cost. For problem Cost - *, the manure in the supply chain is now almost fully processed (98.6%) by either AD only or a combination of AD and SLS, to produce
biogas and earn incentives. On the other hand, due to economic-driven transportation activities, the net nutrient releases of N, P, and K are still high.

The OCS of Scenario 3 is summarized in Table 4.8. The OCS now presents a negative system cost, or a profit of 3.2 million USD/year Other metrics do not show a significant difference with OCS from Scenario 1 (except NH3). This indicates that GHG and DFF do not improve despite incentives to promote AD systems when using the utopia tracking decision making procedure. These incentives only increase the economic performance of the system. Moreover, the cost of OCS in Scenario 3 is not close to the maximum value in the pay-off matrix as in the previous two scenarios but moves toward low cost performance. In the scaled objective space, the distance between the new OCS and utopia point is 1.07 (with optimality gap of 9.2% afyer 35 hours of solving). With incentives imposed, the distance to utopia point is decreased by almost 50% compared to Scenario 1, mainly due to the reduction of system cost.

35: <header>4.4.4 Impact of Renewable Energy Incentives </header>

For this section, we discuss how the government could potentially manipulate the OCS within our decision-making framework by offering incentives to some of those technolo- gies and products (Scenario 3). We considered technologies available in Scenario 1, and based on previous research Sampat et al. (2018b), it was estimated that an incentive of 500 USD/MWh electricity generated by sustainable resource will be needed to reduce the payback period of technologies to less than 10 years. The pay-off matrix of Scenario 3 is shown in Table 4.7.

An incentive for sustainable energy generation is intrinsically mitigating the conflicts between GHG and cost, as well as DFF and cost given that these two metrics are directly related to each other. This trend can also be observed from the pay-off matrix, where the main change occurs in row 1 and column 1. For problems Cost - *, the system cost is now

negative (-13.3 millions USD / year), which means it is now making profit with the high incentive. The manure in the system is fully processed since it is now a valuable resource. The GHG value in line 1 is also decreased by 88.9% due to the production of biogas, which means the imposed incentive has largely mitigated the conflicts between cost and GHG. If we look at column 1, we can find that almost all problems (except NH3 - Cost) have better economic performances. From our previous analysis of Scenario 1, most of the problems will use AD to at least partially process the manure in the region, and storage systems with coverage are installed to minimize NH3 emissions (NH3 - Cost). Therefore, the cost is reduced as a result of installing profitable AD systems. This incentive, however, does not affect environmental metrics. By adding incentives to produce renewable energy, the limiting behavior of the relevant metrics (GHG and DFF) does not change, since the physical capability of available technologies are not changed. The incentives only improve the cost metric as a result of an external source of profit, and have some slight negative impact on nutrient metrics on problem Cost - *.

41: <header>4.5 Conclusions </header>

In this work, we presented a framework that combines life-cycle analysis of manure processing technologies with multi-product multi-objective supply chain modeling and optimization, and used a utopia-tracking method to guide the decision-making process to resolve conflicts between economical and environmental objectives. We applied this

framework in a case study of Upper Yahara Watershed in the State of Wisconsin, U.S. The pay-off matrix generated indicates that the system will not process any manure if cost is treated as the single objective. There are strong conflicts between cost and environmental metrics, such as GHG emissions, NH3 emissions, and nutrient pollution. The best pos- sible GHG emission outcome in the study region is positive but is order of magnitude smaller than other cases. Given the demand setup, P can be balanced more easily than N and K. Some conflicts between environmental metrics are also revealed in the pay-off matrix, including GHG emissions and nutrient pollution, as well as GHG emissions and NH3 emissions. The optimal compromise solution obtained from utopia-tracking method shows that to achieve the shortest distance to the utopia point, the system cost needs to be increased SO that other environmental metrics can be improved together. The deci- sions obtained also involve a mixed use of different technologies (anaerobic digestion and solid-liquid separation) and strategies regarding storage, transportation, and land appli- cation to improve environmental metrics efficiently. We also tested the impact of novel technologies and incentives on the optimal comprise solution. We found that the novel treatment of manure to clean water technology is able to replace most solid-liquid separa- tion technologies in the optimal compromise solution despite of its relatively high costs, and to reduce nutrient pollution due to its better separation efficiency. On the other hand, the incentives on renewable energy are not able to change the environmental performance of the system, but reduce the conflict between GHG emissions and the economic metric. The new optimal compromise solution shows that the system can now generate a profit, with slight changes of environmental metrics.

0: Article to be submitted to Computers and Chemical Engineering (Hu et al., 2021b).
1: The areas of food, energy, and water (FEW) are crucial sectors in human life, and they are posing significant challenges in economic, environmental, and social areas as the popu- lation keeps growing. However, the nature of interactions and interdependencies makes the FEW sectors a complicated nexus system (Finley and Seiber, 2014). For example, the organic waste management problem is tightly coupled with the field of sustainable en- ergy, crop, and dairy food production, since livestock farms generate manure together with animal products, and the energy contained in the agricultural waste can be recov- ered through technologies such as anaerobic digestion. Considering the complexity of the nexus in FEW sectors, supply chain modeling and optimization have been proven as an efficient method for analyzing such type of holistic, integrated, and multi-media prob- lems and estimating its overall economic and environmental impacts (Pan et al., 2015). A multi-product supply chain network usually contains four types of participants: suppli- ers, demanders, technology providers, and transportation providers. In a nutshell, raw

materials from suppliers are processed and transformed by technology providers into in- termediate and final products, which are consumed by demanders in the end. Through- out the process, all goods are transported by transportation providers. Sampat et al. (2017) proposed a generalized modeling network to capture the fundamental elements in the multi-product supply chain network. This type of model also has wide applications in FEW-related research, examples include cellulosic biomass (e.g., crop residue and energy crops) to biodiesel, algae to biofuel, and livestock waste to biogas. These case studies demonstrate how appropriate processing technologies in the FEW sectors can achieve positive profits and environmental improvement for communities. However, they require elaborate sets of literature and experimental data, which highly rely on experts outside of the field of computational research and systems engineering.

To provide the best practices for integrating computational and experimental work in FEW nexus and process industries and to enhance stakeholder engagement in the decision-making process, different tools and software have been developed. Daher and Mohtar developed WEF Nexus Tool (Daher and Mohtar, 2015), a platform to evaluate sce- narios and identify sustainable resource allocation strategies. This tool considers aspects such as water, energy, carbon, and financial, and models the interactions between them to give an overall sustainability index. Martín-Hernández proposed a web-based decision- making application of technology recommendation for dairy farms considering farm size, animal type, local environmental conditions, etc. Some other tools for integrated mod- eling of FEW nexus include WHAT-IF, NexSym, PRIMA, etc. (Payet-Burin et al., 2019; Martinez-Hernandez et al., 2017; Kraucunas et al., 2015), with emphasis on water plan- ning, climate changes, land use, and other specific applications. Most of these mentioned tools incorporate comprehensive supporting datasets and empirical decision-making cri- teria. Some existing tools use mathematical optimization to facilitate the selection and assessment of processes and operations, such as p-graph studio developed by Bertok et al. (2013) which conducts process-network synthesis in a process graph representation. Ng et al. (2018) developed a web application called BUS for superstructure optimization

In the field of supply chain management, various commercial software products exist for conducting business tasks such as demand forecasting, inventory tracking, import, and export, etc. The U.S. Department of Energy developed a tool MFI (Carpenter et al., 2014) to evaluate energy and material flows across different industrial sectors. Reich- Weiser et al. (2008) proposed a supply chain optimization tool SCOPR to assess energy and greenhouse gas metrics in solar energy technologies. Lin et al. (2015) designed a Cyber-GIS-based supply chain optimization tool for analyzing and optimizing biomass- to-ethanol supply chains in the U.S. However, to the best of the authors' knowledge, there has not been a general multi-product supply chain optimization tool reported for optimizing transportation routes and technology selection, even though there are many related published case studies in the field of FEW nexus and sustainable engineering (Abdali et al., 2021; You and Wang, 2011; Sampat et al., 2018a; Hu et al., 2018). Inspired by this, we developed an easy-to-use multi-product supply chain tool, named ADAM (Advanced Decision And Management). ADAM is a web-based platform that facilitates modeling and solution of multi-product supply chain models by leveraging a compact graph-based modeling abstraction, which can significantly improve the user-friendliness of the tool and shorten the learning curve. The format of a website can effectively reduce the effort needed in local software management and provide an interactive graphic user interface for model building and result visualization. ADAM applies an object-oriented data management system, where supply chain models and input data are both treated as objects SO that they can be easily exchanged and reused. The data management system is implemented in a database software in the ADAM back-end. With these features, ADAM is designed to bridge the field of supply chain optimization with experimental research, technology development, and education in the FEW nexus and supply chain manage- ment. We further note that, with the highly abstract modeling framework implemented in ADAM, potential case studies in other areas could also be developed.
In this work, we give a comprehensive review of the design principles of ADAM,

including the mathematical formulation of the multi-product supply chain optimization, the proposed graph-based supply chain modeling abstraction, and the data structure used in ADAM implementation. We also give an introduction to key features and the workflow of ADAM. Two case studies are presented to show the capability of ADAM, including one example in organic waste management and one in plastic waste recycling.

This type of model also has wide applications in FEW-related research, examples include cellulosic biomass (e.g., crop residue and energy crops) to biodiesel, algae to biofuel, and livestock waste to biogas. These case studies demonstrate how appropriate processing technologies in the FEW sectors can achieve positive profits and environmental improvement for communities. However, they require elaborate sets of literature and experimental data, which highly rely on experts outside of the field of computational research and systems engineering.

To provide the best practices for integrating computational and experimental work in FEW nexus and process industries and to enhance stakeholder engagement in the decision-making process, different tools and software have been developed. Daher and Mohtar developed WEF Nexus Tool (Daher and Mohtar, 2015), a platform to evaluate sce- narios and identify sustainable resource allocation strategies. This tool considers aspects such as water, energy, carbon, and financial, and models the interactions between them to give an overall sustainability index. Martín-Hernández proposed a web-based decision- making application of technology recommendation for dairy farms considering farm size, animal type, local environmental conditions, etc. Some other tools for integrated mod- eling of FEW nexus include WHAT-IF, NexSym, PRIMA, etc. (Payet-Burin et al., 2019; Martinez-Hernandez et al., 2017; Kraucunas et al., 2015), with emphasis on water plan- ning, climate changes, land use, and other specific applications. Most of these mentioned tools incorporate comprehensive supporting datasets and empirical decision-making cri- teria. Some existing tools use mathematical optimization to facilitate the selection and assessment of processes and operations, such as p-graph studio developed by Bertok et al. (2013) which conducts process-network synthesis in a process graph representation. Ng et al. (2018) developed a web application called BUS for superstructure optimization

In the field of supply chain management, various commercial software products exist for conducting business tasks such as demand forecasting, inventory tracking, import, and export, etc. The U.S. Department of Energy developed a tool MFI (Carpenter et al., 2014) to evaluate energy and material flows across different industrial sectors. Reich- Weiser et al. (2008) proposed a supply chain optimization tool SCOPR to assess energy and greenhouse gas metrics in solar energy technologies. Lin et al. (2015) designed a Cyber-GIS-based supply chain optimization tool for analyzing and optimizing biomass- to-ethanol supply chains in the U.S. However, to the best of the authors' knowledge, there has not been a general multi-product supply chain optimization tool reported for optimizing transportation routes and technology selection, even though there are many related published case studies in the field of FEW nexus and sustainable engineering (Abdali et al., 2021; You and Wang, 2011; Sampat et al., 2018a; Hu et al., 2018). Inspired by this, we developed an easy-to-use multi-product supply chain tool, named ADAM (Advanced Decision And Management). ADAM is a web-based platform that facilitates modeling and solution of multi-product supply chain models by leveraging a compact graph-based modeling abstraction, which can significantly improve the user-friendliness of the tool and shorten the learning curve. The format of a website can effectively reduce the effort needed in local software management and provide an interactive graphic user interface for model building and result visualization. ADAM applies an object-oriented data management system, where supply chain models and input data are both treated as objects SO that they can be easily exchanged and reused. The data management system is implemented in a database software in the ADAM back-end. With these features, ADAM is designed to bridge the field of supply chain optimization with experimental research, technology development, and education in the FEW nexus and supply chain manage- ment. We further note that, with the highly abstract modeling framework implemented in ADAM, potential case studies in other areas could also be developed.
In this work, we give a comprehensive review of the design principles of ADAM,

including the mathematical formulation of the multi-product supply chain optimization, the proposed graph-based supply chain modeling abstraction, and the data structure used in ADAM implementation. We also give an introduction to key features and the workflow of ADAM. Two case studies are presented to show the capability of ADAM, including one example in organic waste management and one in plastic waste recycling.
7: In this section, we introduce the main design principles of ADAM. We will first introduce the mathematical optimization models behind the scene, and then introduce the graph- based modeling abstraction implemented in ADAM, as well as the data structure used.
8: <header>5.2.1 Mathematical Formulation of Supply Chain Modeling </header>

The mathematical model behind ADAM is the multi-product supply chain optimization model proposed by Sampat et al. (2017). In this section, we conduct a brief review of this model; for detailed information, readers can check the original paper.

In a multi-product supply chain optimization model, the concept of set is used to describe components in the system. All geographical nodes in the system, representing physical locations for suppliers, customers, processing technologies, and candidate loca- tions for installing new technologies, comprise the set of nodes, denoted by N. Each node i in N has two geographical attributes, latitude and longitude. The set P represents the collection of all materials in the system, including feedstock, intermediate products, and final products. The set T represents the collection of all technologies in the system. Each item t in the technology set T has several numerical attributes to record its investment and operational cost and associated material transformations.

We note that the multi-product supply chain optimization model proposed is a general model that can be applied to different types of systematic waste management problems and for different geographical areas, as long as the corresponding data are provided
correctly. Moreover, this model is not restricted to the field of waste management; it can be used to tackle diverse applications.

MATERIAL BALANCE The first set of constraints in the model is material balance con- straints. Equation (5.2.1) is a balance for material p at node i, and this balance hold for arbitrary material in set P and arbitrary node in set N. The left-hand side contains a supply term Si,p, a total inflow term fj,i,k, and a term Egi,p,t to represent the net gen- eration of material p at node i from technologies. The net generation term can be either positive (material is produced), negative (material is consumed), or zero (material is not produced or consumed). The right part of the equation contains a demand term di,p and a total outflow term fi,j,k.

Equation (5.2.2) captures material transformations through technologies. The genera- tion amount of material p at node i through technology t is calculated using the genera- tion term of the reference material of the technology gi,t,Pref(t)' and the relationship between the yield factors At,p.

CAPACITY CONSTRAINTS We use equation (5.2.3)-(5.2.5) to capture the capacities for supply, demand, and generation due to different realistic reasons. In those constraints, we use Sip and Si,p to represent the lower and upper bound for supply Si,p, di,p and di,p to represent the lower and upper bound for demand di,p, and C and Ct to represent the lower and upper bound of processing capacity of technology t.

The binary variable Yi,t is used to indicate whether technology t is installed at node i. Specifically, if Yi,t = 1, then the generation term is bounded by the capacity ranges of technology t; if Yi,t = 0, then the generation term is zero, meaning that there is no material

transformation if the technology is not installed. Sometimes, before solving the model, we know some nodes already have technologies installed. In that case, the corresponding binary variable Yi,t can be prefixed. We note that if all the binary variables Yi,t are fixed, the model reduces to a linear programming problem, where the model is purely used for analyzing the systematic performance of installed technologies. Under this condition, the system behaves like a coordinated market Sampat et al. (2019) and we refer this special case as supply chain management model, whereas others as supply chain design model when there is at least one unfixed binary variable(s).

ECONOMIC METRICS The economic metrics considered in the model include technol- ogy investment cost, technology operational cost, supply cost, transportation cost, and revenue from selling final products. The investment cost of technology is captured by equation (5.2.6), where r is interest rate, N is the expected life of technologies, Kinv,t is the fixed part of investment cost for technology t, and Binv,t is the proportional part of invest- ment cost for technology t. We note that the binary variables Y'it here are only defined for those that are not prefixed; if some technologies are pre-installed and the corresponding binary variables are prefixed, then they are excluded in the investment calculation.

Equation (5.2.7) is used to calculate the operational cost of technologies, where Kop,t is the fixed part of operational cost for technology t, and Bop,t is the proportional part of operational cost for technology t. We can observe that if a technology t is not installed at node i, then Yi,t = 0, meaning gi,t,Pref(t) = 0 according to Equation (5.2.5), then the
cooresponding investment and operational costs are zero as well.

Equation (5.2.8) is used to calculate the transportation cost, where Di,j is the trans- portation distance between node i and j, and Ctrans,p is the transportation cost of product p per unit flow per unit distance. The transportation distance between nodes can be esti- mated by longitude and latitude. For example, it is reported that for the U.S., 1.17 times of the great-circle distance is a reasonable approximation (Ballou, 1998).

Finally, we use (5.2.9) and (5.2.10) to calculate the supply cost and revenue from de- mand. In these two equations, Pip represents the per unit supply price of product p at node i, and Pap represents the per unit demand price of product p at node i.

OBJECTIVE The objective function of this model is to maximize the social welfare (sys- tem profit) defined by equation (5.2.11). The social welfare defined here is a composite metric that fuses all economic metrics defined previously.

The left-hand side contains a supply term Si,p, a total inflow term fj,i,k, and a term Egi,p,t to represent the net gen- eration of material p at node i from technologies. The net generation term can be either positive (material is produced), negative (material is consumed), or zero (material is not produced or consumed). The right part of the equation contains a demand term di,p and a total outflow term fi,j,k.

Equation (5.2.2) captures material transformations through technologies. The genera- tion amount of material p at node i through technology t is calculated using the genera- tion term of the reference material of the technology gi,t,Pref(t)' and the relationship between the yield factors At,p.

CAPACITY CONSTRAINTS We use equation (5.2.3)-(5.2.5) to capture the capacities for supply, demand, and generation due to different realistic reasons. In those constraints, we use Sip and Si,p to represent the lower and upper bound for supply Si,p, di,p and di,p to represent the lower and upper bound for demand di,p, and C and Ct to represent the lower and upper bound of processing capacity of technology t.

The binary variable Yi,t is used to indicate whether technology t is installed at node i. Specifically, if Yi,t = 1, then the generation term is bounded by the capacity ranges of technology t; if Yi,t = 0, then the generation term is zero, meaning that there is no material

transformation if the technology is not installed. Sometimes, before solving the model, we know some nodes already have technologies installed. In that case, the corresponding binary variable Yi,t can be prefixed. We note that if all the binary variables Yi,t are fixed, the model reduces to a linear programming problem, where the model is purely used for analyzing the systematic performance of installed technologies. Under this condition, the system behaves like a coordinated market Sampat et al. (2019) and we refer this special case as supply chain management model, whereas others as supply chain design model when there is at least one unfixed binary variable(s).

ECONOMIC METRICS The economic metrics considered in the model include technol- ogy investment cost, technology operational cost, supply cost, transportation cost, and revenue from selling final products. The investment cost of technology is captured by equation (5.2.6), where r is interest rate, N is the expected life of technologies, Kinv,t is the fixed part of investment cost for technology t, and Binv,t is the proportional part of invest- ment cost for technology t. We note that the binary variables Y'it here are only defined for those that are not prefixed; if some technologies are pre-installed and the corresponding binary variables are prefixed, then they are excluded in the investment calculation.

Equation (5.2.7) is used to calculate the operational cost of technologies, where Kop,t is the fixed part of operational cost for technology t, and Bop,t is the proportional part of operational cost for technology t. We can observe that if a technology t is not installed at node i, then Yi,t = 0, meaning gi,t,Pref(t) = 0 according to Equation (5.2.5), then the
cooresponding investment and operational costs are zero as well.

Equation (5.2.8) is used to calculate the transportation cost, where Di,j is the trans- portation distance between node i and j, and Ctrans,p is the transportation cost of product p per unit flow per unit distance. The transportation distance between nodes can be esti- mated by longitude and latitude. For example, it is reported that for the U.S., 1.17 times of the great-circle distance is a reasonable approximation (Ballou, 1998).

Finally, we use (5.2.9) and (5.2.10) to calculate the supply cost and revenue from de- mand. In these two equations, Pip represents the per unit supply price of product p at node i, and Pap represents the per unit demand price of product p at node i.

OBJECTIVE The objective function of this model is to maximize the social welfare (sys- tem profit) defined by equation (5.2.11). The social welfare defined here is a composite metric that fuses all economic metrics defined previously.
14: <header>5.2.1 Mathematical Formulation of Supply Chain Modeling </header>

The mathematical model behind ADAM is the multi-product supply chain optimization model proposed by Sampat et al. (2017). In this section, we conduct a brief review of this model; for detailed information, readers can check the original paper.

In a multi-product supply chain optimization model, the concept of set is used to describe components in the system. All geographical nodes in the system, representing physical locations for suppliers, customers, processing technologies, and candidate loca- tions for installing new technologies, comprise the set of nodes, denoted by N. Each node i in N has two geographical attributes, latitude and longitude. The set P represents the collection of all materials in the system, including feedstock, intermediate products, and final products. The set T represents the collection of all technologies in the system. Each item t in the technology set T has several numerical attributes to record its investment and operational cost and associated material transformations.

We note that the multi-product supply chain optimization model proposed is a general model that can be applied to different types of systematic waste management problems and for different geographical areas, as long as the corresponding data are provided
correctly. Moreover, this model is not restricted to the field of waste management; it can be used to tackle diverse applications.

MATERIAL BALANCE The first set of constraints in the model is material balance con- straints. Equation (5.2.1) is a balance for material p at node i, and this balance hold for arbitrary material in set P and arbitrary node in set N. The left-hand side contains a supply term Si,p, a total inflow term fj,i,k, and a term Egi,p,t to represent the net gen- eration of material p at node i from technologies. The net generation term can be either positive (material is produced), negative (material is consumed), or zero (material is not produced or consumed). The right part of the equation contains a demand term di,p and a total outflow term fi,j,k.

Equation (5.2.2) captures material transformations through technologies. The genera- tion amount of material p at node i through technology t is calculated using the genera- tion term of the reference material of the technology gi,t,Pref(t)' and the relationship between the yield factors At,p.

CAPACITY CONSTRAINTS We use equation (5.2.3)-(5.2.5) to capture the capacities for supply, demand, and generation due to different realistic reasons. In those constraints, we use Sip and Si,p to represent the lower and upper bound for supply Si,p, di,p and di,p to represent the lower and upper bound for demand di,p, and C and Ct to represent the lower and upper bound of processing capacity of technology t.

The binary variable Yi,t is used to indicate whether technology t is installed at node i. Specifically, if Yi,t = 1, then the generation term is bounded by the capacity ranges of technology t; if Yi,t = 0, then the generation term is zero, meaning that there is no material

transformation if the technology is not installed. Sometimes, before solving the model, we know some nodes already have technologies installed. In that case, the corresponding binary variable Yi,t can be prefixed. We note that if all the binary variables Yi,t are fixed, the model reduces to a linear programming problem, where the model is purely used for analyzing the systematic performance of installed technologies. Under this condition, the system behaves like a coordinated market Sampat et al. (2019) and we refer this special case as supply chain management model, whereas others as supply chain design model when there is at least one unfixed binary variable(s).

ECONOMIC METRICS The economic metrics considered in the model include technol- ogy investment cost, technology operational cost, supply cost, transportation cost, and revenue from selling final products. The investment cost of technology is captured by equation (5.2.6), where r is interest rate, N is the expected life of technologies, Kinv,t is the fixed part of investment cost for technology t, and Binv,t is the proportional part of invest- ment cost for technology t. We note that the binary variables Y'it here are only defined for those that are not prefixed; if some technologies are pre-installed and the corresponding binary variables are prefixed, then they are excluded in the investment calculation.

Equation (5.2.7) is used to calculate the operational cost of technologies, where Kop,t is the fixed part of operational cost for technology t, and Bop,t is the proportional part of operational cost for technology t. We can observe that if a technology t is not installed at node i, then Yi,t = 0, meaning gi,t,Pref(t) = 0 according to Equation (5.2.5), then the
cooresponding investment and operational costs are zero as well.

Equation (5.2.8) is used to calculate the transportation cost, where Di,j is the trans- portation distance between node i and j, and Ctrans,p is the transportation cost of product p per unit flow per unit distance. The transportation distance between nodes can be esti- mated by longitude and latitude. For example, it is reported that for the U.S., 1.17 times of the great-circle distance is a reasonable approximation (Ballou, 1998).

Finally, we use (5.2.9) and (5.2.10) to calculate the supply cost and revenue from de- mand. In these two equations, Pip represents the per unit supply price of product p at node i, and Pap represents the per unit demand price of product p at node i.

OBJECTIVE The objective function of this model is to maximize the social welfare (sys- tem profit) defined by equation (5.2.11). The social welfare defined here is a composite metric that fuses all economic metrics defined previously.
15: Given the model defined in Section 5.2.1, we note that only when the set of nodes N, set of materials P, and set of technologies T are properly defined; then the rest data, such as supply and demand capacities and pre-installed technologies, can be defined since these data have attributes that are coupled to those sets. For example, when we define a supply upper bound, we need to specify the location of the supply and the product supplied, and when we define a pre-installed technology, the technology type and the location where it is installed are also required. This indicates that, before the model is applied, the model builder needs to list all the geographical locations, materials, and technologies that are possibly used in the system. The construction and operation of sets are abstract and cum- bersome and usually require a background in systems engineering and programming. However, many real case studies involve the use of various technical, experimental, and economic data (e.g. product transformation, product price), which makes it crucial to establish collaborations between computational and experimental researchers.

Therefore, we propose a high-level graph-based supply chain modeling framework to compact the model building. The framework leverages two types of graphs: a process graph (p-graph) and ageographical graph (g-graph).

PROCESS GRAPH The process graph, or p-graph, is first proposed by Friedler et al. (1992, 1993) and is then widely applied in process synthesis and superstructure optimiza- tion (Varbanov et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2016; Voll et al., 2013). It is a directed, bipartite graph that is used to represent the process structure. Here, we provide a basic review of concepts related to p-graph, and then provide examples to show its use in supply chain optimization. For more details, readers can refer to the original publication.
A p-graph Gp is represented as (Vp,Ep), where Vp is the set of vertices and Ep is the set of edges. The set Vp can be further partitioned into two disjoined sets, denoted as Mp and Tp:

The vertices in Mp (denoted M vertices) represent all possible materials involved in a process, while the vertices in Tp (denoted T vertices) represent all possible technologies involved in a process. For simplicity, we use the following notation to represent elements in the two sets, where Mi and ti represent some material and technology in the process, respectively. Furthermore, a technology ti can be further represented by a tuple (Ati'l Bti), where Ati is the set of feedstock materials of the technology, and Bti is the set of product materials of the technology (Ati, Bti C Mp). The technology ti is capable of transforming materials in Ati to materials in Bti according to certain transformation rules (stoichiometry relationship).
The set Ep in the p-graph Gp can also be further partitioned into two disjoint sets, denoted as Ep,M->T and Ep,T->:

The set Ep,M-T includes edges that go from one feedstock of some technology to the technology, and the set Ep,T->M includes edges that go from some technology to one of its products. An edge can only exist if the corresponding transformation is physically achievable. It is now obvious that a p-graph always starts and ends on at least one M ver- tices. Specifically, M vertices with no input edges represent raw materials for the whole system, M vertices with no output edges represent final products, and M vertices with both input and output edges represent intermediate products. There are no isolated ver- tices in a p-graph; every vertex must be connected to at least one other vertex. Moreover, every two adjacent vertices V1 and V2, i.e., (U1, U2) E Ep, must be of different categories.

An example of a simple p-graph is given below in Figure 5.1, where Mp = {m1, M2, M3, m4} and Tp = {({m1},{m2,m3}),({m3},{m4})}. The graph represents a process structure where material m1 can be converted to m2 and m3 through technology t1 = ({m1}, {m2, m3}), and then intermediate products m3 can be further transformed to m4 by technology t2 = ({m3}, {m4}). In this example, vertices in set Mp are displayed as circles and vertices in set Tp are displayed as rectangles. Edges in set Ep,M-T are shown in black, and edges in set Ep,T->M are shown in blue.

We note that each p-graph defines a process structure, and additional data need to be attached to T vertices and edges. Each T vertex t E Tp is associated with information of processing capacity (upper bound Ct and lower bound Ct ), investment cost (fixed part Binv,t and proportional part Kinv,t), and operational cost (fixed part Bop,t and proportional
part Kinv,t). These data fully specify constraints (5.2.5)-(5.2.7).

Each arbitrary edge e = (01,02) E Ep contains data about the corresponding transfor- mation coefficient Ae. Specifically, if e E Ep,M->T, then we have ae = At,P where t = V1 and p = V2; otherwise, if e E Ep,T->m, then Ae = at,p where t=u2 and p = V1. Furthermore, the transformation coefficient of reference material of technology t in a p-graph is defined below. By default, we select the main feedstock of technology t, i.e., the input flow with largest absolute value of transformation coefficient, as the reference material. Thus, data in set Ep fully specify constraints (5.2.2).

The same example with data is presented below in Figure 5.2, where we can observe the transformation relationships among products. Every unit of material m1 can be con- verted to 0.2 unit of m2 and 0.8 unit of m3 through technology t1. And then for every unit of M3, it can be further transformed to m4 through technology t2. For technology t1, its operating capacity is 100-1000 units of m1 per year, with an investment cost of 1 million USD and operational cost of 100 USD per unit of M1. And similarly, for technology t2, its operating capacity is 80-800 unit of m3 per year, with an investment cost of 0.5 million USD and operational cost of 40 USD per unit of M3. We note that in this example, the values of transformation coefficients related to t2 do not balance. In reality, this can be caused by inconsistent unit measure, material loss or supplement in the technology (e.g., drying process), property change of the stream (e.g., pressure change of gas), etc.

We claim that a p-graph can completely replace the technology set T and material set P, and it can help model builders to define all possible material transformations in a supply chain system in a more intuitive way. Figure 5.3 is another example of p-graph for the livestcok waste supply chain case study reported in Sampat et al. (2019). In this case, the livestock waste can be separated into solid and liquid fractions. The solid fraction can further go into a granulation step, and the liquid fraction can be processed by a
subsequent struvite recovery technology as well.
Figure 5.3: Example of p-graph in livestock waste supply chain system

GEOGRAPHICAL GRAPH In addition to the p-graph which is used to represent product transformations, another type of graph, geographical graph, or g-graph, is also needed in this graph-based modeling abstraction. A g-graph is represented as (Vg,Eg), where V8 is the set of vertices and Eg is the set of directed edges. The set V8 can be further partitioned into four disjoined sets, denoted as Sg, Dg, TP8, and TC8, i.e.,

The vertices in the four sets, Sg, Dg, TPs, and TC8, represent all suppliers, deman- ders, technology providers, and technology candidates, in the system. Suppliers provide raw materials into the supply chain system, and demanders (consumers) consume prod- ucts. Technology providers represent facilities that have already been installed and can be used for material conversion. Technology candidates represent nodes where additional facilities can be installed. Specifically, the set of technology candidates should always be empty for a supply chain management model since only existing technologies are allowed to use.

We use Si, di, tpi, and tci to represent elements in those sets. Similar to the p-graph, each node in the g-graph has data attached. Each supply node Si E S is defined with corresponding supply material Msi E Mp, a supply capacity Si, and a supply price Psi. Each demand node di is defined with corresponding supply material Mdi E Mp, a de- mand capacity di, and a supply price Pdi. Each technology provider tpi is specified by the installed technology ttp; E Tp and processing capacity Ctpi. Each technology candidate tci is specified by the technology that can be installed, ttci E Tp. We give an example of a small g-graph, containing 2 suppliers (gold), 2 demanders (green), 1 technology provider (blue), and 1 technology candidate (red) in Figure 5.4. Both suppliers offer material m1 but with different capacities and prices. The technology provider offers technology t1 that is able to convert m1 to m2 and M3. The technology candidate offers t2, which is able to convert m3 to M4. One demander can consume material m2 with a capacity of 200 kg per day at a price of 8 USD per kg of m2. Another demander can consume material m4 with a capacity of 320 kg per day at a price of 4 USD per kg of M4.
The set of edges Eg in a g-graph is determined when all vertices are defined with
Figure 5.4: Example of g-graph (vertices and data)

proper data. Specifically, an element in set Eg can be denoted as e = (01,02, p) and it represents a valid transportation route of product p from vertex V1 to V2. The set Eg in a g-graph can be further partitioned into four disjoined sets, denoted as Eg,S-D, Eg,S->T, Eg,T-D, and Eg,T->T, i,e.,
Eg,T->T = TS8 UTC, V2 TS8 UTCs, to2 = (az, B2), pEp,,PEa2}
Here those conditions imply that the product p can be directly transported from V1 to V2 if (1) V1 supplies p and V2 demands p; (2) V1 supplies p and V2 has (or can install)

some technology that takes p as feedstock; (3) V1 has (or can install) some technology that generates p as product and V2 demands p; (4) V1 has (or can install) some technology that generates p as product, and V2 has (or can install) some technology that takes p as feedstock.

Each edge e = (01,02, p) is associated with the transportation cost of moving one unit of material p along this edge, denoted as Ce. Typically, Ce can be computed as a linear function of the distance between V1 and V2 (DV1,02), i.e., Ce = D01,02 Ctrans,pr where Ctrans,p is the unit transportation cost of material p. The distance can be calculated if the geographical coordinates of vertices are specified, and the unit transportation cost can be treated as an attribute of the material.

The same example with edges and data is presented again in Figure 5.5. Material m1 can be transported from S1 or S2 to tp1, where technology t1 uses m1 as feedstock. Material m2 can be generated from t1 in tp1 and consumed by d1. Material m3 can be generated from t1 in tp1 and then used as a (potential) feedstock of t2 in tc1. Finally, material m4 can be potentially generated by t2 in tc and then consumed by d2. Each edge in g-graph is assigned the corresponding transportation cost. For a g-graph, it can completely replace the node set N in the mathematical formulation and define the geographical property in a simpler way. We also note that g-graph and p-graph are closely tied due to shared material and technology elements.
