For example, when we define a supply upper bound, we need to specify the location of the supply and the product supplied, and when we define a pre-installed technology, the technology type and the location where it is installed are also required. This indicates that, before the model is applied, the model builder needs to list all the geographical locations, materials, and technologies that are possibly used in the system. The construction and operation of sets are abstract and cum- bersome and usually require a background in systems engineering and programming. However, many real case studies involve the use of various technical, experimental, and economic data (e.g. product transformation, product price), which makes it crucial to establish collaborations between computational and experimental researchers.

Therefore, we propose a high-level graph-based supply chain modeling framework to compact the model building. The framework leverages two types of graphs: a process graph (p-graph) and ageographical graph (g-graph).

PROCESS GRAPH The process graph, or p-graph, is first proposed by Friedler et al. (1992, 1993) and is then widely applied in process synthesis and superstructure optimiza- tion (Varbanov et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2016; Voll et al., 2013). It is a directed, bipartite graph that is used to represent the process structure. Here, we provide a basic review of concepts related to p-graph, and then provide examples to show its use in supply chain optimization. For more details, readers can refer to the original publication.
A p-graph Gp is represented as (Vp,Ep), where Vp is the set of vertices and Ep is the set of edges. The set Vp can be further partitioned into two disjoined sets, denoted as Mp and Tp:

The vertices in Mp (denoted M vertices) represent all possible materials involved in a process, while the vertices in Tp (denoted T vertices) represent all possible technologies involved in a process. For simplicity, we use the following notation to represent elements in the two sets, where Mi and ti represent some material and technology in the process, respectively. Furthermore, a technology ti can be further represented by a tuple (Ati'l Bti), where Ati is the set of feedstock materials of the technology, and Bti is the set of product materials of the technology (Ati, Bti C Mp). The technology ti is capable of transforming materials in Ati to materials in Bti according to certain transformation rules (stoichiometry relationship).
The set Ep in the p-graph Gp can also be further partitioned into two disjoint sets, denoted as Ep,M->T and Ep,T->:

The set Ep,M-T includes edges that go from one feedstock of some technology to the technology, and the set Ep,T->M includes edges that go from some technology to one of its products. An edge can only exist if the corresponding transformation is physically achievable. It is now obvious that a p-graph always starts and ends on at least one M ver- tices. Specifically, M vertices with no input edges represent raw materials for the whole system, M vertices with no output edges represent final products, and M vertices with both input and output edges represent intermediate products. There are no isolated ver- tices in a p-graph; every vertex must be connected to at least one other vertex. Moreover, every two adjacent vertices V1 and V2, i.e., (U1, U2) E Ep, must be of different categories.

An example of a simple p-graph is given below in Figure 5.1, where Mp = {m1, M2, M3, m4} and Tp = {({m1},{m2,m3}),({m3},{m4})}. The graph represents a process structure where material m1 can be converted to m2 and m3 through technology t1 = ({m1}, {m2, m3}), and then intermediate products m3 can be further transformed to m4 by technology t2 = ({m3}, {m4}). In this example, vertices in set Mp are displayed as circles and vertices in set Tp are displayed as rectangles. Edges in set Ep,M-T are shown in black, and edges in set Ep,T->M are shown in blue.

We note that each p-graph defines a process structure, and additional data need to be attached to T vertices and edges. Each T vertex t E Tp is associated with information of processing capacity (upper bound Ct and lower bound Ct ), investment cost (fixed part Binv,t and proportional part Kinv,t), and operational cost (fixed part Bop,t and proportional
part Kinv,t). These data fully specify constraints (5.2.5)-(5.2.7).

Each arbitrary edge e = (01,02) E Ep contains data about the corresponding transfor- mation coefficient Ae. Specifically, if e E Ep,M->T, then we have ae = At,P where t = V1 and p = V2; otherwise, if e E Ep,T->m, then Ae = at,p where t=u2 and p = V1. Furthermore, the transformation coefficient of reference material of technology t in a p-graph is defined below. By default, we select the main feedstock of technology t, i.e., the input flow with largest absolute value of transformation coefficient, as the reference material. Thus, data in set Ep fully specify constraints (5.2.2).

The same example with data is presented below in Figure 5.2, where we can observe the transformation relationships among products. Every unit of material m1 can be con- verted to 0.2 unit of m2 and 0.8 unit of m3 through technology t1. And then for every unit of M3, it can be further transformed to m4 through technology t2. For technology t1, its operating capacity is 100-1000 units of m1 per year, with an investment cost of 1 million USD and operational cost of 100 USD per unit of M1. And similarly, for technology t2, its operating capacity is 80-800 unit of m3 per year, with an investment cost of 0.5 million USD and operational cost of 40 USD per unit of M3. We note that in this example, the values of transformation coefficients related to t2 do not balance. In reality, this can be caused by inconsistent unit measure, material loss or supplement in the technology (e.g., drying process), property change of the stream (e.g., pressure change of gas), etc.

We claim that a p-graph can completely replace the technology set T and material set P, and it can help model builders to define all possible material transformations in a supply chain system in a more intuitive way. Figure 5.3 is another example of p-graph for the livestcok waste supply chain case study reported in Sampat et al. (2019). In this case, the livestock waste can be separated into solid and liquid fractions. The solid fraction can further go into a granulation step, and the liquid fraction can be processed by a
subsequent struvite recovery technology as well.
Figure 5.3: Example of p-graph in livestock waste supply chain system

GEOGRAPHICAL GRAPH In addition to the p-graph which is used to represent product transformations, another type of graph, geographical graph, or g-graph, is also needed in this graph-based modeling abstraction. A g-graph is represented as (Vg,Eg), where V8 is the set of vertices and Eg is the set of directed edges. The set V8 can be further partitioned into four disjoined sets, denoted as Sg, Dg, TP8, and TC8, i.e.,

The vertices in the four sets, Sg, Dg, TPs, and TC8, represent all suppliers, deman- ders, technology providers, and technology candidates, in the system. Suppliers provide raw materials into the supply chain system, and demanders (consumers) consume prod- ucts. Technology providers represent facilities that have already been installed and can be used for material conversion. Technology candidates represent nodes where additional facilities can be installed. Specifically, the set of technology candidates should always be empty for a supply chain management model since only existing technologies are allowed to use.

We use Si, di, tpi, and tci to represent elements in those sets. Similar to the p-graph, each node in the g-graph has data attached. Each supply node Si E S is defined with corresponding supply material Msi E Mp, a supply capacity Si, and a supply price Psi. Each demand node di is defined with corresponding supply material Mdi E Mp, a de- mand capacity di, and a supply price Pdi. Each technology provider tpi is specified by the installed technology ttp; E Tp and processing capacity Ctpi. Each technology candidate tci is specified by the technology that can be installed, ttci E Tp. We give an example of a small g-graph, containing 2 suppliers (gold), 2 demanders (green), 1 technology provider (blue), and 1 technology candidate (red) in Figure 5.4. Both suppliers offer material m1 but with different capacities and prices. The technology provider offers technology t1 that is able to convert m1 to m2 and M3. The technology candidate offers t2, which is able to convert m3 to M4. One demander can consume material m2 with a capacity of 200 kg per day at a price of 8 USD per kg of m2. Another demander can consume material m4 with a capacity of 320 kg per day at a price of 4 USD per kg of M4.
The set of edges Eg in a g-graph is determined when all vertices are defined with
Figure 5.4: Example of g-graph (vertices and data)

proper data. Specifically, an element in set Eg can be denoted as e = (01,02, p) and it represents a valid transportation route of product p from vertex V1 to V2. The set Eg in a g-graph can be further partitioned into four disjoined sets, denoted as Eg,S-D, Eg,S->T, Eg,T-D, and Eg,T->T, i,e.,
Eg,T->T = TS8 UTC, V2 TS8 UTCs, to2 = (az, B2), pEp,,PEa2}
Here those conditions imply that the product p can be directly transported from V1 to V2 if (1) V1 supplies p and V2 demands p; (2) V1 supplies p and V2 has (or can install)

some technology that takes p as feedstock; (3) V1 has (or can install) some technology that generates p as product and V2 demands p; (4) V1 has (or can install) some technology that generates p as product, and V2 has (or can install) some technology that takes p as feedstock.

Each edge e = (01,02, p) is associated with the transportation cost of moving one unit of material p along this edge, denoted as Ce. Typically, Ce can be computed as a linear function of the distance between V1 and V2 (DV1,02), i.e., Ce = D01,02 Ctrans,pr where Ctrans,p is the unit transportation cost of material p. The distance can be calculated if the geographical coordinates of vertices are specified, and the unit transportation cost can be treated as an attribute of the material.

The same example with edges and data is presented again in Figure 5.5. Material m1 can be transported from S1 or S2 to tp1, where technology t1 uses m1 as feedstock. Material m2 can be generated from t1 in tp1 and consumed by d1. Material m3 can be generated from t1 in tp1 and then used as a (potential) feedstock of t2 in tc1. Finally, material m4 can be potentially generated by t2 in tc and then consumed by d2. Each edge in g-graph is assigned the corresponding transportation cost. For a g-graph, it can completely replace the node set N in the mathematical formulation and define the geographical property in a simpler way. We also note that g-graph and p-graph are closely tied due to shared material and technology elements.

some technology that takes p as feedstock; (3) V1 has (or can install) some technology that generates p as product and V2 demands p; (4) V1 has (or can install) some technology that generates p as product, and V2 has (or can install) some technology that takes p as feedstock.

Each edge e = (01,02, p) is associated with the transportation cost of moving one unit of material p along this edge, denoted as Ce. Typically, Ce can be computed as a linear function of the distance between V1 and V2 (DV1,02), i.e., Ce = D01,02 Ctrans,pr where Ctrans,p is the unit transportation cost of material p. The distance can be calculated if the geographical coordinates of vertices are specified, and the unit transportation cost can be treated as an attribute of the material.

The same example with edges and data is presented again in Figure 5.5. Material m1 can be transported from S1 or S2 to tp1, where technology t1 uses m1 as feedstock. Material m2 can be generated from t1 in tp1 and consumed by d1. Material m3 can be generated from t1 in tp1 and then used as a (potential) feedstock of t2 in tc1. Finally, material m4 can be potentially generated by t2 in tc and then consumed by d2. Each edge in g-graph is assigned the corresponding transportation cost. For a g-graph, it can completely replace the node set N in the mathematical formulation and define the geographical property in a simpler way. We also note that g-graph and p-graph are closely tied due to shared material and technology elements.
20: Given the model defined in Section 5.2.1, we note that only when the set of nodes N, set of materials P, and set of technologies T are properly defined; then the rest data, such as supply and demand capacities and pre-installed technologies, can be defined since these data have attributes that are coupled to those sets. For example, when we define a supply upper bound, we need to specify the location of the supply and the product supplied, and when we define a pre-installed technology, the technology type and the location where it is installed are also required. This indicates that, before the model is applied, the model builder needs to list all the geographical locations, materials, and technologies that are possibly used in the system. The construction and operation of sets are abstract and cum- bersome and usually require a background in systems engineering and programming. However, many real case studies involve the use of various technical, experimental, and economic data (e.g. product transformation, product price), which makes it crucial to establish collaborations between computational and experimental researchers.

Therefore, we propose a high-level graph-based supply chain modeling framework to compact the model building. The framework leverages two types of graphs: a process graph (p-graph) and ageographical graph (g-graph).

PROCESS GRAPH The process graph, or p-graph, is first proposed by Friedler et al. (1992, 1993) and is then widely applied in process synthesis and superstructure optimiza- tion (Varbanov et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2016; Voll et al., 2013). It is a directed, bipartite graph that is used to represent the process structure. Here, we provide a basic review of concepts related to p-graph, and then provide examples to show its use in supply chain optimization. For more details, readers can refer to the original publication.
A p-graph Gp is represented as (Vp,Ep), where Vp is the set of vertices and Ep is the set of edges. The set Vp can be further partitioned into two disjoined sets, denoted as Mp and Tp:

The vertices in Mp (denoted M vertices) represent all possible materials involved in a process, while the vertices in Tp (denoted T vertices) represent all possible technologies involved in a process. For simplicity, we use the following notation to represent elements in the two sets, where Mi and ti represent some material and technology in the process, respectively. Furthermore, a technology ti can be further represented by a tuple (Ati'l Bti), where Ati is the set of feedstock materials of the technology, and Bti is the set of product materials of the technology (Ati, Bti C Mp). The technology ti is capable of transforming materials in Ati to materials in Bti according to certain transformation rules (stoichiometry relationship).
The set Ep in the p-graph Gp can also be further partitioned into two disjoint sets, denoted as Ep,M->T and Ep,T->:

The set Ep,M-T includes edges that go from one feedstock of some technology to the technology, and the set Ep,T->M includes edges that go from some technology to one of its products. An edge can only exist if the corresponding transformation is physically achievable. It is now obvious that a p-graph always starts and ends on at least one M ver- tices. Specifically, M vertices with no input edges represent raw materials for the whole system, M vertices with no output edges represent final products, and M vertices with both input and output edges represent intermediate products. There are no isolated ver- tices in a p-graph; every vertex must be connected to at least one other vertex. Moreover, every two adjacent vertices V1 and V2, i.e., (U1, U2) E Ep, must be of different categories.

An example of a simple p-graph is given below in Figure 5.1, where Mp = {m1, M2, M3, m4} and Tp = {({m1},{m2,m3}),({m3},{m4})}. The graph represents a process structure where material m1 can be converted to m2 and m3 through technology t1 = ({m1}, {m2, m3}), and then intermediate products m3 can be further transformed to m4 by technology t2 = ({m3}, {m4}). In this example, vertices in set Mp are displayed as circles and vertices in set Tp are displayed as rectangles. Edges in set Ep,M-T are shown in black, and edges in set Ep,T->M are shown in blue.

We note that each p-graph defines a process structure, and additional data need to be attached to T vertices and edges. Each T vertex t E Tp is associated with information of processing capacity (upper bound Ct and lower bound Ct ), investment cost (fixed part Binv,t and proportional part Kinv,t), and operational cost (fixed part Bop,t and proportional
part Kinv,t). These data fully specify constraints (5.2.5)-(5.2.7).

Each arbitrary edge e = (01,02) E Ep contains data about the corresponding transfor- mation coefficient Ae. Specifically, if e E Ep,M->T, then we have ae = At,P where t = V1 and p = V2; otherwise, if e E Ep,T->m, then Ae = at,p where t=u2 and p = V1. Furthermore, the transformation coefficient of reference material of technology t in a p-graph is defined below. By default, we select the main feedstock of technology t, i.e., the input flow with largest absolute value of transformation coefficient, as the reference material. Thus, data in set Ep fully specify constraints (5.2.2).

The same example with data is presented below in Figure 5.2, where we can observe the transformation relationships among products. Every unit of material m1 can be con- verted to 0.2 unit of m2 and 0.8 unit of m3 through technology t1. And then for every unit of M3, it can be further transformed to m4 through technology t2. For technology t1, its operating capacity is 100-1000 units of m1 per year, with an investment cost of 1 million USD and operational cost of 100 USD per unit of M1. And similarly, for technology t2, its operating capacity is 80-800 unit of m3 per year, with an investment cost of 0.5 million USD and operational cost of 40 USD per unit of M3. We note that in this example, the values of transformation coefficients related to t2 do not balance. In reality, this can be caused by inconsistent unit measure, material loss or supplement in the technology (e.g., drying process), property change of the stream (e.g., pressure change of gas), etc.

We claim that a p-graph can completely replace the technology set T and material set P, and it can help model builders to define all possible material transformations in a supply chain system in a more intuitive way. Figure 5.3 is another example of p-graph for the livestcok waste supply chain case study reported in Sampat et al. (2019). In this case, the livestock waste can be separated into solid and liquid fractions. The solid fraction can further go into a granulation step, and the liquid fraction can be processed by a
subsequent struvite recovery technology as well.
Figure 5.3: Example of p-graph in livestock waste supply chain system

GEOGRAPHICAL GRAPH In addition to the p-graph which is used to represent product transformations, another type of graph, geographical graph, or g-graph, is also needed in this graph-based modeling abstraction. A g-graph is represented as (Vg,Eg), where V8 is the set of vertices and Eg is the set of directed edges. The set V8 can be further partitioned into four disjoined sets, denoted as Sg, Dg, TP8, and TC8, i.e.,

The vertices in the four sets, Sg, Dg, TPs, and TC8, represent all suppliers, deman- ders, technology providers, and technology candidates, in the system. Suppliers provide raw materials into the supply chain system, and demanders (consumers) consume prod- ucts. Technology providers represent facilities that have already been installed and can be used for material conversion. Technology candidates represent nodes where additional facilities can be installed. Specifically, the set of technology candidates should always be empty for a supply chain management model since only existing technologies are allowed to use.

We use Si, di, tpi, and tci to represent elements in those sets. Similar to the p-graph, each node in the g-graph has data attached. Each supply node Si E S is defined with corresponding supply material Msi E Mp, a supply capacity Si, and a supply price Psi. Each demand node di is defined with corresponding supply material Mdi E Mp, a de- mand capacity di, and a supply price Pdi. Each technology provider tpi is specified by the installed technology ttp; E Tp and processing capacity Ctpi. Each technology candidate tci is specified by the technology that can be installed, ttci E Tp. We give an example of a small g-graph, containing 2 suppliers (gold), 2 demanders (green), 1 technology provider (blue), and 1 technology candidate (red) in Figure 5.4. Both suppliers offer material m1 but with different capacities and prices. The technology provider offers technology t1 that is able to convert m1 to m2 and M3. The technology candidate offers t2, which is able to convert m3 to M4. One demander can consume material m2 with a capacity of 200 kg per day at a price of 8 USD per kg of m2. Another demander can consume material m4 with a capacity of 320 kg per day at a price of 4 USD per kg of M4.
The set of edges Eg in a g-graph is determined when all vertices are defined with
Figure 5.4: Example of g-graph (vertices and data)

proper data. Specifically, an element in set Eg can be denoted as e = (01,02, p) and it represents a valid transportation route of product p from vertex V1 to V2. The set Eg in a g-graph can be further partitioned into four disjoined sets, denoted as Eg,S-D, Eg,S->T, Eg,T-D, and Eg,T->T, i,e.,
Eg,T->T = TS8 UTC, V2 TS8 UTCs, to2 = (az, B2), pEp,,PEa2}

Each demand node di is defined with corresponding supply material Mdi E Mp, a de- mand capacity di, and a supply price Pdi. Each technology provider tpi is specified by the installed technology ttp; E Tp and processing capacity Ctpi. Each technology candidate tci is specified by the technology that can be installed, ttci E Tp. We give an example of a small g-graph, containing 2 suppliers (gold), 2 demanders (green), 1 technology provider (blue), and 1 technology candidate (red) in Figure 5.4. Both suppliers offer material m1 but with different capacities and prices. The technology provider offers technology t1 that is able to convert m1 to m2 and M3. The technology candidate offers t2, which is able to convert m3 to M4. One demander can consume material m2 with a capacity of 200 kg per day at a price of 8 USD per kg of m2. Another demander can consume material m4 with a capacity of 320 kg per day at a price of 4 USD per kg of M4.
The set of edges Eg in a g-graph is determined when all vertices are defined with
Figure 5.4: Example of g-graph (vertices and data)

proper data. Specifically, an element in set Eg can be denoted as e = (01,02, p) and it represents a valid transportation route of product p from vertex V1 to V2. The set Eg in a g-graph can be further partitioned into four disjoined sets, denoted as Eg,S-D, Eg,S->T, Eg,T-D, and Eg,T->T, i,e.,
Eg,T->T = TS8 UTC, V2 TS8 UTCs, to2 = (az, B2), pEp,,PEa2}
Here those conditions imply that the product p can be directly transported from V1 to V2 if (1) V1 supplies p and V2 demands p; (2) V1 supplies p and V2 has (or can install)

some technology that takes p as feedstock; (3) V1 has (or can install) some technology that generates p as product and V2 demands p; (4) V1 has (or can install) some technology that generates p as product, and V2 has (or can install) some technology that takes p as feedstock.

Each edge e = (01,02, p) is associated with the transportation cost of moving one unit of material p along this edge, denoted as Ce. Typically, Ce can be computed as a linear function of the distance between V1 and V2 (DV1,02), i.e., Ce = D01,02 Ctrans,pr where Ctrans,p is the unit transportation cost of material p. The distance can be calculated if the geographical coordinates of vertices are specified, and the unit transportation cost can be treated as an attribute of the material.

The same example with edges and data is presented again in Figure 5.5. Material m1 can be transported from S1 or S2 to tp1, where technology t1 uses m1 as feedstock. Material m2 can be generated from t1 in tp1 and consumed by d1. Material m3 can be generated from t1 in tp1 and then used as a (potential) feedstock of t2 in tc1. Finally, material m4 can be potentially generated by t2 in tc and then consumed by d2. Each edge in g-graph is assigned the corresponding transportation cost. For a g-graph, it can completely replace the node set N in the mathematical formulation and define the geographical property in a simpler way. We also note that g-graph and p-graph are closely tied due to shared material and technology elements.
23: Given the model defined in Section 5.2.1, we note that only when the set of nodes N, set of materials P, and set of technologies T are properly defined; then the rest data, such as supply and demand capacities and pre-installed technologies, can be defined since these data have attributes that are coupled to those sets. For example, when we define a supply upper bound, we need to specify the location of the supply and the product supplied, and when we define a pre-installed technology, the technology type and the location where it is installed are also required. This indicates that, before the model is applied, the model builder needs to list all the geographical locations, materials, and technologies that are possibly used in the system. The construction and operation of sets are abstract and cum- bersome and usually require a background in systems engineering and programming. However, many real case studies involve the use of various technical, experimental, and economic data (e.g. product transformation, product price), which makes it crucial to establish collaborations between computational and experimental researchers.

Therefore, we propose a high-level graph-based supply chain modeling framework to compact the model building. The framework leverages two types of graphs: a process graph (p-graph) and ageographical graph (g-graph).

PROCESS GRAPH The process graph, or p-graph, is first proposed by Friedler et al. (1992, 1993) and is then widely applied in process synthesis and superstructure optimiza- tion (Varbanov et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2016; Voll et al., 2013). It is a directed, bipartite graph that is used to represent the process structure. Here, we provide a basic review of concepts related to p-graph, and then provide examples to show its use in supply chain optimization. For more details, readers can refer to the original publication.
A p-graph Gp is represented as (Vp,Ep), where Vp is the set of vertices and Ep is the set of edges. The set Vp can be further partitioned into two disjoined sets, denoted as Mp and Tp:

The vertices in Mp (denoted M vertices) represent all possible materials involved in a process, while the vertices in Tp (denoted T vertices) represent all possible technologies involved in a process. For simplicity, we use the following notation to represent elements in the two sets, where Mi and ti represent some material and technology in the process, respectively. Furthermore, a technology ti can be further represented by a tuple (Ati'l Bti), where Ati is the set of feedstock materials of the technology, and Bti is the set of product materials of the technology (Ati, Bti C Mp). The technology ti is capable of transforming materials in Ati to materials in Bti according to certain transformation rules (stoichiometry relationship).
The set Ep in the p-graph Gp can also be further partitioned into two disjoint sets, denoted as Ep,M->T and Ep,T->:

The set Ep,M-T includes edges that go from one feedstock of some technology to the technology, and the set Ep,T->M includes edges that go from some technology to one of its products. An edge can only exist if the corresponding transformation is physically achievable. It is now obvious that a p-graph always starts and ends on at least one M ver- tices. Specifically, M vertices with no input edges represent raw materials for the whole system, M vertices with no output edges represent final products, and M vertices with both input and output edges represent intermediate products. There are no isolated ver- tices in a p-graph; every vertex must be connected to at least one other vertex. Moreover, every two adjacent vertices V1 and V2, i.e., (U1, U2) E Ep, must be of different categories.

An example of a simple p-graph is given below in Figure 5.1, where Mp = {m1, M2, M3, m4} and Tp = {({m1},{m2,m3}),({m3},{m4})}. The graph represents a process structure where material m1 can be converted to m2 and m3 through technology t1 = ({m1}, {m2, m3}), and then intermediate products m3 can be further transformed to m4 by technology t2 = ({m3}, {m4}). In this example, vertices in set Mp are displayed as circles and vertices in set Tp are displayed as rectangles. Edges in set Ep,M-T are shown in black, and edges in set Ep,T->M are shown in blue.

We note that each p-graph defines a process structure, and additional data need to be attached to T vertices and edges. Each T vertex t E Tp is associated with information of processing capacity (upper bound Ct and lower bound Ct ), investment cost (fixed part Binv,t and proportional part Kinv,t), and operational cost (fixed part Bop,t and proportional
part Kinv,t). These data fully specify constraints (5.2.5)-(5.2.7).

Each arbitrary edge e = (01,02) E Ep contains data about the corresponding transfor- mation coefficient Ae. Specifically, if e E Ep,M->T, then we have ae = At,P where t = V1 and p = V2; otherwise, if e E Ep,T->m, then Ae = at,p where t=u2 and p = V1. Furthermore, the transformation coefficient of reference material of technology t in a p-graph is defined below. By default, we select the main feedstock of technology t, i.e., the input flow with largest absolute value of transformation coefficient, as the reference material. Thus, data in set Ep fully specify constraints (5.2.2).

The same example with data is presented below in Figure 5.2, where we can observe the transformation relationships among products. Every unit of material m1 can be con- verted to 0.2 unit of m2 and 0.8 unit of m3 through technology t1. And then for every unit of M3, it can be further transformed to m4 through technology t2. For technology t1, its operating capacity is 100-1000 units of m1 per year, with an investment cost of 1 million USD and operational cost of 100 USD per unit of M1. And similarly, for technology t2, its operating capacity is 80-800 unit of m3 per year, with an investment cost of 0.5 million USD and operational cost of 40 USD per unit of M3. We note that in this example, the values of transformation coefficients related to t2 do not balance. In reality, this can be caused by inconsistent unit measure, material loss or supplement in the technology (e.g., drying process), property change of the stream (e.g., pressure change of gas), etc.

We claim that a p-graph can completely replace the technology set T and material set P, and it can help model builders to define all possible material transformations in a supply chain system in a more intuitive way. Figure 5.3 is another example of p-graph for the livestcok waste supply chain case study reported in Sampat et al. (2019). In this case, the livestock waste can be separated into solid and liquid fractions. The solid fraction can further go into a granulation step, and the liquid fraction can be processed by a
subsequent struvite recovery technology as well.
Figure 5.3: Example of p-graph in livestock waste supply chain system

Each technology candidate tci is specified by the technology that can be installed, ttci E Tp. Another demander can consume material m4 with a capacity of 320 kg per day at a price of 4 USD per kg of M4.

Figure 5.3 is another example of p-graph for the livestcok waste supply chain case study reported in Sampat et al. (2019). In this case, the livestock waste can be separated into solid and liquid fractions. The solid fraction can further go into a granulation step, and the liquid fraction can be processed by a
subsequent struvite recovery technology as well.
Figure 5.3: Example of p-graph in livestock waste supply chain system

GEOGRAPHICAL GRAPH In addition to the p-graph which is used to represent product transformations, another type of graph, geographical graph, or g-graph, is also needed in this graph-based modeling abstraction. A g-graph is represented as (Vg,Eg), where V8 is the set of vertices and Eg is the set of directed edges. The set V8 can be further partitioned into four disjoined sets, denoted as Sg, Dg, TP8, and TC8, i.e.,

The vertices in the four sets, Sg, Dg, TPs, and TC8, represent all suppliers, deman- ders, technology providers, and technology candidates, in the system. Suppliers provide raw materials into the supply chain system, and demanders (consumers) consume prod- ucts. Technology providers represent facilities that have already been installed and can be used for material conversion. Technology candidates represent nodes where additional facilities can be installed. Specifically, the set of technology candidates should always be empty for a supply chain management model since only existing technologies are allowed to use.

We use Si, di, tpi, and tci to represent elements in those sets. Similar to the p-graph, each node in the g-graph has data attached. Each supply node Si E S is defined with corresponding supply material Msi E Mp, a supply capacity Si, and a supply price Psi. Each demand node di is defined with corresponding supply material Mdi E Mp, a de- mand capacity di, and a supply price Pdi. Each technology provider tpi is specified by the installed technology ttp; E Tp and processing capacity Ctpi. Each technology candidate tci is specified by the technology that can be installed, ttci E Tp. We give an example of a small g-graph, containing 2 suppliers (gold), 2 demanders (green), 1 technology provider (blue), and 1 technology candidate (red) in Figure 5.4. Both suppliers offer material m1 but with different capacities and prices. The technology provider offers technology t1 that is able to convert m1 to m2 and M3. The technology candidate offers t2, which is able to convert m3 to M4. One demander can consume material m2 with a capacity of 200 kg per day at a price of 8 USD per kg of m2. Another demander can consume material m4 with a capacity of 320 kg per day at a price of 4 USD per kg of M4.
The set of edges Eg in a g-graph is determined when all vertices are defined with
Figure 5.4: Example of g-graph (vertices and data)

proper data. Specifically, an element in set Eg can be denoted as e = (01,02, p) and it represents a valid transportation route of product p from vertex V1 to V2. The set Eg in a g-graph can be further partitioned into four disjoined sets, denoted as Eg,S-D, Eg,S->T, Eg,T-D, and Eg,T->T, i,e.,
Eg,T->T = TS8 UTC, V2 TS8 UTCs, to2 = (az, B2), pEp,,PEa2}
Here those conditions imply that the product p can be directly transported from V1 to V2 if (1) V1 supplies p and V2 demands p; (2) V1 supplies p and V2 has (or can install)

some technology that takes p as feedstock; (3) V1 has (or can install) some technology that generates p as product and V2 demands p; (4) V1 has (or can install) some technology that generates p as product, and V2 has (or can install) some technology that takes p as feedstock.

Each edge e = (01,02, p) is associated with the transportation cost of moving one unit of material p along this edge, denoted as Ce. Typically, Ce can be computed as a linear function of the distance between V1 and V2 (DV1,02), i.e., Ce = D01,02 Ctrans,pr where Ctrans,p is the unit transportation cost of material p. The distance can be calculated if the geographical coordinates of vertices are specified, and the unit transportation cost can be treated as an attribute of the material.

The same example with edges and data is presented again in Figure 5.5. Material m1 can be transported from S1 or S2 to tp1, where technology t1 uses m1 as feedstock. Material m2 can be generated from t1 in tp1 and consumed by d1. Material m3 can be generated from t1 in tp1 and then used as a (potential) feedstock of t2 in tc1. Finally, material m4 can be potentially generated by t2 in tc and then consumed by d2. Each edge in g-graph is assigned the corresponding transportation cost. For a g-graph, it can completely replace the node set N in the mathematical formulation and define the geographical property in a simpler way. We also note that g-graph and p-graph are closely tied due to shared material and technology elements.
26: Given the model defined in Section 5.2.1, we note that only when the set of nodes N, set of materials P, and set of technologies T are properly defined; then the rest data, such as supply and demand capacities and pre-installed technologies, can be defined since these data have attributes that are coupled to those sets. For example, when we define a supply upper bound, we need to specify the location of the supply and the product supplied, and when we define a pre-installed technology, the technology type and the location where it is installed are also required. This indicates that, before the model is applied, the model builder needs to list all the geographical locations, materials, and technologies that are possibly used in the system. The construction and operation of sets are abstract and cum- bersome and usually require a background in systems engineering and programming. However, many real case studies involve the use of various technical, experimental, and economic data (e.g. product transformation, product price), which makes it crucial to establish collaborations between computational and experimental researchers.

Therefore, we propose a high-level graph-based supply chain modeling framework to compact the model building. The framework leverages two types of graphs: a process graph (p-graph) and ageographical graph (g-graph).

PROCESS GRAPH The process graph, or p-graph, is first proposed by Friedler et al. (1992, 1993) and is then widely applied in process synthesis and superstructure optimiza- tion (Varbanov et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2016; Voll et al., 2013). It is a directed, bipartite graph that is used to represent the process structure. Here, we provide a basic review of concepts related to p-graph, and then provide examples to show its use in supply chain optimization. For more details, readers can refer to the original publication.
A p-graph Gp is represented as (Vp,Ep), where Vp is the set of vertices and Ep is the set of edges. The set Vp can be further partitioned into two disjoined sets, denoted as Mp and Tp:

The vertices in Mp (denoted M vertices) represent all possible materials involved in a process, while the vertices in Tp (denoted T vertices) represent all possible technologies involved in a process. For simplicity, we use the following notation to represent elements in the two sets, where Mi and ti represent some material and technology in the process, respectively. Furthermore, a technology ti can be further represented by a tuple (Ati'l Bti), where Ati is the set of feedstock materials of the technology, and Bti is the set of product materials of the technology (Ati, Bti C Mp). The technology ti is capable of transforming materials in Ati to materials in Bti according to certain transformation rules (stoichiometry relationship).
The set Ep in the p-graph Gp can also be further partitioned into two disjoint sets, denoted as Ep,M->T and Ep,T->:

The set Ep,M-T includes edges that go from one feedstock of some technology to the technology, and the set Ep,T->M includes edges that go from some technology to one of its products. An edge can only exist if the corresponding transformation is physically achievable. It is now obvious that a p-graph always starts and ends on at least one M ver- tices. Specifically, M vertices with no input edges represent raw materials for the whole system, M vertices with no output edges represent final products, and M vertices with both input and output edges represent intermediate products. There are no isolated ver- tices in a p-graph; every vertex must be connected to at least one other vertex. Moreover, every two adjacent vertices V1 and V2, i.e., (U1, U2) E Ep, must be of different categories.

An example of a simple p-graph is given below in Figure 5.1, where Mp = {m1, M2, M3, m4} and Tp = {({m1},{m2,m3}),({m3},{m4})}. The graph represents a process structure where material m1 can be converted to m2 and m3 through technology t1 = ({m1}, {m2, m3}), and then intermediate products m3 can be further transformed to m4 by technology t2 = ({m3}, {m4}). In this example, vertices in set Mp are displayed as circles and vertices in set Tp are displayed as rectangles. Edges in set Ep,M-T are shown in black, and edges in set Ep,T->M are shown in blue.

We note that each p-graph defines a process structure, and additional data need to be attached to T vertices and edges. Each T vertex t E Tp is associated with information of processing capacity (upper bound Ct and lower bound Ct ), investment cost (fixed part Binv,t and proportional part Kinv,t), and operational cost (fixed part Bop,t and proportional
part Kinv,t). These data fully specify constraints (5.2.5)-(5.2.7).

Each arbitrary edge e = (01,02) E Ep contains data about the corresponding transfor- mation coefficient Ae. Specifically, if e E Ep,M->T, then we have ae = At,P where t = V1 and p = V2; otherwise, if e E Ep,T->m, then Ae = at,p where t=u2 and p = V1. Furthermore, the transformation coefficient of reference material of technology t in a p-graph is defined below. By default, we select the main feedstock of technology t, i.e., the input flow with largest absolute value of transformation coefficient, as the reference material. Thus, data in set Ep fully specify constraints (5.2.2).

The same example with data is presented below in Figure 5.2, where we can observe the transformation relationships among products. Every unit of material m1 can be con- verted to 0.2 unit of m2 and 0.8 unit of m3 through technology t1. And then for every unit of M3, it can be further transformed to m4 through technology t2. For technology t1, its operating capacity is 100-1000 units of m1 per year, with an investment cost of 1 million USD and operational cost of 100 USD per unit of M1. And similarly, for technology t2, its operating capacity is 80-800 unit of m3 per year, with an investment cost of 0.5 million USD and operational cost of 40 USD per unit of M3. We note that in this example, the values of transformation coefficients related to t2 do not balance. In reality, this can be caused by inconsistent unit measure, material loss or supplement in the technology (e.g., drying process), property change of the stream (e.g., pressure change of gas), etc.

We claim that a p-graph can completely replace the technology set T and material set P, and it can help model builders to define all possible material transformations in a supply chain system in a more intuitive way. Figure 5.3 is another example of p-graph for the livestcok waste supply chain case study reported in Sampat et al. (2019). In this case, the livestock waste can be separated into solid and liquid fractions. The solid fraction can further go into a granulation step, and the liquid fraction can be processed by a
subsequent struvite recovery technology as well.
Figure 5.3: Example of p-graph in livestock waste supply chain system

28: With the discussion above, we propose an object-oriented supply chain modeling system, where the supply chain models are treated as model objects. The overall data structure is shown below in Figure 5.6. Each graph model object includes two graph objects, a p-graph and a g-graph. A p-graph is defined by its vertices and edges, where the vertices in a p-graph include the set of materials and the set of technologies, and the edges in a p-graph include the set of all transformation relationships (material - technology pair).

Specific materials, technologies, and transformation relationships are treated as objects as well. They have their corresponding attributes that need to be specified when defining the p-graph (e.g., for a technology, its capacity and cost data are required). A g-graph is mainly defined by its vertices, and then edges can be automatically generated based on (5.2.12)-(5.2.15). Vertices in a g-graph include the set of suppliers, demanders, tech- nology providers, and technology candidates. Each specific vertex also has its associated attributes when defining the g-graph as discussed above. Specifically, demand and sup- ply vertices require an attribute of material that is supplied or consumed, which points to an element in the material set in p-graph. And similarly, technology providers and candidates require an attribute of technology that is installed or can be installed, which points to an element in the technology set in the p-graph. This proposed data structure of the object-oriented graph-based supply chain models forms the key foundation of data storage and management systems in ADAM.
Object A is defined on object B

30: With the discussion above, we propose an object-oriented supply chain modeling system, where the supply chain models are treated as model objects. The overall data structure is shown below in Figure 5.6. Each graph model object includes two graph objects, a p-graph and a g-graph. A p-graph is defined by its vertices and edges, where the vertices in a p-graph include the set of materials and the set of technologies, and the edges in a p-graph include the set of all transformation relationships (material - technology pair).

Specific materials, technologies, and transformation relationships are treated as objects as well. They have their corresponding attributes that need to be specified when defining the p-graph (e.g., for a technology, its capacity and cost data are required). A g-graph is mainly defined by its vertices, and then edges can be automatically generated based on (5.2.12)-(5.2.15). Vertices in a g-graph include the set of suppliers, demanders, tech- nology providers, and technology candidates. Each specific vertex also has its associated attributes when defining the g-graph as discussed above. Specifically, demand and sup- ply vertices require an attribute of material that is supplied or consumed, which points to an element in the material set in p-graph. And similarly, technology providers and candidates require an attribute of technology that is installed or can be installed, which points to an element in the technology set in the p-graph. This proposed data structure of the object-oriented graph-based supply chain models forms the key foundation of data storage and management systems in ADAM.

32: From the proposed data architecture, we observe that materials and technologies are two fundamental elements in graph building and supply chain modeling. Specifically, vertices in a p-graph exactly represent materials and technologies, and vertices in a g- graph are built upon materials and technologies. Therefore, identifying valid materials and technologies is needed before constructing a graph-based supply chain model.

For some users with their specific background, although multiple supply chain case studies might be conducted, the set of materials and technologies can overlap by a big fraction from case to case due to the application area. For example, in the context of cow manure management supply chains, common materials include cow manure, biogas, di- gestate, solid cow manure, and liquid cow manure, and common technologies include anaerobic digestion, solid-liquid generation. Therefore, effort in supply chain modeling

can be reduced if materials and technologies are stored and managed properly SO that users can directly extract items from a material or technology pool. Inspired by this, we implement a material and technology database system in ADAM. Materials and technolo- gies are stored in the corresponding tables in database software, with each object being a row in the table and each attribute as a column. Apart from the attributes displayed in Figure 5.6, some additional auxiliary attributes are also defined in the table, such as material descriptions, technology unit, measuring unit, etc., to improve user experience and data management. Transformation relationships are also stored in an implicit ta- ble that connects materials and technologies. Each item in the transformation table has three columns: material, technology, and transformation coefficient, where the first two columns act as foreign keys pointing to the material and technology tables, respectively. An illustration of the database system is shown in Figure 5.7, where item 1 in transforma- tion table means that, the transformation coefficient of material m1 in t1 is - -1, meaning that m1 is the main feedstock (and reference material) of t1.
Figure 5.7: Material and technology database in ADAM

We note that the ADAM database contains two parts, a public database system and a private database system. The public database system is maintained by ADAM admin users and managers; all materials and technologies contained as well as their associated data are added from peer-reviewed literature. The public database can only be modified by admin users in order to keep the high quality of data. On the other hand, each user can

modify items in their private database system arbitrarily. When building a graph-based supply chain model, users have access to both public and private databases (Figure 5.8). Currently, the public database mainly contains materials and technologies in the field of organic waste management.

36: The previously discussed database system defines all possible technologies, materials, and transformation relationships that can be deployed during the supply chain modeling. With that, users are not required to provide a complete p-graph every time a supply chain model is being constructed. Instead, ADAM directly pulls out corresponding materials, technologies, transformation relationships, and associated data from the database and builds up the p-graph implicitly when the user specifies related information.

ADAM contains a visualized, interactive interface for users to build up g-graphs for supply chain models. The interface employs an open-source mapping system, Open- StreetMap (Haklay and Weber, 2008), where users can place any type of geographical vertices. For each vertex, in addition to the attributes defined previously, ADAM further requires geographical coordinates for transportation distance estimation (Goetschalckx, 2011). In Figure 5.9, we show an example of a g-graph (nodes only) in ADAM interface.
In this figure, we have three supply nodes, four demand nodes, two technology providers, and one technology candidate, indicated by yellow, green, blue, and red markers, respec- tively.
Leaflet Map data OpenStreetMap under ODbL
Figure 5.9: Example of a g-graph (nodes only) in ADAM interface

To reduce complexity, the g-graph construction is decomposed into three steps in ADAM, namely supply specification, technology specification, and demand specifica- tion. Based on a specific application, users are required to identify the materials used as feedstocks and place them as suppliers with required attributes. ADAM is able to auto- matically identify feasible technology pathways from the database that enable the process the feedstock. Among those feasible technologies, users will specify the ones of interest in the second step and place them as technology providers or technology candidates. In the third step, users need to specify demand data from a list of products provided by ADAM. As users gradually put different materials and technologies into the supply chain when constructing the g-graph, the corresponding p-graph is built up incrementally and automatically by ADAM behind the scene (Figure 5.10).

After all vertices in a g-graph are properly defined, edges can be generated automat- ically by ADAM to represent all possible transportation routes based on rules (5.2.12)- (5.2.15). Figure 5.11 shows the complete g-graph for the example in Figure 5.9. For clarity, the left subfigure shows routes for feedstock only, and the right subfigure shows

routes for derived products in the system with different colors. Users are also allowed to remove any unrealistic edges or use a distance filter to only keep edges shorter than some predefined distance limit.
Leaflet Map data OpenStreetMap under ODbL
Leaflet Map data OpenStreetMap under ODbL
Figure 5.11: Example of a complete g-graph (nodes and edges)

41: <header>5.3.3 Model Management </header>

A supply chain model is successfully established once its associated p-graph and g-graph are constructed. A specific model is stored as a g-graph only, and the corresponding table contains all g-graph components and their attributes. The p-graph of that model can be easily reconstructed by extracting materials and technologies from g-graph vertices attributes. Similar to materials and technologies, ADAM provides auxiliary attributes to
help users manage their model objects, such as model name and description, etc.

ADAM provides methods defined on top of the model objects. A specific model can be easily duplicated with its associated p-graph and g-graph copied. This allows users to conduct parametric studies by copying a baseline model and modifying components in those graphs. Users are also allowed to merge two models, and ADAM will automatically take the union of the corresponding g-graphs and reconstruct the p-graph.

We note that the stored models are also divided into public models and private mod- els. Public models (case studies) use materials and technologies from the public database only, and any user can view and make a copy of those public models and modify their data. This feature provides a modeling template for new users to shorten the learning curve. On the other hand, users are allowed to build private models by using materials and technologies from public and private databases. A private model can only be modi- fied by its owner. Admin users have the right to make a copy of any private model and then publish it as a case study if the p-graph uses the public database (Figure 5.12).

42: <header>5.3.3 Model Management </header>

A supply chain model is successfully established once its associated p-graph and g-graph are constructed. A specific model is stored as a g-graph only, and the corresponding table contains all g-graph components and their attributes. The p-graph of that model can be easily reconstructed by extracting materials and technologies from g-graph vertices attributes. Similar to materials and technologies, ADAM provides auxiliary attributes to
help users manage their model objects, such as model name and description, etc.

ADAM provides methods defined on top of the model objects. A specific model can be easily duplicated with its associated p-graph and g-graph copied. This allows users to conduct parametric studies by copying a baseline model and modifying components in those graphs. Users are also allowed to merge two models, and ADAM will automatically take the union of the corresponding g-graphs and reconstruct the p-graph.

We note that the stored models are also divided into public models and private mod- els. Public models (case studies) use materials and technologies from the public database only, and any user can view and make a copy of those public models and modify their data. This feature provides a modeling template for new users to shorten the learning curve. On the other hand, users are allowed to build private models by using materials and technologies from public and private databases. A private model can only be modi- fied by its owner. Admin users have the right to make a copy of any private model and then publish it as a case study if the p-graph uses the public database (Figure 5.12).

43: <header>5.3.4 Software Architecture </header>

ADAM is implemented as an online tool to provide easy access to wide users. It is de- veloped using a high-level python-based (Python 3.7) website development framework, Django (version 2.0.0+). With Django, ADAM can directly communicate with database software and render HTML templates. On the back-end, the database software used by default is MySQL (8.0.0+). PostgreSQL (12.0+) has also been tested and is compatible. The mathematical optimization is implemented using JuMP in Julia (1.0.0+). The opti- mization solver used is Clp (for linear programming) and Cbc (for mixed-integer linear programming), both of which are efficient, open-source solvers. ADAM also has a simple router on the back-end to provide true transportation routes for trucks, which is built upon the OSRM program.

On the front-end, we mainly use Bootstrap (4.3.1+) and JQuery (1.12.1+) for enhanc- ing the functionality of webpage styling and interactiveness. Leaflet (1.6.0+) and related additional plugins are used for implementing the geographical mapping system. Other auxiliary packages include AlertifyJS, Draw2D, Ionicons, etc. Figure 5.13 summarizes the packages used during the development of ADAM.

The code of ADAM is all open-source on GitHub. Currently, we have a demo version of ADAM online, which is host on Amazon Web Service using the LightSail module (http://54.208.179.171:8000). With all code available to the public, we allow users to download the ADAM packages and install a local server with provided local installation guidance on GitHub. With this, users are able to run all models on their local machine or local network. Users are also encouraged to modify the code based on their needs, e.g. to replace open-source optimization solvers with more advanced ones.

The first example is a supply chain management model about using livestock waste to generate electricity. The data in this example are predefined and the model is pre-solved. Once users are familiar with the general steps in model building, they can click on the DASHBOARD button and then register for ADAM. 49: <header>5.4.2 Model Building </header>

Users can choose to build a model from an existing case study or from a blank template. Our published case studies provide all datasets and comprehensive documentation, and users can learn the background information of the model, the detailed reasoning of setting p-graph, g-graphs, and corresponding data. Once a case study is published, users have the freedom to conduct parametric studies, i.e., modify and resolve the model and then compare with public results. For more advanced users who want to build up customized supply chain models based on their needs, an alternative model building method that ADAM provides is to create a new, blank model. Before building the model, users are required to enter the needed materials and technologies into the database system, SO that g-graph can extract that information when specifying supply, demand, and technology.

The model building is divided into six steps in total, which are the same as the tutorial examples. The first step is to specify the model type, where two options are available: supply chain management and supply chain design. A management model can only use technology providers to achieve material transformation, while a design model allows to install new technologies at technology candidates to process materials. In steps 2-4, supply, technology, and demand data are required. ADAM provides several options for data input, including manual input online, upload via CSV data files, and data load from another model. For the manual option, ADAM provides an empty form with fields of geographical locations and g-graph vertex attributes for users to fill. For data upload, ADAM provides a template CSV file where users can put their data, and with that for- mat, ADAM can compile the file into graph components. In Figure 5.16(a) and 5.16(b), we show screenshots of supply data specification by manual input and data upload, respec- tively. In each step, ADAM conducts a data validity check to make sure all input data are of the correct format and valid for use (e.g., supply price and capacity should be float numbers). In step 5, transportation routes will be generated automatically by ADAM and the model is ready for execution.

52: <header>5.4.3 Model Solving and Visualization </header>

Once all data are correctly set, the "Run the model" button in step 6 will be activated and users can execute the model by simply clicking it. Two emails will be sent to users separately when the model starts to run and finishes solving.

Once the model finishes, ADAM reports the status of the model of either completed or error (which usually arises from unbounded or infeasible model due to improper input data). In the former case, ADAM reports the systematic performance of the supply chain, including the overall social welfare, total revenue, supply cost, transportation cost, tech- nology investment cost, and technology operational cost. The detailed model results will be available in the ADAM visualization tool, where users can view the optimal supply chain setup, such as optimal technology placement and processing capacity, supply and demand amount, and transportation routes. For the supply chain management model, the clearing price of each product is also retrieved from the solver and plotted as a colored contour map. An example of result visualization for a simple model in ADAM is given in Figure 5.17, where the optimal transportation routes of three materials are plotted as
directed lines with different colors, and the price map of cow manure is displayed. Other results can be viewed by double-clicking the corresponding g-graph components.

55: In this section, we give a brief introduction of two application examples. For each one, we show its corresponding p-graph and g-graph, introduce about data setting, and have a short discussion on the model results.
56: Livestock waste can be used for producing biogas through the technology anaerobic di- gestion (AD). The generated biogas is commonly used for electricity generation. However, AD systems in the US are constantly facing financial barriers, mainly due to high uncer- tainty in investment and operations, as well as the relatively low price of output products in current markets. To motivate the use of renewable energy, the U.S. provides an incen- tive called RECs (Renewable Energy Certificates). Under this scheme, every unit (MWh) of renewable electricity generated receives a REC, and the producer of renewable energy can then sell the RECs either in a compliance market or in a voluntary market (Sampat et al., 2018b).

In this example, we examine the impact of REC value on a dairy manure supply chain and the installation of AD systems in Upper Rock River Watershed, Wisconsin. The p- graph and g-graph of are shown in Figure 5.18(a) and 5.18(b), respectively. Cow manure is collected by CAFOs (concentrated animal feeding operations, large farms with more than 1000 animal units), and can be used for AD and electricity generation. For 1 metric tonne of cow manure, 31.2 kWh of electricity and 0.98 metric tonne of digestate will be generated. In addition, a hypothetical product is also produced along with renewable energy, i.e., 0.03 unit of RECs. We note that this is a common modeling technique that can be applied to capture additional benefits or impacts of interest of a process, such as carbon emissions and water consumption. The technology cost information is collected from literature. In the g-graph, 22 CAFOs in the study region are included. Each CAFO acts as a supplier, technology candidate, and demander simultaneously. The supply amount of cow manure is estimated by the farm size. The CAFOs can consume electricity onsite with a market value (0.08 USD/kWh) and digestate for land application. The hypothetical product, RECs, are also assumed to be sold locally with some specified incentive price.
Leaflet Map data OpenStreetMap under ODbL

The supply chain design of two REC incentive values, o and 100 USD/MWh, are shown in Figure 5.19(a) and 5.19(b), respectively. From the results, we can observe that without any government incentive, the technology will not be installed since the process is not profitable. All farms only act as a supplier and demander of raw cow manure,

indicating that the cow manure is collected and then directly land applied. When the value of RECs is increased to 100 USD/MWh, all manure in the study region is used for electricity generation, and most CAFOs (18 out of 22) are installed with the technology. Other farms do not install because they have neighbor CAFOs nearby, and transporting manure is cheaper than investing a new technology.

However, AD systems in the US are constantly facing financial barriers, mainly due to high uncer- tainty in investment and operations, as well as the relatively low price of output products in current markets. To motivate the use of renewable energy, the U.S. provides an incen- tive called RECs (Renewable Energy Certificates). Under this scheme, every unit (MWh) of renewable electricity generated receives a REC, and the producer of renewable energy can then sell the RECs either in a compliance market or in a voluntary market (Sampat et al., 2018b).

60: During the last seven decades, the production of plastic products and plastic waste have grown at an extraordinary rate. Traditionally, plastic waste accumulates in landfills since it rarely degrades under natural conditions. Recently, researchers developed various path- ways to extract more valuable products from plastic waste, such as energy and recycled plastic.

In this example, a plastic waste supply chain for polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is discussed. The p-graph of the supply chain is shown in Figure 5.20(a). The suppliers send PET pellets to a bottle production technology that produces empty PET bottles. Those bottles are filled with beverages at some bottle filling facility. Then, the bottled beverages are purchased and consumed by customers, after which the remaining empty bottles are discarded. The discarded bottles go through a materials recovery facility (MRF), to sort

the plastics. The products can be sent to landfills directly, be used in a waste-to-energy (WTE) technology, or in a reclaimer technology. The WTE technology converts the plastic into electricity using heat, and the reclaimer technology recycles recovers rPET (recycled PET) pellets. rPET pellets are used in clothing industries due to reduced quality. The g-graph is shown in Figure 5.20(b), where we consider the beverage consumption in south Wisconsin. Most PET pellets are generated outside of the state, while the material transformation happens within the state. The detailed placement of g-graph vertices and corresponding data are obtained from the literature (Schneider et al., 2010; Rudolph et al., 2020; Duffy, 2016). The value of electricity and rPET can be tuned to encourage the system to utilize plastic waste instead of sending it to landfills.
Leaflet Map data OpenStreetMap under ODbL
Leaflet Map data OpenStreetMap under ODbL
Two supply chain designs are shown in Figure 5.21, for different price scenarios of rPET, where lines with different colors represent transportations for different materials in

the p-graph. Under market price (500 USD per metric tonne), rPET is not a competitive product comparing to electricity or direct landfill. When its price increases to 1000 USD per metric tonne, the reclaimer technologies are activated. For detailed documentation and results, readers are encouraged to view the plastic waste case study in ADAM.
Figure 5.21: Example 2 results with different rPET prices (a) 500 USD/metric tonne (b) 1000 USD/metric tonne

63: In this work, we present a web-based tool ADAM that facilitates the modeling and so- lution of multi-product supply chain models. ADAM provides a highly visualized and interactive platform with the use of a compact graph-based modeling abstraction to im- prove the user-friendliness of model building. The proposed graph-based model uses a p-graph and a g-graph to fully represent the data and relationships defined in the math- ematical formulation of the multi-product supply chain model. The hierarchy of supply chain model, p-graph and g-graph, vertices and edges provides the possibility of achiev- ing a scalable data and object management system. The ADAM technology and material database and model management system efficiently improve the flexibility, convenience, and modularity of model building. The embedded GIS visualization tool can quickly display supply chain optimization results quickly and interactively. A simple tutorial and two case studies are given to further show the capability of ADAM.
CASE STUDY IN ADAM: PRETREATMENT OF ANAEROBIC DIGESTION
64: Livestock manure has long been identified as a major source of environmental pollution, which calls for alternative practices for manure management that are both economically feasible and environmentally beneficial. Processing manure to biogas through anaerobic digestion recovers the energy that contributes minimal net carbon to the atmosphere and reduces the risk from land spreading of pathogens (Akbulut, 2012). Anaerobic digestion not only stabilizes organic matter in animal manure, but also recovers energy in the form of biogas and reduces environmental impact of manure application (i.e., reduction of greenhouse gas emissions) (MacDonald, 2009). Despite significant environmental benefits associated with manure processing, the recovery of value-added products from livestock manure is not commonplace due to high investment costs and low market values being offered for the recovered products (Gloy and Dressler, 2010; Yousuf et al., 2016).

Researchers have explored various methods to strengthen the competitiveness of anaer- obic digestion process, including co-digestion (Angelidaki and Ellegaard, 2003; Gelegenis et al., 2007), applying policy incentives (Sampat et al., 2018b), the addition of biochar

(Masebinu et al., 2019), and pretreatment of manure (Ardic et al., 2005; Bolado-Rodríguez et al., 2016; Jönsson and Martín, 2016; Li et al., 2013). Recent experimental results have shown that proper pretreatment methods can enhance the fermentation process of dairy manure and significantly increase (2-3 fold) the biogas yield (Kim and Karthikeyan, 2021). To identify the economic feasibility of these pretreatment methods, techno-economic anal- ysis needs to be conducted to quantify the trade-off between increased revenue and poten- tial additional equipment and operational expenses. In addition, transportation logistics to process animal manure needs to be worked out due to significant diversity in the size and nature of animal production systems.

Techno-economic analyses of biomass pretreatment systems often lack basis for direct comparison due to varying properties of the raw materials (i.e., feedstock) and system design (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006). Many studies have focused on crop-based biogas systems (Bohn et al., 2007; Gerin et al., 2008; Heller et al., 2004), different waste man- agement strategies (Cherubini et al., 2009; Zappini et al., 2010), and on specific biogas utilization options (Börjesson and Mattiasson, 2008). The aim of this study is to present a comparative analysis on the technical and economic performance of various pretreatment technologies that can produce accurate and reliable evaluation for dairy farms incorpo- rating anaerobic digester units.

In the current study, various dairy manure pretreatment strategies were directly eval- uated, including (a) no pretreatment, (b) acid using hydrochloric acid (HCI), (c) alkaline using sodium hydroxide (NaOH), (d) sulfite (SPORL), and (e) alkaline hydrogen peroxide (AHP). For each pretreatment strategy, two different pretreatment severities (i.e., moder- ate and severe) were considered. For each pretreatment scenario, a techno-economic analysis was conducted by estimating the corresponding mass (i.e., manure and biogas) and energy balance using a combination of experimental and simulation data. Our anal- ysis computed capital investment and annual production cost of pretreatment, anaerobic digestion, biogas cleanup (i.e., H2S removal), and combined heat and power (CHP) unit. To identify the most cost-effective pretreatment technology for multiple value-added end
products, three different biogas utilization scenarios were considered: (a) electricity, (b) biomethane, and (c) liquefied biomethane.

A supply chain optimization framework was also used to test the impact of logistical issues. The Upper Yahara Watershed in southern Wisconsin where the city of Madison and Lake Mendota are located, was used as the study watershed due to the presence of intense dairy production activities. The supply chain modeling and analysis is achieved in ADAM, as introduced in Chapter 5.
