68: <header>6.1 Introduction </header>

Livestock manure has long been identified as a major source of environmental pollution, which calls for alternative practices for manure management that are both economically feasible and environmentally beneficial. Processing manure to biogas through anaerobic digestion recovers the energy that contributes minimal net carbon to the atmosphere and reduces the risk from land spreading of pathogens (Akbulut, 2012). Anaerobic digestion not only stabilizes organic matter in animal manure, but also recovers energy in the form of biogas and reduces environmental impact of manure application (i.e., reduction of greenhouse gas emissions) (MacDonald, 2009). Despite significant environmental benefits associated with manure processing, the recovery of value-added products from livestock manure is not commonplace due to high investment costs and low market values being offered for the recovered products (Gloy and Dressler, 2010; Yousuf et al., 2016).

Researchers have explored various methods to strengthen the competitiveness of anaer- obic digestion process, including co-digestion (Angelidaki and Ellegaard, 2003; Gelegenis et al., 2007), applying policy incentives (Sampat et al., 2018b), the addition of biochar

(Masebinu et al., 2019), and pretreatment of manure (Ardic et al., 2005; Bolado-Rodríguez et al., 2016; Jönsson and Martín, 2016; Li et al., 2013). Recent experimental results have shown that proper pretreatment methods can enhance the fermentation process of dairy manure and significantly increase (2-3 fold) the biogas yield (Kim and Karthikeyan, 2021). To identify the economic feasibility of these pretreatment methods, techno-economic anal- ysis needs to be conducted to quantify the trade-off between increased revenue and poten- tial additional equipment and operational expenses. In addition, transportation logistics to process animal manure needs to be worked out due to significant diversity in the size and nature of animal production systems.

Techno-economic analyses of biomass pretreatment systems often lack basis for direct comparison due to varying properties of the raw materials (i.e., feedstock) and system design (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006). Many studies have focused on crop-based biogas systems (Bohn et al., 2007; Gerin et al., 2008; Heller et al., 2004), different waste man- agement strategies (Cherubini et al., 2009; Zappini et al., 2010), and on specific biogas utilization options (Börjesson and Mattiasson, 2008). The aim of this study is to present a comparative analysis on the technical and economic performance of various pretreatment technologies that can produce accurate and reliable evaluation for dairy farms incorpo- rating anaerobic digester units.

In the current study, various dairy manure pretreatment strategies were directly eval- uated, including (a) no pretreatment, (b) acid using hydrochloric acid (HCI), (c) alkaline using sodium hydroxide (NaOH), (d) sulfite (SPORL), and (e) alkaline hydrogen peroxide (AHP). For each pretreatment strategy, two different pretreatment severities (i.e., moder- ate and severe) were considered. For each pretreatment scenario, a techno-economic analysis was conducted by estimating the corresponding mass (i.e., manure and biogas) and energy balance using a combination of experimental and simulation data. Our anal- ysis computed capital investment and annual production cost of pretreatment, anaerobic digestion, biogas cleanup (i.e., H2S removal), and combined heat and power (CHP) unit. To identify the most cost-effective pretreatment technology for multiple value-added end
products, three different biogas utilization scenarios were considered: (a) electricity, (b) biomethane, and (c) liquefied biomethane.

A supply chain optimization framework was also used to test the impact of logistical issues. The Upper Yahara Watershed in southern Wisconsin where the city of Madison and Lake Mendota are located, was used as the study watershed due to the presence of intense dairy production activities. The supply chain modeling and analysis is achieved in ADAM, as introduced in Chapter 5.
69: Methods of dairy manure biomass pretreatment and anaerobic digestion were replicated from previously reported studies (Kim and Karthikeyan, 2021), as shown in Table 6.1. Four different farm sizes were considered (i.e., 1,000, 2,500, 5,000, and 10,000 animal units, or AUs) to capture the impact of scale, with each AU producing 25 metric tons of manure annually (Larson et al., 2016). Manure collection and land application system was assumed to be established at the dairy farms, and no capital investment involved was considered. The main components of the proposed process are the pretreatment unit, anaerobic digester, biogas cleanup, and CHP unit.
Table 6.1: Summary of CH4 yield values used in this study. Units are in mL-CH4/g-VS.
PRETREATMENT The equipment cost of the pretreatment mixer was estimated based on the sizing of the reactor (Biegler et al., 1997), which was calculated from the annual

flow rate of manure slurry and additive chemicals. The base cost of the digester tank was calculated using the power law method. The power law has been developed as a rule of thumb for obtaining approximate costs using the ratio of equipment sizes (González et al., 2020). For the price of each chemical involved in the pretreatment strategy, the average value available on the Chembid platform (https://www.chembid.com was used, as shown in Table 6.2. The costs associated with irradiation (for sulfite pretreatment) were calculated based on an economic analysis on microwave processing (Hasna, 2011). The cost of an industrial-scale microwave equipment was estimated as 2,450 USD per kW. Operational and maintenance costs for the microwave were also estimated assuming that the irradiation system requires electricity and a generator (i.e., magnetron) replacement every 5,000 hours of operation.
Table 6.2: Price of chemicals used in cost analysis.
Chemical|Price (USD/metric tonne|Form HCI|266|37% solution NaOH|392|solid H2SO4|519|98% solution NaSO3|303|solid H2O2|350|50% solution

ANAEROBIC DIGESTER The cost of a digester was estimated using the reported cases of anaerobic digestion systems on U.S. livestock production facilities (Beddoes et al., 2007). The published values were regressed using a 0.6 power law to obtain the relationship between manure flowrate and digester cost. Annual general operational cost including maintenance and labor was assumed to be 7.5% of the investment cost (Beddoes et al., 2007). The estimated cost for each farm size is validated using reported data (Lazarus and Rudstrom, 2007; Elsasser, 2006; Budzianowski and Budzianowska, 2015)

BIOGAS CLEANUP AND UPGRADING The major contaminant in biogas is hydrogen sulfide (H2S). H2S is always present in biogas, normally at concentrations between 80- 4,000 ppm depending on the feedstock (Allegue and Hinge, 2014). H2S is not only corro-

sive to most equipment, but it can also lead to sulfur dioxide emissions when combusted. Due to its potential problems to the instrumentation and environment, H2S is recom- mended to be removed from biogas. Several technologies exist to remove H2S from gas streams and increase the quality of raw biogas before utilizing it as an energy source.

Three biogas utilization scenarios were considered: clean/upgrade biogas and sell as (a) electricity, (b) biomethane, and (c) liquefied biomethane (Sheets and Shah, 2018). The process flow diagram for each scenario is shown in Figure 6.1. In each scenario, biogas cleaning and upgrading technologies are needed to remove components such as CO2, H2S, and H2O. To convert biogas into electricity, biogas needs to be desulphurized then processed through a CHP unit. Costs for the activated carbon desulphurization process (i.e., Siloxa) and CHP unit were estimated based on a previously reported study (Kvist, 2011; Kaufman et al., 2020; Lantz, 2012b). The amount of electricity generated is based on the CH4 content of the biogas and a heating value of 1,000 BTU/ft3 (37.3 MJ/m3) for CH4. The produced biogas has 55% CH4 on a volume basis (Zulkefli et al., 2016), which was similar to the value obtained in our batch experiments conducted with dairy manure collected from this study watershed. Assuming a thermal efficiency of 30%, one kWh of electricity generation corresponds to 12 ft3 of CH4.

An alternative to generating electricity is the recovery of biomethane. Biomethane is obtained after the biogas is upgraded to remove H2O, H2S, and CO2 to achieve the purity of pipeline quality natural gas. This biomethane can then be injected into the natural gas pipeline infrastructure. Water scrubbing was selected as the biogas upgrading technology, and the associated costs were estimated based on a previous study (Sun et al., 2015). Once biogas is upgraded, the purified biomethane can be compressed and stored as liquefied biomethane. Liquification of gaseous biomethane requires a single mixed refrigerant (SMR) process, and the capital and operational costs involved in liquification were estimated from Capra et al. (2019).
Dairy Manure|Scrubber|Compressor Dairy Manure|Biogas AD H2S|Liquified Biomethane
Figure 6.1: Biogas utilization scenarios and process schemes

The estimated cost for each farm size is validated using reported data (Lazarus and Rudstrom, 2007; Elsasser, 2006; Budzianowski and Budzianowska, 2015)

BIOGAS CLEANUP AND UPGRADING The major contaminant in biogas is hydrogen sulfide (H2S). H2S is always present in biogas, normally at concentrations between 80- 4,000 ppm depending on the feedstock (Allegue and Hinge, 2014). H2S is not only corro-

sive to most equipment, but it can also lead to sulfur dioxide emissions when combusted. Due to its potential problems to the instrumentation and environment, H2S is recom- mended to be removed from biogas. Several technologies exist to remove H2S from gas streams and increase the quality of raw biogas before utilizing it as an energy source.

Three biogas utilization scenarios were considered: clean/upgrade biogas and sell as (a) electricity, (b) biomethane, and (c) liquefied biomethane (Sheets and Shah, 2018). The process flow diagram for each scenario is shown in Figure 6.1. In each scenario, biogas cleaning and upgrading technologies are needed to remove components such as CO2, H2S, and H2O. To convert biogas into electricity, biogas needs to be desulphurized then processed through a CHP unit. Costs for the activated carbon desulphurization process (i.e., Siloxa) and CHP unit were estimated based on a previously reported study (Kvist, 2011; Kaufman et al., 2020; Lantz, 2012b). The amount of electricity generated is based on the CH4 content of the biogas and a heating value of 1,000 BTU/ft3 (37.3 MJ/m3) for CH4. The produced biogas has 55% CH4 on a volume basis (Zulkefli et al., 2016), which was similar to the value obtained in our batch experiments conducted with dairy manure collected from this study watershed. Assuming a thermal efficiency of 30%, one kWh of electricity generation corresponds to 12 ft3 of CH4.

An alternative to generating electricity is the recovery of biomethane. Biomethane is obtained after the biogas is upgraded to remove H2O, H2S, and CO2 to achieve the purity of pipeline quality natural gas. This biomethane can then be injected into the natural gas pipeline infrastructure. Water scrubbing was selected as the biogas upgrading technology, and the associated costs were estimated based on a previous study (Sun et al., 2015). Once biogas is upgraded, the purified biomethane can be compressed and stored as liquefied biomethane. Liquification of gaseous biomethane requires a single mixed refrigerant (SMR) process, and the capital and operational costs involved in liquification were estimated from Capra et al. (2019).

75: Figure 6.2 illustrates the break down of annual expenses under different moderate pre- treatment strategies when utilizing biogas for electricity generation at a dairy farm with 5,000 AUs. When no pretreatment is applied (i.e., Control; raw untreated dairy manure used for anaerobic digestion), general operational costs involved with the anaerobic diges- tion technology become the major contributor (31%) of the annual expense. The results are similar even with moderate-acid pretreatment. However, when moderate-alkaline and moderate-AHP pretreatments are applied, the cost of chemicals involved in each pre- treatment strategy becomes the major contributor to the total annual expense. In fact, with AHP pretreatment, the chemical costs account for approximately 51% of the total annual expense. Figure 6.3 shows the break down of annual expenses under different biogas utilization scenarios. For simplicity and for having the lowest breakeven cost in all three biogas utilization scenarios, the severe-alkaline pretreatment at a dairy farm with

1,000 AUs was considered. Biogas cleanup cost was the major contributor (30%) in the biomethane scenario, while biogas cleanup cost accounted for only 7% and 20% in the electricity and liquefied biomethane scenarios, respectively.

78: <header>6.2.3 Breakeven Prices </header>

The value of the three end-products considered were gradually increased to determine the price at which a specific scenario (i.e., pretreatment severity, pretreatment method, farm size, end-product) becomes economically feasible in terms of net present value assuming a lifespan of 20 years and interest rate of 5%. The calculation of this breakeven cost was performed by considering the total capital investment as well as annual revenue. The total annual operating cost was estimated by accounting for maintenance, utilities, labor, taxes, insurance, depreciation, and general expenses (Seider et al., 2009).

The breakeven cost corresponding to each scenario is shown in Table 6.3 and com- pared to the market value of each end-product (Krich et al., 2005; Sampat et al., 2018b). The breakeven cost for the Control (i.e., no pretreatment) showed a decreasing trend as farm size increased, yet always higher than the market value of the end-product. Acid (moderate), alkaline (moderate and severe), and AHP (moderate and severe) pretreat- ments showed promising results with reduced breakeven cost compared to the Control, whereas acid (severe) and SPORL (moderate and severe) pretreatments resulted in greater breakeven cost than the Control. While pretreatments can yield a significant increase in biogas production and reduce the breakeven cost of a product (compared to Control), the anaerobic digestion systems (with or without pretreatment) were in general not prof- itable. In any given scenario, the breakeven cost of a product was greater than the market value. However, the breakeven costs approached the market values as the dairy farm sizes increased.

At dairy farms with 1,000 AUs, severe-alkaline pretreatment was the most economical option, regardless of the biogas utilization scenario (i.e., end-product). At dairy farms with 2,500 AU, severe-alkaline pretreatment remained to be the most economical option along with moderate-acid pretreatment resulting in the same breakeven cost for Electricity and Biomethane scenarios. However, as the farm size increased to larger than 5,000 AUs, moderate-acid pretreatment turned out to be the most economical option in any biogas
Table 6.3: Breakeven price of product for each biogas utilization scenario under different pretreatment severity and dairy farm size. Units for the end-products follow the market value.
utilization scenario.

The breakeven costs of acid (severe) and SPORL (moderate and severe) pretreatments were higher than the Control regardless of the farm size. While the high cost from severe- acid pretreatment was caused by an extremely low CH4 yield due to furan inhibition, the high breakeven cost for SPORL (moderate and severe) pretreatments can be attributed to the costs associated with irradiation. To provide cost analysis for individual process components, a detailed breakdown of capital investment and operational costs involved in SPORL pretreatment (i.e., moderate-SPORL/1,000AU/electricity scenario) is shown in Figure 6.4. As illustrated, expenses related to microwave operation were the major con- tributors of the annual expense. Based on their extremely high breakeven costs of the end-products, acid (severe) and SPORL (moderate and severe) pretreatments are disre- garded from subsequent analysis and discussion.

While the breakeven prices (severe-alkaline, 1,000 AUs) for electricity and liquefied biomethane are 287% and 339% of the market value, respectively, the breakeven price for biomethane is 626% of the market value. Even with liquefied biomethane requiring an

additional liquefication process, the breakeven price was closer to the market value than the biomethane scenario. However, it should be noted that liquefied biomethane systems are most likely to require a storage unit where temperature and pressure should be con- trolled. In the current study, costs involved in installation and maintenance of a storage unit was not considered in the liquefied biomethane scenario. Adding a storage unit would only add extra capital, and thus would not impact the conclusion that electricity is the most economical option.

Figure 6.4: Breakdown of capital investment and operational costs involved in moderate- SPORL pretreatment with 1,000 AU under the electricity scenario.

While the breakeven prices (severe-alkaline, 1,000 AUs) for electricity and liquefied biomethane are 287% and 339% of the market value, respectively, the breakeven price for biomethane is 626% of the market value. Even with liquefied biomethane requiring an

additional liquefication process, the breakeven price was closer to the market value than the biomethane scenario. However, it should be noted that liquefied biomethane systems are most likely to require a storage unit where temperature and pressure should be con- trolled. In the current study, costs involved in installation and maintenance of a storage unit was not considered in the liquefied biomethane scenario. Adding a storage unit would only add extra capital, and thus would not impact the conclusion that electricity is the most economical option.
84: The value of the three end-products considered were gradually increased to determine the price at which a specific scenario (i.e., pretreatment severity, pretreatment method, farm size, end-product) becomes economically feasible in terms of net present value assuming a lifespan of 20 years and interest rate of 5%. The calculation of this breakeven cost was performed by considering the total capital investment as well as annual revenue. The total annual operating cost was estimated by accounting for maintenance, utilities, labor, taxes, insurance, depreciation, and general expenses (Seider et al., 2009).

The breakeven cost corresponding to each scenario is shown in Table 6.3 and com- pared to the market value of each end-product (Krich et al., 2005; Sampat et al., 2018b). The breakeven cost for the Control (i.e., no pretreatment) showed a decreasing trend as farm size increased, yet always higher than the market value of the end-product. Acid (moderate), alkaline (moderate and severe), and AHP (moderate and severe) pretreat- ments showed promising results with reduced breakeven cost compared to the Control, whereas acid (severe) and SPORL (moderate and severe) pretreatments resulted in greater breakeven cost than the Control. While pretreatments can yield a significant increase in biogas production and reduce the breakeven cost of a product (compared to Control), the anaerobic digestion systems (with or without pretreatment) were in general not prof- itable. In any given scenario, the breakeven cost of a product was greater than the market value. However, the breakeven costs approached the market values as the dairy farm sizes increased.

At dairy farms with 1,000 AUs, severe-alkaline pretreatment was the most economical option, regardless of the biogas utilization scenario (i.e., end-product). At dairy farms with 2,500 AU, severe-alkaline pretreatment remained to be the most economical option along with moderate-acid pretreatment resulting in the same breakeven cost for Electricity and Biomethane scenarios. However, as the farm size increased to larger than 5,000 AUs, moderate-acid pretreatment turned out to be the most economical option in any biogas
Table 6.3: Breakeven price of product for each biogas utilization scenario under different pretreatment severity and dairy farm size. Units for the end-products follow the market value.

The breakeven costs of acid (severe) and SPORL (moderate and severe) pretreatments were higher than the Control regardless of the farm size. While the high cost from severe- acid pretreatment was caused by an extremely low CH4 yield due to furan inhibition, the high breakeven cost for SPORL (moderate and severe) pretreatments can be attributed to the costs associated with irradiation. To provide cost analysis for individual process components, a detailed breakdown of capital investment and operational costs involved in SPORL pretreatment (i.e., moderate-SPORL/1,000AU/electricity scenario) is shown in Figure 6.4. As illustrated, expenses related to microwave operation were the major con- tributors of the annual expense. Based on their extremely high breakeven costs of the end-products, acid (severe) and SPORL (moderate and severe) pretreatments are disre- garded from subsequent analysis and discussion.
Figure 6.4: Breakdown of capital investment and operational costs involved in moderate- SPORL pretreatment with 1,000 AU under the electricity scenario.

While the breakeven prices (severe-alkaline, 1,000 AUs) for electricity and liquefied biomethane are 287% and 339% of the market value, respectively, the breakeven price for biomethane is 626% of the market value. Even with liquefied biomethane requiring an

additional liquefication process, the breakeven price was closer to the market value than the biomethane scenario. However, it should be noted that liquefied biomethane systems are most likely to require a storage unit where temperature and pressure should be con- trolled. In the current study, costs involved in installation and maintenance of a storage unit was not considered in the liquefied biomethane scenario. Adding a storage unit would only add extra capital, and thus would not impact the conclusion that electricity is the most economical option.
85: The Upper Yahara Watershed has more than 200 animal farms with 148 of them be- ing dairy farms. Those dairy farms have around 44,000 AUs in total and can generate 1,100,000 metric tons of manure annually (Sharara et al., 2018), which can fully support the annual electricity use of 2,600 households in the U.S. just with conventional anaerobic digestion (i.e., no pretreatment). Among those dairy farms, 5 of them have more than 1,000 AUs and can be considered concentrated animal operating units (CAFOs) where 28% of the region's dairy manure is generated. This distribution of dairy manure is rep- resentative of a typical distribution in the State of Wisconsin, where approximately one- third of dairy manure is generated from CAFOs. Previous research has been conducted in this study region regarding manure storage planning (Larson et al., 2016), nutrient pollution (Hu et al., 2019), and manure pelletizing analysis (Sharara et al., 2018).
86: Breakeven costs included in the previous section are mainly for onsite production activi- ties. However, the logistics of manure management is an important issue while analyzing biomass-based processing schemes, since this feedstock supply is usually distributed in

nature. The economic feasibility of biomass processing is dependent on its location, and the relative abundance of raw materials (i.e., manure) in the surroundings. Therefore, to reveal the true breakeven prices of derived products and the profitability of the technol- ogy, a region-specific supply chain analysis needs to be conducted.

In this work, we applied the graph-based supply chain optimization framework, ADAM (introduced in Chapter 5), and conducted a case study in the Upper Yahara Wa- tershed. ADAM framework automates the model building, problem-solving, and visual- ization process of a generic organic waste supply chain optimization method as proposed in Sampat et al. (2017).

To define a supply chain model in ADAM, we need to specifically define materi- als and technologies in the ADAM database. Materials involved in the model includ- ing dairy manure, electricity, biomethane, liquefied biomethane, and digestate. We treat each processing scenario as a blackbox technology in ADAM. The corresponding product transformation relationships are calculated based on experimental data shown in Table 6.1. The investment and operational costs are obtained from the techno-economic analy- sis. To capture the impact of scales, a linear interpolation is included for farm sizes that are not directly analyzed. Figure 6.5 shows an example of the p-graph of the control set, where the same technology is duplicated to represent the different costs under different sizes.
Figure 6.5: P-graph in supply chain modeling (control)

Then, we need to construct the g-graph of this case study, i.e., we need to specify the supply, demand, and technology candidates in the study region. The suppliers are considered to be all the dairy farms (148 farms in total) in the system, whose manure generation amount is estimated based on the reported farm sizes. We assumed the farms with more than 200 AUs to be candidates appropriate for pretreatment technology im- plementation. Based on the recommendations from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, farms with more than 500 herds of cows are considered suitable for anaerobic digester (U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, 2021C). With logistic issues included in our analysis, for a specific farm, it can not only process the manure generated onsite but also from nearby farms. Therefore, we lowered the threshold to 200 AUs to introduce flexibility in the supply chain design. We further noticed that in this region, there are 50,600 acres of croplands (0.87 AU/acre) surrounding dairy farms, with a total count of 1,200 fields, which is almost 8 times of the dairy farms. Therefore, in the supply chain, we assumed the hauling of raw manure and digestate for land application can be ignored and they are directly consumed by farms. The electricity and upgraded biomethane can be directly sold to the grid. For liquefied biomethane, since the current market is not ma- ture in Wisconsin, we simply placed three hypothetical collection centers in urban areas of Waunakee, DeForest, and Sun Prairie in the study region. We note that this assumption may lead to an underestimation of transportation costs of liquefied biomethane, since the actual consumer could be located in a different region. A snapshot of specified g-graph is shown in Figure 6.6. With the above setup and assumptions, ADAM was applied to solve the supply chain problem to find the optimal strategy for technology selection, placement and sizing. The transportation routes and manure processing plans are also determined to maximize the system profit. A particular supply chain problem is initially defined for a certain pretreatment method and biogas utilization scenario, and then solved repeatedly to find the relationship between product price and the percentage of processed manure (i.e., participation of farms).

90: Techno-economic analysis discussed above showed that acid pretreatment (moderate) and alkaline pretreatment (both moderate and severe) can help reduce the breakeven prices of the biogas-derived products. Specifically, acid pretreatment has a better scalability and is a better option for centralized facilities. As can be found in Table 6.3, for farms > 5,000 AUs, acid pretreatment has the lowest breakeven prices for all end-products (i.e., for 10,000 AUs, the electricity price is 0.10 USD/kWh). Table 6.3 also reveals that alkaline pretreatments have similar breakeven prices for smaller farm sizes (< 2,500 AUs), but gradually becomes less competitive because of its high chemical cost. The chemical cost in pretreatment step is proportional to farm size and does not benefit from scaling up. Same trend can also be found between AHP pretreatment and Control (Table 4). AHP outperforms Control in smaller farms (< 2,500 AUs) due to increased CH4 yield, while Control is favored in larger farm sizes ( 5,000 AUs) since it has no additional chemical cost.

The supply chain analysis in ADAM further tests the scalability considering trans- portation of manure and derived products in the Upper Yahara Watershed. The rela- tionship between the prices of biogas-derived products paid to farms and percentage of

processed manure (i.e., extent of participating farms) is shown in Figures 6.7, 6.8, and 6.9, corresponding to electricity, biomethane, and liquefied biomethane production, respec- tively. From the figures we can see that for all three end-products, the minimum prices to drive manure processing are higher than the corresponding current market prices (high- lighted by vertical red lines), when the end-products are derived from other pathways (e.g., electricity from fossil fules). This indicates that without incentives from govern- ment, the manure-based processes are not strongly competitive in the current market. Electricity is still considered as the most promising product due to the smaller price gap (with respect to market price) as shown in Figure 6.7.
Figure 6.7: Relationship between electricity price and percentage of processed manure in Upper Yahara Watershed.

While there are substantial gaps between profitable product prices and their current reference points (i.e., market value), pretreatment can provide a positive impact by re- ducing the extent of incentives/subsidies needed. As shown in Figures 6.7-6.9, all pre- treatment curves are above the Control (i.e., no pretreatment) and have steeper slopes, meaning that the supply chain system with pretreatment can achieve breakeven point with fewer incentives and by processing lower amounts of manure compared to the Con-
Figure 6.8: Relationship between biomethane price and percentage of processed manure in Upper Yahara Watershed.
Figure 6.9: Relationship between liquefied biomethane price and percentage of processed manure in Upper Yahara Watershed.

trol. We also note that when the product prices are low, acid pretreatment will always be the first technology to be considered. This is because the low product prices cannot compensate for the transportation cost of manure, and only the several largest CAFOs are able to install technologies to process manure onsite. This observation is consistent with our techno-economic analysis indicating that acid pretreatment scales best for large facilities.

As the product price is improved (by incentives and subsidies), alkaline pretreatments gradually outperform acid pretreatment. This phenomenon can be mainly attributed to the expensive hauling costs and sparse distribution of manure production units in the study watershed. As an example, two supply chain designs for the acid pretreatment with electricity generation are shown in Figure 6.10, where red dots represent farms without pretreatment technology, blue dots represent farms with pretreatment technology, and brown lines are transportation flows of dairy manure. From this figure, it can be seen that when the electricity price paid to farms is set to 0.15 USD/kWh, there is almost no transportation of dairy manure, and the only farms that can install profitable processes are the large CAFOs. However, for a high electricity product price (0.70 USD/kWh), all farms participate in manure processing with acid pretreatment and AD, but the size of newly installed technologies are still considered medium, which can handle manure from 1,000- 2,500 AUs. This indicates that although large technologies can benefit (due to its size) by installing moderate-acid pretreatment system, the high transportation cost of manure and low manure density are the main obstacles of deploying large technologies. Therefore, the supply chain system turns out to mainly install medium-size technologies for any given pretreatment options, and the severe-alkaline pretreatment is the most economical option based on Table 6.3 and Figure 6.7-6.9.

Figure 6.10: Optimal supply chain designs for (a) moderate-acid pretreatment, electricity generation, low electricity price (0.15 USD/kWh), and (b) moderate-acid pretreatment, electricity generation, high electricity price (0.70 USD/kWh).

92: Techno-economic analysis discussed above showed that acid pretreatment (moderate) and alkaline pretreatment (both moderate and severe) can help reduce the breakeven prices of the biogas-derived products. Specifically, acid pretreatment has a better scalability and is a better option for centralized facilities. As can be found in Table 6.3, for farms > 5,000 AUs, acid pretreatment has the lowest breakeven prices for all end-products (i.e., for 10,000 AUs, the electricity price is 0.10 USD/kWh). Table 6.3 also reveals that alkaline pretreatments have similar breakeven prices for smaller farm sizes (< 2,500 AUs), but gradually becomes less competitive because of its high chemical cost. The chemical cost in pretreatment step is proportional to farm size and does not benefit from scaling up. Same trend can also be found between AHP pretreatment and Control (Table 4). AHP outperforms Control in smaller farms (< 2,500 AUs) due to increased CH4 yield, while Control is favored in larger farm sizes ( 5,000 AUs) since it has no additional chemical cost.

The supply chain analysis in ADAM further tests the scalability considering trans- portation of manure and derived products in the Upper Yahara Watershed. The rela- tionship between the prices of biogas-derived products paid to farms and percentage of

processed manure (i.e., extent of participating farms) is shown in Figures 6.7, 6.8, and 6.9, corresponding to electricity, biomethane, and liquefied biomethane production, respec- tively. From the figures we can see that for all three end-products, the minimum prices to drive manure processing are higher than the corresponding current market prices (high- lighted by vertical red lines), when the end-products are derived from other pathways (e.g., electricity from fossil fules). This indicates that without incentives from govern- ment, the manure-based processes are not strongly competitive in the current market. Electricity is still considered as the most promising product due to the smaller price gap (with respect to market price) as shown in Figure 6.7.
Figure 6.7: Relationship between electricity price and percentage of processed manure in Upper Yahara Watershed.

While there are substantial gaps between profitable product prices and their current reference points (i.e., market value), pretreatment can provide a positive impact by re- ducing the extent of incentives/subsidies needed. As shown in Figures 6.7-6.9, all pre- treatment curves are above the Control (i.e., no pretreatment) and have steeper slopes, meaning that the supply chain system with pretreatment can achieve breakeven point with fewer incentives and by processing lower amounts of manure compared to the Con-
Figure 6.8: Relationship between biomethane price and percentage of processed manure in Upper Yahara Watershed.
Figure 6.9: Relationship between liquefied biomethane price and percentage of processed manure in Upper Yahara Watershed.

trol. We also note that when the product prices are low, acid pretreatment will always be the first technology to be considered. This is because the low product prices cannot compensate for the transportation cost of manure, and only the several largest CAFOs are able to install technologies to process manure onsite. This observation is consistent with our techno-economic analysis indicating that acid pretreatment scales best for large facilities.

As the product price is improved (by incentives and subsidies), alkaline pretreatments gradually outperform acid pretreatment. This phenomenon can be mainly attributed to the expensive hauling costs and sparse distribution of manure production units in the study watershed. As an example, two supply chain designs for the acid pretreatment with electricity generation are shown in Figure 6.10, where red dots represent farms without pretreatment technology, blue dots represent farms with pretreatment technology, and brown lines are transportation flows of dairy manure. From this figure, it can be seen that when the electricity price paid to farms is set to 0.15 USD/kWh, there is almost no transportation of dairy manure, and the only farms that can install profitable processes are the large CAFOs. However, for a high electricity product price (0.70 USD/kWh), all farms participate in manure processing with acid pretreatment and AD, but the size of newly installed technologies are still considered medium, which can handle manure from 1,000- 2,500 AUs. This indicates that although large technologies can benefit (due to its size) by installing moderate-acid pretreatment system, the high transportation cost of manure and low manure density are the main obstacles of deploying large technologies. Therefore, the supply chain system turns out to mainly install medium-size technologies for any given pretreatment options, and the severe-alkaline pretreatment is the most economical option based on Table 6.3 and Figure 6.7-6.9.

Figure 6.10: Optimal supply chain designs for (a) moderate-acid pretreatment, electricity generation, low electricity price (0.15 USD/kWh), and (b) moderate-acid pretreatment, electricity generation, high electricity price (0.70 USD/kWh).

96: Our study results indicate that while pretreatments can yield a significant increase in bio- gas production and reduce the breakeven cost of a biogas-derived product, the anaerobic digestion systems (with or without pretreatment) are not typically profitable if there is no incentive provided. The breakeven price points for all end-products are higher than the market prices. However, the breakeven costs approached the market values as the dairy farm sizes increased. Techno-economic analysis revealed that moderate-acid pretreatment performs better for large facilities ( 5,000 AUs), while severe-alkaline pretreatment is more attractive for smaller dairy farms (< 2,500 AUs). Supply chain analysis showed that in the Upper Yahara Watershed study region, alkaline pretreatment (moderate and severe) could be the best pretreatment option as manure production is highly distributed and it is not economical to transport manure to a centralized facility. Our analysis shows that employing severe-alkaline pretreatment and utilizing biogas as electricity is the most economical option when considering anaerobic digestion in the study region. Our anal- ysis is currently limited to the study region of Upper Yahara Watershed, and may be further expanded to the entire State of Wisconsin where the supply chain is influenced
by multiple interactions. Capturing various local and regional scale interactions will help identify critical decisions made at state-level scales.

1: The main contributions of this work include the development of computational frame- works that combine supply chain modeling and optimization with additional computa- tional and analytical tools, such as life cycle analysis, simulations, and conflict resolution methodologies, to reveal trade-offs in organic waste management problems and provide decision-making information. A web-based platform ADAM is developed to facilitate supply chain modeling, solution, and visualization procedure and bridge experimental and computational research.
2: <header>Computational Frameworks for Economic and Environmental Analysis </header>

In Chapter 2, we presented a computational framework that combines life cycle analy- sis of carbon emissions, conceptual process design and cost estimations, and supply chain optimization models to analyze the economic and environmental performance of novel fatty acid and biogas recovery technologies. A real case study in Wisconsin is conducted by considering multiple types of organic waste streams, and our results indicate that with theoretical fatty acid yield factors, the combination of fatty acid and biogas recovery sys-

tems can simultaneously increase system profit and decrease greenhouse gas emissions. A series of sensitivity analyses are conducted to analyze the impact of external factors and determine the target yield factors that can make this novel technology economically feasible.

In Chapter 3, we presented a computational framework that merges a dynamic logis- tical network design model, a nutrient transport model, and an algae growth simulation to reveal the impact of waste management strategies on harmful algal blooms (HABs). The framework is demonstrated by a case study in Upper Yahara Watershed, Wisconsin, and we find that the logistics network management for waste and nutrients can reduce the incidence rates of HABs effectively, but reducing to a non-harmful level may require long-term efforts such as installing advanced manure treatment technologies and stor- age systems. Among the waste management strategies analyzed, enforcing strict nutrient management plans can achieve the best HAB levels, and coordinated manure application planning in winter has a significant influence on the occurrence of HABs in the following summer.
3: <header>Computational Frameworks for Economic and Environmental Analysis </header>

In Chapter 2, we presented a computational framework that combines life cycle analy- sis of carbon emissions, conceptual process design and cost estimations, and supply chain optimization models to analyze the economic and environmental performance of novel fatty acid and biogas recovery technologies. A real case study in Wisconsin is conducted by considering multiple types of organic waste streams, and our results indicate that with theoretical fatty acid yield factors, the combination of fatty acid and biogas recovery sys-

tems can simultaneously increase system profit and decrease greenhouse gas emissions. A series of sensitivity analyses are conducted to analyze the impact of external factors and determine the target yield factors that can make this novel technology economically feasible.

In Chapter 3, we presented a computational framework that merges a dynamic logis- tical network design model, a nutrient transport model, and an algae growth simulation to reveal the impact of waste management strategies on harmful algal blooms (HABs). The framework is demonstrated by a case study in Upper Yahara Watershed, Wisconsin, and we find that the logistics network management for waste and nutrients can reduce the incidence rates of HABs effectively, but reducing to a non-harmful level may require long-term efforts such as installing advanced manure treatment technologies and stor- age systems. Among the waste management strategies analyzed, enforcing strict nutrient management plans can achieve the best HAB levels, and coordinated manure application planning in winter has a significant influence on the occurrence of HABs in the following summer.
4: The previous two chapters mainly consider one environmental metric, either carbon emissions or nutrient pollution. In Chapter 4, we presented a decision-making procedure that resolves conflicts in multiple economic and environmental metrics by integrating comprehensive life cycle analysis, multi-objective multi-product supply chain modeling, and a utopia-tracking methodology. The life cycle analysis is conducted for 11 technolo- gies and includes 6 environmental metrics. We apply the framework to the same study region, the Upper Yahara Watershed, and obtain the optimal compromise solution. The solution shows that although there exist conflicts between environmental metrics, with increased system cost, those metrics can be improved together. The decision obtained in- volves a mixed-use of different technologies and waste management strategies to achieve balance among those metrics.
5: <header>Software Platform ADAM for Supply Chain Modeling </header>

In Chapter 5, we introduced our web-based supply chain modeling and optimization software, ADAM. ADAM uses a graph-based supply chain modeling abstraction and fa- cilitates model building, solving, and visualization. The format of a website provides an interactive graphic user interface that significantly improves user-friendliness. The key features of ADAM are highlighted, including the embedded material and technol- ogy database system, the object-oriented model and data management system, and the intuitive model building procedure with well-designed tutorials and public case studies. The architecture of the software, its workflows, and two simple examples are provided in Chapter 5. With these features, ADAM is designed to bridge the field of supply chain optimization with experimental research, technology development, and education in the FEW nexus and supply chain management.

In Chapter 6, we provided a comprehensive case study in the field of organic waste management combining experimental research and supply chain modeling in ADAM. The case study uses experimental biogas yield data from different pretreatment methods of anaerobic digestion for conceptual process designs. A techno-economic analysis is conducted to reveal the technology costs and breakeven prices of derived products. The cost information and transformation relationships are input into the ADAM material and technology database system. A series of supply chain models are constructed to account for the influence of material transportation and scale of economy. They are solved to reveal the relationship between product prices and farm participation in the study region.

7: The future work directions are mainly motivated by three aspects of the organic waste management problem. First, the complexity of established supply chain optimization models can grow exponentially with increased resolution, and proper solution approx-

imation methodology should be developed and implemented. Second, the long-term impacts of recommended optimal decisions are not well understood, and more compre- hensive computational and analytical tools need to be incorporated. And third, to further improve the functionality of ADAM, more advanced features should be designed, imple- mented, and tested.
8: The work in this dissertation includes various supply chain case studies with local boundaries, and they are solved by state-of-art commercial solvers, such as Gurobi. We still observe that some of those models cannot be solved to optimality within reason- able CPU time. This is because the model size explodes exponentially as the resolution (number of geographical nodes) increases. Furthermore, the mixed-integer formulation of the multi-product supply chain model involves the decision of binary variables, which introduces additional difficulty in problem-solving.

While small-size, local supply chains can be tackled properly, the solution is not glob- ally optimal if we expand the study region or increase the model resolution. Therefore, it is necessary to develop methodologies to provide good solutions for multi-product sup- ply chains. Iterative network aggregation and decomposition methods have been devel- oped for transportation problems and network flow problems (Haghani and Daskin, 1983; Barton et al., 1979; Zipkin, 1980; Rogers et al., 1991; Bärmann et al., 2015; Jörnsten and Leisten, 1995), and the idea of network aggregation can be applied to derive reasonable upper and lower bounds in multi-product supply chain design problems. In addition, an- other common strategy used in commercial supply chains design is hierarchical network design to obtain reasonably good solutions for commercial activities (Miller, 2012; Vla- chos, 2014). Therefore, the insight can also be applied in organic waste management. The hierarchical interactions in local, regional, national, and even global organic waste sup- ply chains need to be captured to identify proper solutions and quantify its quality. For example, nutrient balance at different scales is worth exploring to reveal proper national

and regional technology placement and product flows. We also note that since there is a trade-off between the model resolution and complexity, methods in selecting optimal resolution for decision-making should be developed and validated.

The hierarchical interactions in local, regional, national, and even global organic waste sup- ply chains need to be captured to identify proper solutions and quantify its quality. For example, nutrient balance at different scales is worth exploring to reveal proper national

and regional technology placement and product flows. We also note that since there is a trade-off between the model resolution and complexity, methods in selecting optimal resolution for decision-making should be developed and validated.
11: The work presented in this dissertation tries to establish connections between decision- making and the corresponding concrete environmental consequences. In Chapter 2, we use the definition of the social cost of carbon to monetize the impact of carbon emis- sions, and in Chapter 3, we further integrate simulation models to reveal the impact of nutrient pollution on human health. However, the long-term impact of the recommended decisions is still uncertain.

To understand the environmental impacts, more comprehensive computational method- ologies should be incorporated to provide feedback to the decision-making process. For example, to model the nutrient flows and its timescales more accurately, advanced simu- lation tools, such as SWAT and EPIC, could be potentially connected with supply chain optimizations to reveal long-term impacts of technology installations and manure storage planning. On the other hand, to keep the supply chain at a similar resolution to those sim- ulation tools, more aspects and datasets should be considered during the supply chain design phase, such as altitude, transportation time, and more accurate transportation routes. And for carbon emissions, although most current sustainable supply chain case studies only report overall greenhouse gas emissions, the impact on local and global cli- mate change should also be discussed with proper qualitative methods (Friedlingstein et al., 2003).

We note that all these efforts aim to provide decision-makers a clearer picture of the problem, and eventually to educate the public about the actions that they can take. Pro- viding concrete environmental examples is one of the effective approaches from the com- putational perspective, but it still requires collaborative efforts from other areas, such as education and communication, to achieve these goals.

14: A demo version of ADAM was released in October 2020. Similar to other software products, we will keep updating ADAM according to our internal testing, reported user requirements, and directions of experimental and computational research.

Currently, we are in the process of redesigning the data and model management sys- tem, SO that users can collaborate, share datasets, and work on the same model. Addi- tionally, we aim to provide more intuitive visualization tools to view product transfor- mations and supply chain performance. We also plan to introduce modeling flexibility into ADAM, such as explicit modeling of environmental metrics, budget constraints, and selection of objective functions. In the meantime, we will further develop case studies in ADAM to expand its database system and application areas (e.g., plastic waste). We hope ADAM can be used in future research projects to simplify supply chain modeling and analysis and enhance collaborations between computational and experimental groups, and also be used in education about process and systems design and analysis.

We acknowledge financial support from the U.S. Department of Agriculture (grant 2017- 67003-26055), from the National Science Foundation (grant CBET-1604374), and from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (contract number EP-18-C-000016).
